To address this challenge, we propose Consensus Embedding-based Federated Inference (CE-FI), a framework that enables pretrained models to cooperate at inference time without sharing model parameters or raw inputs and without assuming a common encoder. CE-FI introduces two components: a Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and a Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only, so the cooperative stage does not require additional labeled data. Experiments on image classification benchmarks -- CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100 -- under diverse non-IID conditions show that CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods that require stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks indicate applicability beyond image classification, although performance depends on the ensemble strategy. Further analysis identifies representation alignment as the primary bottleneck.

AI.tldr: CE-FI enables pre-trained models to cooperate during inference without sharing parameters, raw data, or requiring common encoders, using only unlabeled data to learn consensus embeddings.
AI.motivation: There's growing need for cooperation between independently deployed ML models while preserving privacy, but existing methods require sharing input data, model parameters, or common encoders, which limits applicability in privacy-sensitive or cross-organizational settings.
AI.method: CE-FI introduces two components: 1) Consensus Embedding (CE) layer that maps heterogeneous intermediate representations into a common embedding space, and 2) Cooperative Output (CO) layer that produces predictions from these embeddings. Both layers are trained using shared unlabeled data only.
AI.result: Experiments on CIFAR-10/100 under diverse non-IID conditions show CE-FI consistently outperforms solo inference and performs comparably to conventional methods requiring stronger sharing assumptions. Additional evaluations on text and time-series tasks show broader applicability, with performance depending on ensemble strategy.
AI.conclusion: CE-FI enables privacy-preserving federated inference without parameter/data sharing or common encoders, using only unlabeled data. Representation alignment is identified as the primary bottleneck, and the framework shows promising performance across multiple domains.

title: XDecomposer: Learning Prior-Free Set Decomposition for Multiphase X-ray Diffraction
summary: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases (components) cannot be reliably disentangled. While recent advances in representation-based crystal retrieval and generation suggest the possibility of inferring structures directly from PXRD, existing approaches largely assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings. Here, we present XDecomposer, a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists, structural templates, or prior knowledge of phase number. We formulate multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. A phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism, together with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction, enables accurate source separation while preserving crystallographic fidelity. Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems, while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures. These results provide a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduce long-standing dependence on prior-guided iteratively phase matching. The code is openly available at https://github.com/Licht0812/XDecomposer

AI.tldr: XDecomposer is a prior-free framework for joint decomposition and identification of multiphase XRD patterns without requiring candidate phase lists or prior knowledge of phase number.
AI.motivation: Multiphase powder X-ray diffraction (PXRD) analysis remains a fundamental bottleneck in structure identification, as real-world synthesis often produces complex mixtures whose constituent phases cannot be reliably disentangled. Existing approaches assume single-phase inputs and break down in multiphase settings.
AI.method: Formulates multiphase diffraction analysis as a set prediction problem, where the model infers an unordered set of phase-resolved components, their mixture proportions, and corresponding structural representations within a unified architecture. Uses phase-query-driven decomposition mechanism with diffraction-consistent physical reconstruction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on both simulated and experimental datasets show that XDecomposer substantially improves reconstruction accuracy and phase identification across diverse chemical systems while maintaining strong generalization to unseen mixtures.
AI.conclusion: Provides a practical route toward data-driven, source-resolved multiphase XRD analysis and reduces long-standing dependence on prior-guided iterative phase matching. The framework represents significant progress in automated multiphase XRD analysis.

title: WARP: A Benchmark for Primal-Dual Warm-Starting of Interior-Point Solvers
summary: Solving AC Optimal Power Flow (AC-OPF) is of central importance in electricity market operations, where interior-point methods (IPMs) such as IPOPT are the standard solvers. A growing body of work uses machine learning to predict primal warm-start iterates, reporting iteration reductions of 30-46\%. We show that these reported gains rest on an inappropriate evaluation baseline: prior methods benchmark against the flat start $V_m = 1, V_a = 0$, whereas the solver's actual default - the variable-bound midpoint $(l+u)/2$ - is near-optimal for log-barrier centrality. Against this corrected baseline, no primal-only warm-start method reduces solver iterations. We trace the failure to a geometric property of interior-point methods: primal prediction accuracy is anticorrelated with convergence speed, and providing the ground-truth optimal solution $x^*$ without dual variables causes the solver to diverge. Oracle experiments establish that the complete primal-dual-barrier state $(x^*, λ^*, z^*, μ^*)$ reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 - an 85\% reduction that is structurally inaccessible to primal-only methods. To enable rigorous evaluation of warm-start methods on this task, we release a benchmark suite comprising dual-labeled AC-OPF datasets with IPOPT-extracted solutions, a corrected evaluation protocol, and WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts the full interior-point state $(\hat{x}, \hatλ, \hat{z}, \hatμ)$ on the heterogeneous constraint graph. WARP achieves a 76\% reduction in IPOPT iterations while natively accommodating N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.

AI.tldr: Primal-only warm-start methods for AC-OPF don't actually reduce solver iterations when properly benchmarked against the solver's default initialization. The paper introduces a full primal-dual-barrier warm-start method that achieves 76% iteration reduction and a benchmark suite for rigorous evaluation.
AI.motivation: Existing machine learning methods for AC-OPF warm-starting claim iteration reductions of 30-46% but use inappropriate benchmarks (flat start instead of solver's default variable-bound midpoint). There's a need for proper evaluation and methods that actually reduce iterations.
AI.method: The paper identifies flaws in prior primal-only approaches, establishes that complete primal-dual-barrier state is necessary, and proposes WARP - a topology-conditioned encode-process-decode interaction network that predicts full interior-point state on heterogeneous constraint graphs.
AI.result: Oracle experiments show optimal primal-dual-barrier state reduces IPOPT iterations from 23 to 3 (85% reduction). WARP achieves 76% iteration reduction in IPOPT and natively handles N-1 contingency topology variations without retraining.
AI.conclusion: Primal-only warm-start methods don't reduce solver iterations when properly benchmarked; successful approaches must predict the complete interior-point state. The paper provides corrected evaluation protocols and WARP as an effective solution.

title: Agentic, Context-Aware Risk Intelligence in the Internet of Value
summary: The Internet of Value (IoV) is a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network in which the dominant marginal risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and the policy a system is willing to commit to) rather than a property of any single chain. We argue that a risk primitive adequate for this regime is a composition of five engines: a prediction engine over price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; a Bittensor verification subnet that decentralises and economically scores prediction outputs; a sentiment-fusion engine over text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; an agentic engine under constitutional, role-bound action constraints; and an API-risk and scenario engine that converts forecasts into pre-committed action programs in the sense of Monte-Carlo scenario generation. We anchor the architecture in two empirical artefacts: a 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and a 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc reported with explicit class-imbalance honesty. The case study supports deployability; the validator-loss decomposition is stated formally and is falsifiable.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a comprehensive risk management architecture for the Internet of Value (IoV) that combines five specialized engines for prediction, verification, sentiment analysis, agentic action, and scenario generation, with empirical validation on Solana.
AI.motivation: The Internet of Value (IoV) operates in a heterogeneous, partially-trusted network where the dominant risk is composite (route, sentiment, liquidity, and policy commitment) rather than being a property of any single chain. Current risk assessment approaches are inadequate for this complex environment.
AI.method: The authors propose a risk primitive composed of five engines: 1) Prediction engine for price, liquidity, volatility, and route health; 2) Bittensor verification subnet for decentralized scoring of predictions; 3) Sentiment-fusion engine analyzing text, on-chain flow, and grey-literature feeds; 4) Agentic engine with constitutional, role-bound action constraints; 5) API-risk and scenario engine converting forecasts into pre-committed action programs using Monte-Carlo scenario generation.
AI.result: The architecture is empirically validated through: 1) A 27-hour policy-constrained liquidity stress-response experiment on Solana, and 2) A 168-hour prediction-router calibration arc with explicit class-imbalance honesty reporting. The case study demonstrates deployability, and the validator-loss decomposition is formally stated and falsifiable.
AI.conclusion: The proposed five-engine architecture provides an adequate risk primitive for the IoV's complex composite risk environment, with empirical evidence supporting its deployability and formal validation framework. The approach addresses the heterogeneous nature of IoV networks where traditional single-chain risk assessment fails.

title: CRAFT: Forgetting-Aware Intervention-Based Adaptation for Continual Learning
summary: Large language models (LLMs) can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting. We propose CRAFT, a continual learning framework that avoids updating model weights by instead learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations. CRAFT proceeds in three stages: it first routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; it then fine-tunes the model using a Kullback-Leibler (KL) divergence against the group's prior state, which directly controls forgetting and determines convergence; finally, it merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. This design unifies routing, regularization, and merging through a single KL-based objective. CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. These results suggest that controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs.

AI.tldr: CRAFT is a continual learning framework that avoids weight updates by learning low-rank interventions on hidden representations, using KL divergence for routing, regularization, and merging tasks to prevent catastrophic forgetting in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Large language models can acquire new capabilities through fine-tuning, but continual adaptation often leads to catastrophic forgetting, where learning new tasks causes the model to forget previously learned ones. There's a need for effective continual learning methods that maintain performance on all tasks.
AI.method: CRAFT operates in three stages: 1) Routes each task to a group of similar tasks based on output-distribution divergence; 2) Fine-tunes the model using KL divergence against the group's prior state to control forgetting and determine convergence; 3) Merges interventions for the updated task into the shared representation using the same KL signal. The framework avoids updating model weights and instead learns low-rank interventions on hidden representations.
AI.result: CRAFT improves overall performance and reduces forgetting compared to strong LoRA-based approaches across multiple benchmarks and model scales, while remaining robust to task ordering. It demonstrates better capability preservation than existing methods.
AI.conclusion: Controlling adaptation in representation space, guided by output-space divergence, provides a scalable and principled approach to continual learning in LLMs. The KL-based objective effectively unifies routing, regularization, and merging for effective continual adaptation without catastrophic forgetting.

title: Null Space Constrained Contrastive Visual Forgetting for MLLM Unlearning
summary: The core challenge of machine unlearning is to strike a balance between target knowledge removal and non-target knowledge retention. In the context of Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs), this challenge becomes even more pronounced, as knowledge is further divided into visual and textual modalities that are tightly intertwined. In this paper, we introduce an MLLM unlearning approach that aims to forget target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge. Specifically, we freeze the LLM backbone and achieve unlearning by fine-tuning the visual module. First, we propose a Contrastive Visual Forgetting (CVF) mechanism to separate target visual knowledge from retained visual knowledge, guiding the representations of target visual concepts toward appropriate regions in the feature space. Second, we identify the null space associated with retained knowledge and constrain the unlearning process within this space, thereby significantly mitigating degradation in knowledge retention. Third, beyond static unlearning scenarios, we extend our approach to continual unlearning, where forgetting requests arrive sequentially. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate that our approach achieves a strong balance between effective forgetting and robust knowledge retention.

AI.tldr: A novel machine unlearning approach for Multimodal Large Language Models that selectively forgets target visual knowledge while preserving non-target visual knowledge and all textual knowledge through contrastive visual forgetting and null space constraint techniques.
AI.motivation: Machine unlearning in MLLMs faces the unique challenge of balancing target knowledge removal with retention of non-target knowledge across tightly intertwined visual and textual modalities, where visual knowledge needs selective forgetting while preserving all textual knowledge.
AI.method: The approach freezes the LLM backbone and fine-tunes only the visual module with three key components: 1) Contrastive Visual Forgetting mechanism to separate target from retained visual knowledge in feature space, 2) Null space constraint to confine unlearning within knowledge-preserving regions, and 3) Extension to continual unlearning for sequential forgetting requests.
AI.result: Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks demonstrate the approach achieves strong balance between effective forgetting of target visual knowledge and robust retention of non-target visual and all textual knowledge.
AI.conclusion: The proposed method successfully addresses the challenging MLLM unlearning problem by introducing modality-specific techniques that enable precise visual knowledge forgetting while maintaining overall model performance, with scalability to continual unlearning scenarios.

title: CoMemNet: Contrastive Sampling with Memory Replay Network for Continual Traffic Prediction
summary: In recent years, the integration of non-topological space modeling with temporal learning methods has emerged as an effective approach for capturing spatio-temporal information in non-Euclidean graphs. However, most existing methods rely on static underlying graph structures, which are inadequate for capturing the continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks. To address this challenge, we propose a simple yet efficient dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction, named CoMemNet. The fast-converging Online branch undertakes the primary prediction tasks, while the momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler). This sampler selects a node set with significant dynamic network feature changes for training, effectively mitigating the issue of catastrophic forgetting. Additionally, the backbone incorporates a lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) to consolidate old knowledge through memory replay and address the risk of memory explosion. Finally, we provide two newly curated open-source datasets. Experimental results demonstrate that CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art (SOTA) performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The code is available at: https://github.com/meiwu5/CoMemNet.

AI.tldr: CoMemNet: A dual-branch continual learning framework for traffic prediction that addresses catastrophic forgetting in streaming networks using dynamic contrastive sampling and adaptive memory buffers, achieving SOTA results with new open-source datasets.
AI.motivation: Current spatio-temporal learning methods for non-Euclidean graphs rely on static underlying structures, which are inadequate for capturing continuously expanding and evolving patterns in streaming traffic networks where networks evolve over time.
AI.method: Proposes CoMemNet with a dual-branch continual learning framework: 1) Fast-converging Online branch for primary prediction, 2) Momentum-updated Target branch extracts historical information using Wasserstein Distance features to create a Dynamic Contrastive Sampler (DC Sampler) that selects nodes with significant dynamic feature changes, 3) Lightweight Node-Adaptive Temporal Memory Buffer (TMRB-N) consolidates old knowledge through memory replay and prevents memory explosion.
AI.result: CoMemNet achieves state-of-the-art performance across all three large-scale real-world datasets. The authors also provide two newly curated open-source datasets to support future research.
AI.conclusion: The proposed CoMemNet framework effectively addresses catastrophic forgetting in streaming traffic networks through its dual-branch continual learning approach with dynamic contrastive sampling and adaptive memory management, demonstrating superior performance over existing methods.

title: PREFER: Personalized Review Summarization with Online Preference Learning
summary: Product reviews significantly influence purchasing decisions on e-commerce platforms. However, the sheer volume of reviews can overwhelm users, obscuring the information most relevant to their specific needs. Current e-commerce summarization systems typically produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for the fact that (i) different users care about different product characteristics, and (ii) these preferences may evolve with interactions. To address the challenge of unknown latent preferences, we propose an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user. Our system iteratively refines its understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time. We provide a case study using the Amazon Reviews'23 dataset, showing in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.

AI.tldr: Online learning framework that generates personalized review summaries by continuously refining understanding of user preferences through feedback, addressing limitations of static generic summarization systems.
AI.motivation: Product reviews overwhelm users with volume, and current summarization systems produce generic, static summaries that fail to account for (i) different users caring about different product characteristics, and (ii) evolving user preferences with interactions.
AI.method: Proposed an online learning framework that generates personalized summaries for each user and iteratively refines understanding of user preferences by incorporating feedback directly from the generated summaries over time.
AI.result: Case study using Amazon Reviews'23 dataset showed in controlled simulations that online preference learning improves alignment with target user interests while maintaining summary quality.
AI.conclusion: Online preference learning through personalized summarization effectively addresses the challenge of unknown latent user preferences, providing better alignment with individual user interests while preserving summary quality compared to static generic summarization approaches.

title: Multi-Dimensional Behavioral Evaluation of Agentic Stock Prediction Systems Using LLM Judges with Closed-Loop Reinforcement Learning Feedback
comment: 9 pages, 2 figures, 8 tables. Short Communication submitted to Knowledge-Based Systems (Elsevier)
summary: Agentic stock prediction systems make sequences of interdependent decisions (regime detection, pathway routing, reinforcement learning control) whose individual quality is hidden by aggregate metrics such as mean absolute percentage error (MAPE) or directional accuracy. We present a behavioral evaluation framework that addresses this gap. Behavioral traces logged at every autonomous decision point are grouped into five-day episodes and scored along six domain-specific dimensions (regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery) by an ensemble of three large language model (LLM) judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro). Perturbation-based validation on 420 episodes yields targeted score drops of $-1.6$ to $-2.4$ on intended dimensions versus an average of $-0.32$ on the remaining five, with cross-model agreement up to Krippendorff's $α= 0.85$. The composite behavioral score, used here only for cross-episode reporting, correlates at $ρ= 0.72$ with realized 20-day Sharpe ratio from offline backtesting. Closing the loop, the framework converts deficient per-dimension scores into a credit-assigned penalty term added to the Soft Actor-Critic (SAC) reward. Three short fine-tuning cycles, all confined to the validation period, produce on the held-out 2017-2025 test period a one-day MAPE reduction from 0.61% to 0.54% (an 11.5% relative reduction; $p<0.001$, Cohen's $d=0.31$), a directional accuracy increase from 71% to 74%, and an 18% Sharpe ratio improvement (95% bootstrap CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where the original system was most behaviorally deficient. Results are from offline backtesting and do not address effects specific to live deployment.

AI.tldr: Proposes a behavioral evaluation framework for agentic stock prediction systems using LLM judges to score decision-making along six dimensions, enabling targeted improvement via reward augmentation that significantly improves trading metrics.
AI.motivation: Traditional aggregate metrics like MAPE and directional accuracy fail to reveal the quality of individual interdependent decisions in agentic stock prediction systems, creating a gap in understanding and improving system behavior at the decision level.
AI.method: Logs behavioral traces at each autonomous decision point, groups into five-day episodes, scores with ensemble of three LLM judges (GPT 5.4, Claude 4.6 Opus, Gemini 3.1 Pro) on six dimensions: regime detection, routing, adaptation, risk calibration, strategy coherence, error recovery. Uses perturbation-based validation and converts deficient scores into credit-assigned penalty term for Soft Actor-Critic reward fine-tuning.
AI.result: Framework shows strong validation (targeted score drops of -1.6 to -2.4 on intended dimensions vs -0.32 average on others, cross-model agreement α=0.85, composite score correlates ρ=0.72 with 20-day Sharpe). Fine-tuning reduces MAPE from 0.61% to 0.54% (11.5% reduction, p<0.001), increases directional accuracy from 71% to 74%, improves Sharpe ratio by 18% (95% CI [8.2%, 27.4%]), with gains concentrated in high-volatility episodes where system was most deficient.
AI.conclusion: Behavioral evaluation framework with LLM judges effectively identifies and improves specific decision-making deficiencies in agentic trading systems, leading to significant performance gains while maintaining interpretability, though results are limited to offline backtesting and may differ in live deployment.

title: Wisteria: A Unified Multi-Scale Feature Learning Framework for DNA Language Model
summary: DNA language model aims to decipher the regulatory grammar and semantic of genomes by capturing long range dependencies in DNA sequences. Existing methods emphasize long range token interactions but often ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies. In this paper, we propose Wisteria, a genomic language model that integrates multi scale feature learning within a unified framework for DNA sequence. Specifically, Wisteria augments the Mamba based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs and regulatory patterns, while gated multilayer perceptrons refine global dependencies. We further introduce a Fourier based attention mechanism to support frequency domain modeling, periodic extension and length generalization. Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines. These results indicate that Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi scale genomic sequence analysis.

AI.tldr: Wisteria is a DNA language model that integrates multi-scale feature learning using gated dilated convolutions for local motifs and gated MLPs for global dependencies, with Fourier-based attention for frequency domain modeling.
AI.motivation: Existing DNA language models focus on long-range token interactions but ignore the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies, which is crucial for understanding genomic regulatory grammar and semantics.
AI.method: Wisteria augments Mamba-based architecture with gated dilated convolutions to capture local motifs/patterns, gated multilayer perceptrons for global dependencies, and introduces Fourier-based attention for frequency domain modeling, periodic extension, and length generalization.
AI.result: Across four experimental settings with both short and long range dependencies, Wisteria demonstrates strong performance on downstream benchmarks against competitive DNA language model baselines.
AI.conclusion: Wisteria effectively unifies local and global dependency modeling for multi-scale genomic sequence analysis, indicating it successfully captures the interplay between local motifs and global dependencies.

title: Weak-to-Strong Generalization is Nearly Inevitable (in Linear Models)
summary: Weak-to-strong generalization is a phenomenon in post-training whereby a strong student model, when finetuned solely with feedback from a weaker teacher, can not only surpass the teacher, but can improve upon its own capabilities. Recent work of Burns et al. (2023) demonstrated that this can occur in the setting of frontier language models, and subsequently there has been a flurry of both empirical work trying to exploit this phenomenon, as well as theoretical work attempting to understand it. In this work, we demonstrate that weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression, under mild distributional assumptions on the data. In fact, we show that this happens for most student-teacher pairs, suggesting that weak-to-strong generalization is in fact \emph{almost inevitable}, even in this basic setting. Notably, our setting does not require the student to be more expressive or have more model capacity in any way compared to the teacher, which runs contrary to the prevailing theoretical belief that a mismatch in model capacity is a central mechanism to weak-to-strong generalization.

AI.tldr: Weak-to-strong generalization occurs in basic linear logistic regression, suggesting it's almost inevitable even without capacity differences between student and teacher models.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed weak-to-strong generalization in frontier language models, but theoretical understanding is limited. The prevailing belief is that capacity mismatch between student and teacher is central to the phenomenon.
AI.method: The paper demonstrates weak-to-strong generalization occurs in standard linear logistic regression under mild distributional assumptions, without requiring the student to be more expressive or have greater capacity than the teacher.
AI.result: Weak-to-strong generalization happens for most student-teacher pairs in this basic setting, suggesting the phenomenon is almost inevitable and can occur without capacity differences.
AI.conclusion: The findings challenge the prevailing theoretical belief that model capacity mismatch is central to weak-to-strong generalization, showing it can occur in simple linear models under mild assumptions.

title: Intentmaking and Sensemaking: Human Interaction with AI-Guided Mathematical Discovery
summary: Artificial intelligence offers powerful new tools for scientific discovery, but the interaction paradigms required to effectively harness these systems remain underexplored. In this paper, we present findings from a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians who used AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to tackle advanced problems in their fields of expertise. We identify and characterize a distinct workflow we term intentmaking, the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining one's experimental goals through active system interaction. We frame this as a natural extension to sensemaking, the cognitive process of building an understanding of complex or novel data. We suggest that users enter a cycle of intentmaking (defining and updating their experiment) and sensemaking (interpreting the results) which repeats many times during the course of an investigation. Our documentation of these themes suggests an approach to designing AI tools for scientific discovery that goes beyond the existing question/answer model of many current systems, treating them as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants.

AI.tldr: Researchers studied how mathematicians use AI coding assistants, discovering a new workflow called "intentmaking" where users iteratively refine their experimental goals through system interaction, forming a cycle with sensemaking.
AI.motivation: Current AI tools for scientific discovery use limited question/answer interaction models that don't effectively support the complex, iterative nature of scientific exploration. There's a need to understand how experts actually use AI systems in practice to inform better design approaches.
AI.method: Conducted a formative user study with 11 expert mathematicians using AlphaEvolve, an evolutionary coding agent, to solve advanced problems in their fields. Used qualitative analysis to identify patterns in how they interacted with the AI system.
AI.result: Identified a distinct workflow called "intentmaking" - the iterative process of discovering, defining, and refining experimental goals through active system interaction. Found that users enter cycles of intentmaking (defining experiments) and sensemaking (interpreting results) that repeat throughout investigations.
AI.conclusion: AI tools for scientific discovery should be designed as collaborative instruments rather than opaque black-box assistants, moving beyond simple question/answer models to support the iterative intentmaking-sensemaking cycles that characterize real scientific exploration.

title: RVPO: Risk-Sensitive Alignment via Variance Regularization
summary: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via an arithmetic mean, leaving them vulnerable to constraint neglect: high-magnitude success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing "bottleneck" rewards vital for reliable multi-objective alignment. We propose Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation, shifting the objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency." We show via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. We evaluate RVPO on rubric-based medical and scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged reward signals (Qwen2.5-3B/7B/14B) and on tool-calling with rule-based constraints (Qwen2.5-1.5B/3B). By preventing the model from neglecting difficult constraints to exploit easier objectives, RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, $p < 0.001$) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods, demonstrating that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.

AI.tldr: RVPO is a risk-sensitive RLHF method that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation to prevent constraint neglect, improving multi-objective alignment by forcing consistent performance across all objectives rather than optimizing for high-magnitude successes in some areas that mask failures in others.
AI.motivation: Current critic-less RLHF methods aggregate multi-objective rewards via arithmetic mean, making them vulnerable to constraint neglect where high success in one objective can numerically offset critical failures in others (e.g., safety or formatting), masking low-performing bottleneck rewards crucial for reliable multi-objective alignment.
AI.method: Proposes Reward-Variance Policy Optimization (RVPO), a risk-sensitive framework that penalizes inter-reward variance during advantage aggregation. Shows via Taylor expansion that a LogSumExp (SoftMin) operator effectively acts as a smooth variance penalty. Evaluates on rubric-based medical/scientific reasoning with up to 17 concurrent LLM-judged rewards and tool-calling with rule-based constraints.
AI.result: RVPO improves overall scores on HealthBench (0.261 vs. 0.215 for GDPO at 14B, p < 0.001) and maintains competitive accuracy on GPQA-Diamond without the late-stage degradation observed in other multi-reward methods. Demonstrates that variance regularization mitigates constraint neglect across model scales without sacrificing general capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Variance regularization via RVPO effectively addresses constraint neglect in multi-objective RLHF, shifting the optimization objective from "maximize sum" to "maximize consistency," leading to more reliable alignment across diverse reward signals while maintaining overall performance.

title: Which Are the Low-Resource Languages of the Semantic Web?
comment: ESWC 2026 - 23rd European Semantic Web Conference, May 2026, Dubrovnik, Croatia
summary: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from the global digital transformation. Multilingual Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs (LOD KGs) could contribute to mitigating this divide through cross-lingual transfer; however, no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages has yet been established in the context of LOD KGs. In this poster, we present a methodology to analyze the distribution of languages across LOD KGs and propose a preliminary multi-level categorization based on DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. This categorization is leveraged to bring a formal definition of low-, high-, and medium-resource languages that could be later leveraged to select cross-lingual transfer candidates.

AI.tldr: A methodology to categorize languages in Linked Open Data Knowledge Graphs as low-, medium-, or high-resource for enabling cross-lingual transfer.
AI.motivation: Digital technologies are widening the Open Access Data divide between high- and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from global digital transformation. Multilingual LOD KGs could help via cross-lingual transfer, but there's no clear quantitative definition of low-resource languages in this context.
AI.method: Proposes a methodology to analyze language distribution across LOD KGs and establishes a preliminary multi-level categorization based on three major knowledge graphs: DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata.
AI.result: Develops a formal definition/categorization of low-, medium-, and high-resource languages within the context of LOD KGs, which can be used to select appropriate cross-lingual transfer candidates.
AI.conclusion: Establishes a needed quantitative framework for defining language resource levels in LOD KGs, providing a foundation for targeted cross-lingual transfer strategies to address the digital divide in Open Access Data.

title: Full-Spectrum Graph Neural Network: Expressive and Scalable
summary: It is well established that spectral graph neural networks (GNNs) can universally approximate node signals; however, their expressive power remains bounded by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Lehman test, which is mirrored in their lack of universality for higher-order signals. To go beyond this bound, we propose the Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN), a second-order generalization of classical spectral GNNs. FSpecGNN advances spectral filtering in two perspectives: (1) it lifts the signal from the node domain to the node-pair domain; and (2) it extends the univariate spectral filter over eigenvalues to a bivariate filter over eigenvalue pairs. We show that classical spectral GNNs arise as a diagonal special case of FSpecGNN, and prove that FSpecGNN can be at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals, the latter being particularly beneficial for heterophilic graph learning. Moreover, FSpecGNN admits scalable implementations that avoid explicit node-pair-level computations; combined with a low-rank approximation that reduces full-spectrum convolution to a combination of polynomial spectral filters, it enables learning on large graphs. Empirically, FSpecGNN validates the predicted expressivity and delivers strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks.

AI.tldr: FSpecGNN is a second-order spectral graph neural network that extends beyond standard GNNs by operating on node-pair signals and using bivariate spectral filters, enabling better performance on heterophilic graphs while maintaining scalability.
AI.motivation: Standard spectral GNNs are limited in expressive power by the 1-dimensional Weisfeiler-Lehman test and lack universality for higher-order signals. There's a need to go beyond these limitations, especially for tasks involving heterophilic graph learning.
AI.method: Proposes Full-Spectrum GNN (FSpecGNN) with two key innovations: (1) lifting signals from node domain to node-pair domain, and (2) extending univariate spectral filters over eigenvalues to bivariate filters over eigenvalue pairs. Uses scalable implementations with low-rank approximations to avoid explicit node-pair computations.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows FSpecGNN can be at most as expressive as Local 2-GNN while universally approximating node-pair signals. Empirical validation demonstrates strong performance on heterophilic benchmarks and confirms predicted expressivity.
AI.conclusion: FSpecGNN successfully extends spectral GNNs beyond 1-WL limitations, provides a second-order generalization with better expressivity for heterophilic graph learning, and maintains practical scalability through efficient implementations.

title: In Data or Invisible: Toward a Better Digital Representation of Low-Resource Languages with Knowledge Graphs
summary: Emerging digital technologies are exacerbating the existing divide in Open Access Data (OAD) between high-and low-resource languages, excluding many communities from participating in the global digital transformation. In this PhD proposal, we aim to address this gap, focusing on the language coverage of Linked Open Data knowledge graphs (LOD KGs). First, we identify key variables that characterize language distribution in LOD, including the number of Wikipedia articles per language edition and the number of language-tagged entities in LOD KGs. These variables are analyzed across three major multilingual LOD KGs, DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata, providing insights into the representation and distribution of languages within LOD. Building on this analysis, we intend to study the impact of cross-lingual transfer candidate selection on the task of multilingual KG completion. In particular, we plan to investigate strategies based on linguistic proximity and the availability of curated annotated alignments between languages. Language proximity also motivates us to explore the benefits of analogical reasoning that relies on (dis)similarities and has not yet been investigated to identify correspondences across languages to improve KG completion performance and enhance language coverage in LOD.

AI.tldr: This PhD proposal addresses the digital divide in Open Access Data by focusing on language coverage in Linked Open Data knowledge graphs, analyzing language distribution patterns, and investigating cross-lingual transfer and analogical reasoning methods to improve multilingual KG completion.
AI.motivation: The growing digital divide in Open Access Data between high-resource and low-resource languages is excluding many communities from global digital transformation. There's a need to address language coverage gaps in Linked Open Data knowledge graphs to ensure more equitable access and participation.
AI.method: 1) Identify key variables characterizing language distribution in LOD (Wikipedia articles per language, language-tagged entities). 2) Analyze these variables across three major multilingual LOD KGs: DBpedia, BabelNet, and Wikidata. 3) Study cross-lingual transfer candidate selection for multilingual KG completion using linguistic proximity and curated alignments. 4) Explore analogical reasoning based on (dis)similarities to identify cross-language correspondences.
AI.result: The abstract presents a research proposal rather than completed results, but outlines expected outcomes: insights into language representation/distribution patterns in LOD KGs, evaluation of cross-lingual transfer strategies, and development of analogical reasoning approaches for improving KG completion performance and enhancing language coverage.
AI.conclusion: The proposed research aims to develop methods to bridge the language divide in Linked Open Data, potentially enabling more equitable access to knowledge resources and reducing digital exclusion of low-resource language communities through improved multilingual KG completion techniques.

title: Adaptive Selection of LoRA Components in Privacy-Preserving Federated Learning
summary: Differentially private federated fine-tuning of large models with LoRA suffers from aggregation error caused by LoRA's multiplicative structure, which is further amplified by DP noise and degrades both stability and accuracy. Existing remedies apply a single update mode uniformly across all layers and all communication rounds (or alternate them on a fixed schedule), ignoring both the structural asymmetry between the two LoRA factors and the round-wise dynamics of training. We propose AS-LoRA, an adaptive framework defined by three axes (i) layer-wise freedom, in which each layer independently selects its active component, (ii) round-wise adaptivity, in which the selection updates over communication rounds, and (iii) a curvature-aware score derived from a second-order approximation of the loss. Theoretically, AS-LoRA eliminates the reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, implicitly biases solutions toward flatter minima, and incurs no additional privacy cost. Across GLUE, SQuAD, CIFAR-100, and Tiny-ImageNet under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions, AS-LoRA improves over the federated LoRA baselines by up to $+7.5$ pp on GLUE and $+12.5$ pp on MNLI-mm for example, while matching or exceeding SVD-based aggregation methods at $33\text{--}180 \times$ lower aggregation cost and with negligible communication overhead. Code for the proposed method is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/as_lora-F75F/.

AI.tldr: AS-LoRA: Adaptive federated fine-tuning framework that eliminates aggregation error in DP-LoRA by allowing layer-wise selection of active LoRA components with round-wise adaptation and curvature-aware scoring.
AI.motivation: Differentially private federated fine-tuning with LoRA suffers from aggregation error caused by LoRA's multiplicative structure, worsened by DP noise, leading to instability and accuracy degradation. Existing methods apply uniform update modes across all layers and rounds, ignoring structural asymmetry and training dynamics.
AI.method: AS-LoRA framework with three axes: (1) layer-wise freedom where each layer independently selects its active component (A or B), (2) round-wise adaptivity where selections update over communication rounds, and (3) curvature-aware scoring derived from second-order loss approximation.
AI.result: Improves over federated LoRA baselines by up to +7.5 pp on GLUE and +12.5 pp on MNLI-mm, matches or exceeds SVD-based aggregation methods at 33-180× lower aggregation cost with negligible communication overhead, under strict DP budgets and non-IID partitions.
AI.conclusion: AS-LoRA eliminates reconstruction-error floor of layer-tied schedules, accelerates convergence, biases solutions toward flatter minima, incurs no additional privacy cost, and provides substantial performance improvements with minimal overhead.

title: ICU-Bench:Benchmarking Continual Unlearning in Multimodal Large Language Models
summary: Although Multimodal Large Language Models (MLLMs) have achieved remarkable progress across many domains, their training on large-scale multimodal datasets raises serious privacy concerns, making effective machine unlearning increasingly necessary. However, existing benchmarks mainly focus on static or short-sequence settings, offering limited support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic deployments. To bridge this gap, we introduce ICU-Bench, a continual multimodal unlearning benchmark built on privacy-critical document data. ICU-Bench contains 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from two document domains, medical reports and labor contracts, with 9,500 images, 16,000 question-answer pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Additionally, new continual unlearning metrics are introduced, facilitating a comprehensive analysis of forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, retained utility, and stability throughout the continual unlearning process. Through extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods on ICU-Bench, we show that existing methods generally struggle in continual settings and exhibit clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences. These findings highlight the need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion.

AI.tldr: ICU-Bench: A new benchmark for continual multimodal unlearning with privacy-sensitive document data, revealing limitations of existing methods in realistic long-sequence privacy deletion scenarios.
AI.motivation: Multimodal LLMs trained on large-scale data raise serious privacy concerns, but existing benchmarks focus only on static or short-sequence settings, lacking support for evaluating continual privacy deletion requests in realistic deployments.
AI.method: Created ICU-Bench with 1,000 privacy-sensitive profiles from medical reports and labor contracts, containing 9,500 images, 16,000 QA pairs, and 100 forget tasks. Introduced new continual unlearning metrics covering forgetting effectiveness, historical forgetting preservation, utility retention, and stability.
AI.result: Extensive experiments with representative unlearning methods show that existing methods generally struggle in continual settings, exhibiting clear limitations in balancing forgetting quality, utility preservation, and scalability over long task sequences.
AI.conclusion: The findings highlight the need for multimodal unlearning methods explicitly designed for continual privacy deletion, as current approaches are insufficient for realistic long-sequence privacy protection scenarios.

title: A Measure-Theoretic Finite-Sample Theory for Adaptive-Data Fitted Q-Iteration
summary: While reinforcement learning (RL) promises to revolutionize the control of complex nonlinear robotic systems, a profound gap persists between the heuristic success of model-free off-policy deep RL and the underlying theory, which remains largely confined to tabular or linearizable settings. We identify the cause of this gap as an emergent isolation of three traditions: (i) measure-theoretic MDP foundations on general spaces limit their analysis to exact dynamic programming and ignore all error sources of a learning process; (ii) deterministic error propagation analysis addresses the approximation error via concentrability coefficients without a finite-sample analysis of the estimation error; and (iii) PAC generalization bounds characterize the estimation errors of simplified topologies. We bridge these traditions with a unified theoretical framework for fitted Q-iteration (FQI) on general measurable Borel spaces. Our main result provides a finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound by chaining measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces. We prove that sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data collection. We further extend this analysis to provide the first cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces. These results lay the necessary foundations for the formal analysis of many modern deep RL algorithms.

AI.tldr: The paper develops a unified theoretical framework for fitted Q-iteration on general measurable spaces, bridging measure-theoretic MDP foundations, deterministic error propagation analysis, and PAC generalization bounds to provide finite-sample performance guarantees for deep reinforcement learning algorithms.
AI.motivation: There's a significant gap between the practical success of model-free deep RL in controlling complex robotic systems and its theoretical foundations, which are largely confined to simplified settings like tabular or linearizable MDPs. This gap arises from the isolation of three theoretical traditions that don't adequately address real-world learning processes.
AI.method: The authors create a unified theoretical framework for fitted Q-iteration on general measurable Borel spaces. They combine measure-theoretic probability with Bellman-operator contraction in Banach spaces, using sequential Rademacher complexity to control Bellman-regression generalization under policy-dependent data collection.
AI.result: The main result is a finite-sample, adaptive-data performance bound for FQI. The paper proves that sequential Rademacher complexity controls Bellman-regression generalization, and extends this to provide the first cumulative, pathwise online regret guarantee for FQI in continuous spaces.
AI.conclusion: These results bridge previously isolated theoretical traditions and lay the necessary mathematical foundations for formal analysis of modern deep RL algorithms, addressing the gap between practical heuristic success and theoretical understanding.

title: MAS-Algorithm: A Workflow for Solving Algorithmic Programming Problems with a Multi-Agent System
summary: Algorithmic problem solving serves as a rigorous testbed for evaluating structured reasoning in AI coding systems, as it directly reflects a model's ability to perform structured reasoning in complex scenarios.Existing approaches predominantly rely on model-centric strategies, such as architectural modifications and data scaling, which are costly and offer limited interpretability. Alternative methods leveraging external tools or prompting techniques (e.g., chain-of-thought) are often fragmented and lack a unified framework. In this paper, we propose MAS-Algorithm, a systematic multi-agent workflow for algorithmic problem solving inspired by the practices of competitive programmers and algorithm engineers. Our framework decomposes the end-to-end solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible coordination among agents. The design emphasizes both rigor and extensibility, allowing it to generalize across diverse problem types.Experimental results on a self-constructed benchmark demonstrate consistent improvements across multiple Qwen series models, achieving an average gain of 6.48% in acceptance rate. In contrast, parameter-efficient fine-tuning on the same data yields only a marginal improvement of 0.89%. We further observe a 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro, along with consistent improvements across additional accuracy and efficiency metrics.Beyond performance gains, we conduct comprehensive analyses to better understand the reasoning process within the workflow, including error patterns and cross-scenario behaviors. We further perform customized replacement and ablation studies to explore the upper bound of the framework, showing that individual agents can contribute improvements of up to 27.7%. These results highlight the strong potential of MAS-Algorithm for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning.

AI.tldr: MAS-Algorithm is a multi-agent workflow framework for algorithmic problem solving that outperforms traditional model-centric approaches and achieves significant improvements in code acceptance rates through structured reasoning and agent coordination.
AI.motivation: Existing approaches for algorithmic problem solving in AI coding systems have limitations: model-centric strategies (architectural modifications, data scaling) are costly and lack interpretability, while external tool/prompting methods are fragmented without unified frameworks.
AI.method: Proposed MAS-Algorithm - a systematic multi-agent workflow inspired by competitive programmers and algorithm engineers. It decomposes solving process into modular stages, enabling structured reasoning, tool integration, and flexible agent coordination with emphasis on rigor and extensibility.
AI.result: Achieved average 6.48% gain in acceptance rate on self-constructed benchmark across Qwen series models vs. 0.89% from parameter-efficient fine-tuning. Also showed 4.72% gain on LiveCodeBench-Pro with consistent improvements across accuracy/efficiency metrics. Individual agents contributed up to 27.7% improvements in ablation studies.
AI.conclusion: MAS-Algorithm demonstrates strong potential for advancing AI-driven algorithmic reasoning through its systematic multi-agent framework, showing significant performance gains and offering better understanding of reasoning processes compared to existing approaches.

title: Revealing Modular Gradient Noise Imbalance in LLMs: Calibrating Adam via Signal-to-Noise Ratio
summary: The impressive performance of large language models (LLMs) arises from their massive scale and heterogeneous module composition. However, this structural heterogeneity introduces additional optimization challenges. While adaptive optimizers such as Adam(W) provide per-parameter adaptivity, they do not explicitly account for module-level gradient heterogeneity, resulting in slower convergence, suboptimal performance, or training instability. Existing approaches typically rely on manually tuned module-specific learning rates or specific optimization strategies, which are computationally costly and difficult to generalize across tasks or models. To establish a more principled approach, we first analyze the noise-damping behavior of Adam in high-noise modules and introduce \textbf{Module-wise Learning Rate Scaling via SNR (MoLS)}. MoLS estimates module-level SNRs to scale Adam updates, allowing automated module-wise learning rate allocation without manual tuning. Empirical results through multiple LLM training benchmarks demonstrate that MoLS improves convergence speed and generalization, achieving performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific learning rates, while remaining compatible with memory-efficient training algorithms.

AI.tldr: MoLS is a module-wise learning rate scaling method for LLMs that uses signal-to-noise ratio estimation to automatically allocate learning rates across different modules, improving convergence and generalization without manual tuning.
AI.motivation: LLMs have heterogeneous module composition which creates optimization challenges. Adaptive optimizers like Adam don't account for module-level gradient heterogeneity, leading to slower convergence, suboptimal performance, or training instability. Existing manual approaches are computationally costly and hard to generalize.
AI.method: The authors analyze Adam's noise-damping behavior in high-noise modules and propose Module-wise Learning Rate Scaling via SNR (MoLS). MoLS estimates module-level signal-to-noise ratios (SNRs) to scale Adam updates, enabling automated module-wise learning rate allocation without manual tuning.
AI.result: Empirical results across multiple LLM training benchmarks show that MoLS improves convergence speed and generalization. It achieves performance comparable to carefully tuned module-specific learning rates while remaining compatible with memory-efficient training algorithms.
AI.conclusion: MoLS provides a principled approach to address module-level gradient heterogeneity in LLMs, offering automated learning rate allocation that improves optimization without the computational cost and generalization limitations of manual tuning methods.

title: HaM-World: Soft-Hamiltonian World Models with Selective Memory for Planning
summary: World models enable model-based planning through learned latent dynamics, but imagined rollouts become unstable as the planning horizon grows or the dynamics distribution shifts. We argue that this instability reflects two missing structures in planner-facing latents: history-conditioned memory for approximate Markov completeness, and geometric organization that separates configuration, momentum, and task semantics. We propose HaM-World (HMW), a structured world model that decomposes the latent state into a canonical (q, p) subspace and a context subspace c, while using Mamba selective state-space memory as the history-conditioned input to the same latent dynamics. Within this interface, (q, p) evolves through an energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residual/control dynamics, while c captures semantic, dissipative, and non-conservative factors. This gives the planner a single latent state shared by dynamics prediction, reward/value estimation, imagined rollouts, and CEM action search. On four DeepMind Control Suite tasks, HaM-World reaches the highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%), reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of a strong baseline model, and wins 11/12 k in {3,5,7} MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations spanning dynamics shifts, action delay, and observation masking, HaM-World achieves the highest return in every condition, with average OOD-return gains of 10.2% on Finger Spin and 13.6% on Reacher Easy. Mechanism diagnostics further show bounded action-free Hamiltonian-energy drift, structured energy variation under policy rollouts, and coherent control-induced energy transfer, supporting the intended Soft-Hamiltonian dynamics design.

AI.tldr: HaM-World is a structured world model that decomposes latent state into canonical (q,p) Hamiltonian subspace and context subspace, using Mamba memory for history conditioning, achieving improved long-horizon planning stability and better OOD performance.
AI.motivation: World models suffer from instability in long-horizon planning when dynamics distribution shifts, due to missing structures in planner-facing latents: history-conditioned memory and geometric organization separating configuration, momentum, and task semantics.
AI.method: Proposes HaM-World with latent state decomposed into canonical (q,p) subspace (for Hamiltonian dynamics) and context subspace c (for semantic/dissipative factors). Uses Mamba selective state-space memory as history-conditioned input to latent dynamics, with (q,p) evolving through energy-derived Hamiltonian vector field plus learnable residuals.
AI.result: Achieves highest Avg. AUC (117.9, +9.5%) on DeepMind Control Suite tasks, reduces long-horizon rollout error to 45% of baseline, wins 11/12 MSE cells. Under 12 OOD perturbations, achieves highest return in every condition with 10.2-13.6% average gains. Shows bounded Hamiltonian-energy drift and coherent energy transfer.
AI.conclusion: HaM-World's structured decomposition of latent state with Hamiltonian dynamics and Mamba memory provides a stable interface for planning, improving long-horizon performance and OOD robustness through principled geometric organization of latent space.

title: Reward Shaping and Action Masking for Compositional Tasks using Behavior Trees and LLMs
summary: Decomposing complex tasks into a sequence of simpler subtasks can improve learning efficiency for an autonomous agent. Reinforcement learning (RL) can be used to optimize agent policies to complete subtasks, but requires well-defined subtask rewards and benefits from action masking. Recent work uses large language models (LLMs) to automate reward shaping and action masking, however none of them fully address reactivity to subtask failure and modularity to varying objects for compositional tasks. To overcome these challenges, we develop masking reward behavior tree (MRBT), a symbolic structure used as a reactive and modular reward and action mask function. We design an MRBT template and derive logical specifications to construct and verify MRBTs for a sequence of object-interaction subtasks. Further, we develop an automated pipeline that uses an LLM to generate MRBTs robust to varying task objects, an SMT-solver to verify correctness of specifications, and a neurosymbolic RL loop to train agents on compositional tasks. Experiments demonstrate successful generation and refinement of five MRBTs, consistently improving training efficiency and task success rates over baselines and MRBTs without action masking. We further highlight three advantages of MRBTs: transferability, modularity, and verifiability.

AI.tldr: MRBT (Masking Reward Behavior Tree) uses symbolic structures with LLMs for reactive, modular reward shaping and action masking in compositional reinforcement learning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-based approaches for automated reward shaping and action masking in RL don't fully address reactivity to subtask failure and modularity to varying objects for compositional tasks.
AI.method: Develop MRBT as symbolic structure for reactive/modular reward/action masking. Design MRBT template with logical specifications, create automated pipeline using LLM to generate MRBTs, SMT-solver for verification, and neurosymbolic RL loop for training.
AI.result: Successfully generated and refined five MRBTs, consistently improving training efficiency and task success rates over baselines and MRBTs without action masking. Demonstrated advantages in transferability, modularity, and verifiability.
AI.conclusion: MRBT provides an effective framework for compositional RL tasks by combining symbolic structures with LLMs for automated reward shaping and action masking, addressing reactivity and modularity limitations of previous approaches.

title: Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust Learning for Debiased Knowledge Tracing
summary: Knowledge Tracing (KT) is fundamental to intelligent education systems, yet relies on educational logs that are selectively observed. The non-random nature of exercise recommendations and student choices inevitably induces severe selection bias. Most existing KT methods neglect this issue, training on observed logs using standard empirical risk, which yields biased mastery estimates and accumulates errors in subsequent recommendations. To address this, we introduce a doubly robust (DR) formulation for KT that integrates a propensity model with an error imputation model, theoretically guaranteeing unbiasedness if either model is accurate. Beyond unbiasedness, in the sequential setting of KT, we identify that the estimator's performance is compromised by variance-dependent stochastic deviations that accumulate over time, thereby causing training instability and limiting performance. To mitigate this, we derive a generalization bound that explicitly characterizes the impact of estimator variance and identifies temporal smoothness as a key factor in controlling it. Building on these theoretical insights, we propose the Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework. TSDR jointly optimizes the KT predictor and the imputation model with a smoothness regularizer, effectively reducing variance while preserving the unbiasedness guarantee of DR. Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones, underscoring the vital role of principled bias correction in KT.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes TSDR, a temporal smoothness doubly robust framework for knowledge tracing that addresses selection bias in educational logs by combining propensity and imputation models with smoothness regularization to reduce variance accumulation over time.
AI.motivation: Knowledge tracing relies on educational logs that suffer from selection bias due to non-random exercise recommendations and student choices. Most existing methods ignore this bias, using standard empirical risk minimization that yields biased mastery estimates and accumulates errors in subsequent recommendations.
AI.method: The paper introduces a doubly robust (DR) formulation that integrates a propensity model with an error imputation model, theoretically guaranteeing unbiasedness if either model is accurate. To address variance-dependent stochastic deviations that accumulate over time, they derive a generalization bound that identifies temporal smoothness as key to controlling variance. They propose the Temporal Smoothness Doubly Robust (TSDR) framework that jointly optimizes the KT predictor and imputation model with a smoothness regularizer.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple real-world benchmarks demonstrate that TSDR consistently enhances various state-of-the-art KT backbones, showing the importance of principled bias correction in knowledge tracing.
AI.conclusion: The TSDR framework effectively addresses selection bias in knowledge tracing by combining doubly robust estimation with temporal smoothness regularization, providing both unbiasedness guarantees and reduced variance accumulation over time, leading to improved performance across different KT backbones.

title: Selective Rollout: Mid-Trajectory Termination for Multi-Sample Agent RL
summary: Group-relative RL training (GRPO) samples a small group of parallel rollouts for every training prompt and uses their within-group reward spread to compute per-trajectory advantages. In agentic environments each rollout is a long multi-turn dialogue with one LLM call per step, so this multi-sample multiplier dominates the total training cost. When every rollout of a prompt ends with the same reward, the group has zero reward variance and contributes no gradient, so the extra rollouts add no information; such groups are common in practice (typically around 40% of all groups), so the wasted-compute fraction is substantial rather than marginal. Existing methods filter such groups at the prompt level, either after their rollouts are paid for or before any rollout begins, but both decide without using information that becomes available during the rollout itself. We instead ask whether the in-group divergence between the partial trajectories at an intermediate step can already predict that the group will be zero-variance: when the parallel rollouts have already converged on the same action prefix, the group is on track to produce a single reward, and we can stop early. We propose a one-parameter gate that stops a group when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between its partial action sequences falls below a threshold. On a 60-iteration on-policy GRPO run on ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B, averaged over four random seeds, the gated arm finishes 10.7% faster in wall-clock (bootstrap 95% CI excludes 0) and shifts held-out success rate on 50 unseen tasks by +2.5 pp, with the held-out gain tracing to a measurable reduction in zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution. Code is available at https://github.com/zhiyuanZhai20/selective-rollout.

AI.tldr: This paper proposes a gating mechanism to stop wasteful parallel rollouts early in Group-relative RL training when trajectories converge to the same actions, reducing training time by ~10.7% while improving performance.
AI.motivation: In Group-relative RL training (GRPO), many rollouts are wasted when all parallel rollouts end with the same reward (zero reward variance), which happens ~40% of the time. Current methods either filter groups after rollouts are complete (wasting compute) or before rollouts begin (missing intermediate information).
AI.method: Proposes a one-parameter gate that stops a group early when the mean pairwise prefix edit distance between partial action sequences falls below a threshold, predicting when the group will have zero variance before completion.
AI.result: On ALFWorld with Qwen2.5-7B, the gated arm finished 10.7% faster in wall-clock time (statistically significant) and improved held-out success rate on 50 unseen tasks by +2.5 percentage points, attributed to reduced zero-advantage gradient-batch dilution.
AI.conclusion: Early stopping based on trajectory convergence during rollouts can substantially reduce wasteful compute in GRPO while improving training efficiency and downstream performance.

title: From Coordinate Matching to Structural Alignment: Rethinking Prototype Alignment in Heterogeneous Federated Learning
summary: Heterogeneous federated learning (HtFL) aims to enable collaboration among clients that differ in both data distributions and model architectures. Prototype-based methods, which communicate class-level feature centers (prototypes) instead of full model parameters, have recently shown strong potential for HtFL. Existing prototype-based HtFL methods typically reuse the MSE-based or cosine-based alignment mechanism developed for homogeneous FL when aligning client-specific representations with global prototypes. These approaches are essentially coordinate alignment, where representations of clients are forced to match the global prototypes in the embedding space in an element-wise manner. Such alignment implicitly assumes that all clients should map their representations into the feature subspace defined by the global prototypes. This assumption is reasonable in homogeneous FL, where all clients share the same feature extractor. However, it becomes problematic in HtFL, since heterogeneous feature extractors naturally induce client-specific feature subspaces, and forcing all clients to optimize within a single global subspace unnecessarily suppresses their learning capacity. We observe that coordinate alignment implicitly couples two distinct objectives: aligning inter-class semantic structure, which is directly beneficial for classification, and enforcing a shared feature basis, which is unnecessary and even harmful under model heterogeneity. Building on this insight, we design FedSAF, which shifts the alignment objective from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. We demonstrate that structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings. Experiments on multiple benchmarks show that our structural alignment outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52\%.

AI.tldr: FedSAF introduces structural alignment for heterogeneous federated learning, focusing on preserving inter-class relational structure instead of forcing coordinate alignment in a shared feature subspace.
AI.motivation: Current prototype-based HtFL methods use coordinate alignment mechanisms (MSE/cosine-based) developed for homogeneous FL, which force client representations to match global prototypes element-wise. This assumes all clients should use the same feature subspace, which is problematic for HtFL where clients have heterogeneous feature extractors with client-specific feature subspaces. This unnecessarily suppresses learning capacity.
AI.method: The paper proposes FedSAF, which shifts alignment from absolute coordinates to inter-class relational structure. It decouples alignment of inter-class semantic structure (beneficial for classification) from enforcing a shared feature basis (harmful under heterogeneity). The method uses structural alignment instead of coordinate alignment.
AI.result: Experiments on multiple benchmarks show FedSAF outperforms state-of-the-art prototype-based HtFL methods by up to 3.52%. Structural alignment consistently outperforms coordinate alignment in heterogeneous settings.
AI.conclusion: For heterogeneous federated learning, preserving inter-class relational structure through structural alignment is more effective than forcing coordinate alignment in a shared feature subspace, as it better accommodates client-specific feature subspaces inherent in heterogeneous models.

title: Retrieval from Within: An Intrinsic Capability of Attention-Based Models
summary: Retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) typically treats retrieval and generation as separate systems. We ask whether an attention-based encoder-decoder can instead retrieve directly from its own internal representations. We introduce INTRA (INTrinsic Retrieval via Attention), a framework where decoder attention queries score pre-encoded evidence chunks that are then directly reused as context for generation. By construction, INTRA unifies retrieval and generation, eliminating the retriever-generator mismatch typical of RAG pipelines. This design also amortizes context encoding by reusing precomputed encoder states across queries. On question-answering benchmarks, INTRA outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines on both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality. Our results demonstrate that attention-based models already possess a retrieval mechanism that can be elicited, rather than added as an external module.

AI.tldr: INTRA is a retrieval-augmented generation framework that uses attention mechanisms for intrinsic retrieval from a model's own representations, eliminating the need for separate retrieval modules and improving performance on QA tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional RAG systems treat retrieval and generation as separate components, leading to retriever-generator mismatch problems. The authors want to explore whether attention-based models can retrieve directly from their own internal representations rather than relying on external retrieval systems.
AI.method: INTRA uses decoder attention queries to score pre-encoded evidence chunks, which are then directly reused as context for generation. This unifies retrieval and generation into a single attention-based system and amortizes context encoding costs by reusing precomputed encoder states across queries.
AI.result: On question-answering benchmarks, INTRA outperforms strong engineered retrieval pipelines in both evidence recall and end-to-end answer quality.
AI.conclusion: Attention-based models already possess an inherent retrieval mechanism that can be elicited without adding external modules, suggesting a more unified approach to retrieval-augmented generation.

title: TheraAgent: Self-Improving Therapeutic Agent for Precise and Comprehensive Treatment Planning
summary: Formulating a treatment plan is inherently a complex reasoning and refinement task rather than a simple generation problem. However, existing large language models (LLMs) mainly rely on one-shot output without explicit verification, which may result in rough, incomplete, and potentially unsafe treatment plans. To address these limitations, we propose TheraAgent, an agentic framework that replaces one-shot generation with an iterative generate-judge-refine pipeline. By mirroring the actual reasoning process of human experts who iteratively revise treatment plans, our framework progressively transforms coarse and incomplete drafts into precise, comprehensive, and safer therapeutic regimens. To facilitate the critical judge component, we introduce TheraJudge, a treatment-specific evaluation module integrated into the inference loop to enforce clinical standards. Experiments show TheraAgent achieves state-of-the-art results on HealthBench, leading in Accuracy and Completeness. In expert evaluations, it attains an 86% win rate against physicians, with superior Targeting and Harm Control. Moreover, the highly agreement between TheraJudge and HealthBench evaluations confirms the reliability of our framework.
