title: Joint Consistency: A Unified Test-Time Aggregation Framework via Energy Minimization
summary: This paper studies test-time aggregation, an approach that generates multiple reasoning traces and aggregates them into a final answer. Most existing methods rely on evaluation signals collected from candidate traces in isolation or answer frequencies, while ignoring comparative interactions among candidates. We propose Joint Consistency (JC), formulated as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization problem, where independent evaluation signals act as external fields and pairwise comparisons act as interactions. JC provides a unified framework for test-time aggregation that subsumes existing voting and weighted aggregation methods as special cases. Our construction of the interaction matrix leverages LLM-as-a-judge comparisons, and admits a theoretical interpretation under answer-level homogeneity assumptions. Moreover, we develop an efficient approximation strategy that makes interaction modeling practical for large-scale test-time aggregation. Experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show that JC consistently outperforms existing baselines across tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Joint Consistency (JC), a unified framework for test-time aggregation that models pairwise interactions between multiple reasoning traces, outperforming existing voting and weighted aggregation methods on math and code reasoning tasks.
AI.motivation: Existing test-time aggregation methods generate multiple reasoning traces but aggregate them using only isolated evaluation signals or answer frequencies, ignoring valuable comparative interactions between candidate traces. This limits their ability to leverage the full information available from multiple reasoning paths.
AI.method: The authors propose Joint Consistency (JC), formulated as a constrained Ising-type energy minimization problem. The method uses independent evaluation signals as external fields and pairwise comparisons (obtained via LLM-as-a-judge) as interactions. They develop an efficient approximation strategy to make interaction modeling practical for large-scale aggregation.
AI.result: Experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show that JC consistently outperforms existing baselines across different tasks, judge models, trace budgets, and trace-generation settings. The framework subsumes existing voting and weighted aggregation methods as special cases.
AI.conclusion: Modeling pairwise interactions between reasoning traces through Joint Consistency provides a more effective test-time aggregation framework than existing methods, offering both theoretical foundations and practical implementation through efficient approximations.

title: Does Synthetic Data Help? Empirical Evidence from Deep Learning Time Series Forecasters
summary: Synthetic data has transformed language model training, yet its role in time series forecasting remains poorly understood. We present a large-scale empirical study: nine experiment groups, 4,218 runs systematically evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signals and seven datasets. The effect is sharply architecture-conditional: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in the majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) are consistently degraded. In selected low-resource settings the gains are striking: TimesNet trained on only 10\% of Weather data with synthetic augmentation surpasses the full-data baseline (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Averaged across all architectures, augmentation hurts in 67\% of trials. We further find that only the Seasonal-Trend generator reliably helps across the tested benchmarks, and that hard curriculum switching is actively harmful (+24\% MSE degradation). These results provide concrete, actionable guidelines on how to use synthetic data: use synthetic augmentation with channel-mixing architectures, use gradual annealing schedules, and treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent. Code is available at \href{https://github.com/hugoiscracked/synthetic-ts/tree/main}

AI.tldr: Synthetic data for time series forecasting works well only with channel-mixing architectures, harms channel-independent models, helps most in low-resource settings, and requires careful implementation choices.
AI.motivation: While synthetic data has been transformative for language models, its effectiveness and proper use in time series forecasting remains poorly understood and lacks systematic empirical guidance.
AI.method: Conducted large-scale empirical study with 4,218 runs across nine experiment groups, evaluating synthetic time series augmentation across five architectures, four synthetic signal generators, and seven datasets under systematic conditions.
AI.result: Architecture-dependent effects: channel-mixing models (TimesNet, iTransformer) benefit in majority of trials, while channel-independent models (DLinear, PatchTST) consistently degrade. Low-resource settings show striking gains (4 of 16 sparsity-dataset combinations). Seasonal-Trend generator works reliably; hard curriculum switching harms performance (+24% MSE degradation). Overall augmentation hurts in 67% of trials across architectures.
AI.conclusion: Concrete guidelines: use synthetic augmentation only with channel-mixing architectures, implement gradual annealing schedules, treat low-resource augmentation as architecture- and dataset-dependent, and avoid hard curriculum switching.

title: Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment for Ambiguous User Queries
summary: Natural-language instance navigation becomes challenging when the initial user request does not uniquely specify the target instance. A practical agent should reduce the user's burden by actively asking only the information needed to distinguish the target from similar distractors, rather than requiring a detailed description upfront. Existing approaches often fall short of this goal: they may stop at the first plausible candidate before sufficiently exploring alternatives, or, even after collecting multiple candidates, ask about the target's attributes derived from individual candidates rather than questions selected to distinguish candidates in the pool. As a result, despite the dialogue, the agent may still fail to distinguish the target from distractors, leading to premature decisions and lengthy user responses. We propose Proactive Instance Navigation with Comparative Judgment (ProCompNav), a two-stage framework that first constructs a candidate pool and then identifies the target through comparative judgment. At each round, ProCompNav extracts an attribute-value pair that splits the current pool, asks a binary yes/no question, and prunes all inconsistent candidates at once. This reframes disambiguation from open-ended target description to pool-level discriminative questioning, where each question is chosen to narrow the candidate set. On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with the same minimal input and non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while substantially reducing Response Length. ProCompNav also achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, suggesting that comparative judgment is broadly useful for instance-level navigation among similar distractors.

AI.tldr: ProCompNav is a proactive dialogue agent that identifies target instances by asking discriminative yes/no questions to efficiently distinguish among candidate options, reducing user burden compared to existing approaches.
AI.motivation: Existing instance navigation approaches are inefficient when initial user requests are ambiguous. They either stop too early at plausible candidates or ask suboptimal attribute questions derived from individual candidates rather than questions designed to distinguish among multiple candidates. This leads to premature decisions and lengthy user responses.
AI.method: ProCompNav uses a two-stage framework: 1) constructs a candidate pool of possible instances, 2) identifies the target through comparative judgment. At each round, it extracts attribute-value pairs that split the current candidate pool, asks binary yes/no questions, and prunes inconsistent candidates, effectively reframing disambiguation as pool-level discriminative questioning.
AI.result: On CoIN-Bench, ProCompNav improves Success Rate over interactive baselines with minimal input and non-interactive baselines with detailed descriptions, while substantially reducing Response Length. It also achieves state-of-the-art Success Rate on TextNav, demonstrating the broad utility of comparative judgment for instance-level navigation.
AI.conclusion: Comparative judgment via discriminative questioning is an effective approach for instance navigation among similar distractors. The ProCompNav framework successfully reduces user burden by asking only necessary information to distinguish targets from alternatives, outperforming existing methods in both success rate and efficiency.

title: Optimal Transport for LLM Reward Modeling from Noisy Preference
summary: Reward models are fundamental to Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF), yet real-world datasets are inevitably corrupted by noisy preference. Conventional training objectives tend to overfit these errors, while existing denoising approaches often rely on homogeneous noise assumptions that fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences. To handle these challenges, we propose SelectiveRM, a framework grounded in optimal transport. We first devise a Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align the distribution of model predictions with preference data. Furthermore, to address the limitation of strict mass conservation which compels the model to fit outliers, we incorporate a Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport. This enables the autonomous exclusion of samples with noisy preference that contradict semantic consistency. Theoretically, we demonstrate that SelectiveRM optimizes a tighter upper bound on the unobserved clean risk. Extensive experiments validate that our approach significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.

AI.tldr: SelectiveRM is a new RLHF framework that uses optimal transport with mass relaxation to autonomously filter noisy preference data, achieving better performance than existing methods.
AI.motivation: Real-world datasets for RLHF reward models contain inevitable noisy preferences, and conventional training methods overfit to these errors. Existing denoising approaches fail to capture the complexity of linguistic preferences due to restrictive assumptions.
AI.method: SelectiveRM uses optimal transport theory with two key innovations: 1) Joint Consistency Discrepancy to align model predictions with preference data, and 2) Mass Relaxation mechanism via partial transport that allows autonomous exclusion of noisy samples contradicting semantic consistency.
AI.result: The approach optimizes a tighter upper bound on unobserved clean risk theoretically, and extensive experiments show it significantly outperforms state-of-the-art baselines across diverse benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: SelectiveRM effectively addresses the noisy preference problem in RLHF reward models through a theoretically grounded optimal transport framework with adaptive denoising capabilities.

title: A Versatile AI Agent for Rare Disease Diagnosis and Risk Gene Prioritization
summary: Accurate and timely diagnosis is essential for effective treatment, particularly in the context of rare diseases. However, current diagnostic workflows often lead to prolonged assessment times and low accuracy. To address these limitations, we introduce Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent system designed to support precision disease diagnosis by integrating diverse data sources, including phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records. Hygieia features a router-based and knowledge-enhanced framework that mitigates hallucination and tailors diagnostic strategies to different disease categories. Notably, it prioritizes risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases and provides confidence scores to assist clinical decision-making. We conducted a comprehensive evaluation demonstrating that Hygieia achieves state-of-the-art performance across multiple diagnostic benchmarks. In collaboration with clinical experts from Yale School of Medicine and Duke-NUS Medical School, we further validated its practical utility by showing (1) Hygieia's superior diagnostic performance compared to physicians with an improvement from 12%-60% and (2) its effectiveness in assisting clinicians with medical records for handling real-world cases. Our findings indicate that Hygieia not only enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability but also significantly reduces clinician workload, highlighting its potential as a valuable tool in clinical decision support systems.

AI.tldr: Hygieia is a multi-modal AI agent that integrates phenotypic, genetic, and clinical data to improve rare disease diagnosis, outperforming physicians by 12-60% and reducing clinician workload.
AI.motivation: Current diagnostic workflows for rare diseases suffer from prolonged assessment times and low accuracy, creating a need for more efficient and precise diagnostic support systems.
AI.method: Developed Hygieia, a multi-modal AI agent system with a router-based and knowledge-enhanced framework that integrates phenotypic features, genetic profiles, and clinical records, prioritizes risk-related genomic factors for rare diseases, and provides confidence scores for clinical decision-making.
AI.result: Hygieia achieved state-of-the-art performance across multiple diagnostic benchmarks, showed 12-60% improvement over physicians' diagnostic accuracy, and was validated as effective in assisting clinicians with real-world cases.
AI.conclusion: Hygieia enhances diagnostic accuracy and interpretability while significantly reducing clinician workload, demonstrating strong potential as a valuable clinical decision support tool for rare disease diagnosis.

title: Requests of a Feather Must Flock Together: Batch Size vs. Prefix Homogeneity in LLM Inference
summary: Auto-regressive token generation in large language models is memory-bound because it requires "attending to" key and value tensors (KV cache) of all previous tokens. Prior work aims to improve the efficiency of this decode process by batching multiple requests together, and maximizing batch size subject to GPU memory constraints. The key observation of our work is that with prefix-sharing workloads, smaller, prefix-homogeneous batches -- where all requests share a common prefix -- can achieve higher decode throughput than larger, heterogeneous batches, due to better spatial and temporal locality during KV cache accesses. However, prefix-aware schedulers in state-of-the-art inference engines maximize prefix reuse within a batch only to reduce KV cache memory footprint, but do not stop batch formation at smaller homogeneous batches that could have performed better. Further, we show that shared prefix detection in existing schedulers relies on radix-tree traversals, incurring substantial CPU overhead that is often comparable to GPU execution time. This paper presents Feather, a prefix-aware scheduler that uses reinforcement learning (RL) to learn the optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. We also introduce Chunked Hash Tree (CHT), a lightweight data structure that enables fast prefix detection and efficient request selection for the RL scheduler, avoiding expensive tree traversals. We integrate Feather into vLLM and SGLang, and our evaluation shows that Feather achieves 2--10$\times$ higher end-to-end throughput as compared to existing schedulers, while doing no worse than the status quo when the workload does not have enough prefix sharing. Feather achieves these gains by reducing the total number of KV cache accesses, surpassing the performance of prefix-aware attention kernels that have the same goal.

AI.tldr: Feather is a prefix-aware scheduler for LLM inference that uses reinforcement learning to optimize the tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity, achieving 2-10x higher throughput through better KV cache locality.
AI.motivation: Auto-regressive token generation in LLMs is memory-bound due to KV cache accesses. Existing schedulers don't optimize for prefix homogeneity within batches, missing opportunities for better spatial/temporal locality. Current prefix detection methods have high CPU overhead from radix-tree traversals.
AI.method: 1) Reinforcement learning scheduler (Feather) learns optimal tradeoff between batch size and prefix homogeneity. 2) Chunked Hash Tree (CHT) data structure for fast prefix detection without expensive tree traversals. 3) Integrated into vLLM and SGLang frameworks.
AI.result: Feather achieves 2-10x higher end-to-end throughput compared to existing schedulers. It reduces total KV cache accesses, outperforming prefix-aware attention kernels. Maintains same performance as status quo when workloads lack prefix sharing.
AI.conclusion: Optimizing for prefix homogeneity rather than just maximizing batch size significantly improves LLM inference efficiency. Feather's RL-based scheduling with lightweight prefix detection (CHT) provides substantial throughput gains for prefix-sharing workloads while maintaining compatibility with existing frameworks.

title: Price of Fairness in Short-Term and Long-Term Algorithmic Selections
comment: The short version of this paper appears in the proceedings of IJCAI-26
summary: Algorithmic decision-making in high-stakes settings can have profound impacts on individuals and populations. While much prior work studies fairness in static settings, recent results show that enforcing static fairness constraints may exacerbate long-run disparities. Motivated by this tension, we study a stylized sequential selection problem in which a decision-maker repeatedly selects individuals, affecting both immediate utility and the population distribution over time. We introduce notions of group fairness for both the short and long term and theoretically analyze the trade-off between fairness and utility via the Price of Fairness (PoF). We characterize optimal and fair policies in the short term and show that the PoF can be large even when group distributions are nearly identical. In contrast, we show that long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve a low PoF. We also empirically validate these theoretical observations using both synthetic and real datasets.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes fairness in sequential selection problems, showing that static fairness constraints may worsen long-term disparities, but simple investment policies can achieve both fairness and utility over time.
AI.motivation: Algorithmic decision-making in high-stakes settings impacts individuals and populations, and while much work studies fairness in static settings, recent results show that enforcing static fairness constraints may exacerbate long-run disparities, creating a tension between short-term fairness and long-term outcomes.
AI.method: The authors study a stylized sequential selection problem where a decision-maker repeatedly selects individuals, affecting both immediate utility and population distribution over time. They introduce notions of group fairness for both short and long term, theoretically analyze trade-offs via Price of Fairness (PoF), characterize optimal fair policies, and empirically validate findings with synthetic and real datasets.
AI.result: Theoretical analysis shows that Price of Fairness can be large even when group distributions are nearly identical in the short term, but long-term disparities can vanish under simple investment policies that achieve low Price of Fairness. Empirical validation on synthetic and real datasets supports these theoretical observations.
AI.conclusion: The paper demonstrates that focusing solely on short-term static fairness may worsen long-term disparities, but well-designed sequential selection policies can achieve both fairness and utility over time, suggesting the importance of considering temporal dynamics in algorithmic fairness.

title: TFM-Retouche: A Lightweight Input-Space Adapter for Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models (TFMs), such as TabPFN-2.6, TabICLv2, ConTextTab, Mitra, LimiX, and TabDPT, achieve strong zero-shot performance through in-context learning, but their inductive biases remain fixed at inference time. Adapting a pretrained TFM to a specific dataset or task typically requires either full fine-tuning, which is computationally expensive, or parameter-efficient tuning methods (PEFT) such as LoRA, which must be tailored to the internal architecture of each TFM. Furthermore, the evidence on whether weight-space fine-tuning improves accuracy or calibration is mixed \citep{tanna_exploring_2026,rubachev_finetuning_2025}. We introduce TFM-Retouche, a lightweight input-space residual adapter that is architecture-agnostic by design with respect to the frozen TFM backbone. TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in the input space to align the input data with the inductive biases of the pretrained model. The adapter is trained end-to-end through the frozen TFM, with a post-training identity guard that falls back to the unmodified TFM whenever adaptation does not help on held-out validation. On TabArena-Lite (51 datasets spanning binary classification, multiclass classification, and regression), TabICLv2-Retouche -- the framework instantiated on TabICLv2 -- is the top-ranked method on the leaderboard with light per-task tuning and ensembling, lifting aggregate Elo by +56 over the frozen TabICLv2 base and sitting on the Pareto front of predictive quality versus both training and inference time.

AI.tldr: TFM-Retouche introduces a lightweight, architecture-agnostic input-space residual adapter that fine-tunes tabular foundation models by learning small input corrections rather than modifying model weights, achieving top performance on TabArena-Lite with minimal tuning.
AI.motivation: Current tabular foundation models (TFMs) have fixed inductive biases at inference time, and adapting them requires expensive full fine-tuning or architecture-specific parameter-efficient tuning methods (PEFT). Existing weight-space fine-tuning approaches show mixed results on accuracy and calibration improvements.
AI.method: TFM-Retouche learns a small residual correction in the input space to align input data with the pretrained model's inductive biases. It's an architecture-agnostic adapter that trains end-to-end through the frozen TFM backbone, with a post-training identity guard that reverts to the unmodified TFM when adaptation doesn't help on validation data.
AI.result: TabICLv2-Retouche (instantiated on TabICLv2) becomes the top-ranked method on TabArena-Lite leaderboard (51 datasets covering binary/multiclass classification and regression), achieving +56 aggregate Elo improvement over frozen TabICLv2 base. It sits on the Pareto front for predictive quality vs. both training and inference time.
AI.conclusion: TFM-Retouche provides an effective, lightweight approach for adapting tabular foundation models that is architecture-agnostic, computationally efficient, and outperforms existing tuning methods while maintaining the original model's calibration and requiring minimal per-task adaptation.

title: Safactory: A Scalable Agent Factory for Trustworthy Autonomous Intelligence
summary: As large models evolve from conversational assistants into autonomous agents, challenges increasingly arise from long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real environment interaction. Existing agenticinfrastructure remain fragmented across evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, making it difficult to discover risks systematically and improve models in a continuous closed loop. In this report, we present \textbf{Safactory}, a scalable agent factory for trustworthy autonomous intelligence. Safactory integrates three tightly coupled platforms: a \textbf{Parallel Simulation Platform} for trajectory generation, a \textbf{Trustworthy Data Platform} for trajectory storage and experience extraction, and an \textbf{Autonomous Evolution Platform} for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation. As far as we know, Safactory is the first framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy autonomous intelligence.

AI.tldr: Safactory is a scalable agent factory framework that integrates simulation, data management, and evolution platforms for developing trustworthy autonomous AI agents through a unified evolutionary pipeline.
AI.motivation: The paper addresses challenges in autonomous agent development as models evolve from conversational assistants to agents requiring long-horizon decision making, tool use, and real environment interaction. Existing infrastructure is fragmented across evaluation, data management, and agent evolution, making systematic risk discovery and continuous improvement difficult.
AI.method: Safactory integrates three tightly coupled platforms: 1) Parallel Simulation Platform for trajectory generation, 2) Trustworthy Data Platform for trajectory storage and experience extraction, and 3) Autonomous Evolution Platform for asynchronous reinforcement learning and on-policy distillation.
AI.result: The authors present Safactory as the first framework to propose a unified evolutionary pipeline for next-generation trustworthy autonomous intelligence, addressing the fragmentation problem in current agent infrastructure.
AI.conclusion: Safactory provides a scalable solution for developing trustworthy autonomous agents by integrating simulation, data management, and evolution in a continuous closed-loop system, representing a novel approach to systematic agent development and risk discovery.

title: When Brain Networks Travel: Learning Beyond Site
summary: Graph-based learning on functional magnetic resonance imaging (fMRI) has shown strong potential for brain network analysis. However, existing methods degrade under cross-site out-of-distribution (OOD) settings because site-conditioned confounders induce non-pathological shortcuts, while functional connectivity constructed by temporal averaging obscures transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites. In this paper, we propose Cross-site OOD Robust brain nEtwork (CORE), a unified framework for brain network learning across unseen sites. CORE first performs site-aware confounder decoupling to mitigate site-conditioned bias and extract a cross-site population scaffold of reproducible diagnostic connectivity edges. It then profiles transient pathway dynamics over this scaffold using lightweight temporal descriptors and organizes scaffold edges into a line graph for transferable pathway-level modeling. Finally, CORE introduces a prior-guided subject-adaptive gating mechanism that leverages scaffold-derived population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability. Extensive experiments under leave-one-site-out evaluation on real-world datasets (ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD) show that CORE consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines, with up to 6.7% relative gain. Furthermore, CORE remains robust to atlas variations, maintaining performance gains across different brain parcellation schemes.

AI.tldr: CORE is a brain network learning framework that handles cross-site out-of-distribution challenges through site-aware confounder decoupling, temporal pathway dynamics modeling on population scaffolds, and adaptive gating mechanisms.
AI.motivation: Existing graph-based fMRI methods degrade in cross-site OOD settings due to site-conditioned confounders creating non-pathological shortcuts, and temporal averaging in functional connectivity obscures transient neurodynamics, limiting generalization to unseen sites.
AI.method: CORE framework: 1) Site-aware confounder decoupling to extract cross-site population scaffolds of diagnostic connectivity edges, 2) Profiles transient pathway dynamics using lightweight temporal descriptors over scaffolds organized as line graphs, 3) Prior-guided subject-adaptive gating that leverages population priors while preserving subject-specific connectivity variability.
AI.result: Extensive leave-one-site-out experiments on ABIDE, REST-meta-MDD, SRPBS, and ABCD datasets show CORE consistently outperforms SOTA baselines with up to 6.7% relative gain. Remains robust to atlas variations across different brain parcellation schemes.
AI.conclusion: CORE provides a unified framework for robust brain network learning across unseen sites by addressing site-conditioned bias and temporal dynamics limitations, enabling better generalization in cross-site OOD settings.

title: Data Language Models: A New Foundation Model Class for Tabular Data
summary: Every major data modality now has a foundation model that understands it natively: text has language models, images have vision models, audio has audio models. Tabular data, the modality on which many consequential real-world AI decisions are made, does not. Every approach to tabular AI today, from gradient-boosted trees to the latest tabular foundation models, requires a preprocessing pipeline before any model can consume the data. None of them understand tabular data as a modality. We introduce the Data Language Model (DLM), the missing foundation model for tabular data. A DLM understands tables the way a language model understands sentences: natively, without serialization or preprocessing, directly from raw cell values. It is the tabular data layer on which AI models, agents, and vertical AI applications can be built, eliminating the preprocessing pipelines that currently stand between raw data and every AI system that consumes it. We present Schema-1, the first DLM: a 140M parameter model trained on more than 2.3M synthetic and real-world tabular datasets. Schema-1 outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and the tabular foundation models we evaluate on established row-level prediction benchmarks. On missing value reconstruction it achieves lower reconstruction error than all classical statistical methods and frontier large language models on mean performance across conditions, establishing that structural understanding of a dataset's own distributional geometry is more useful for imputation than world knowledge encoded in language. It identifies the industry sector of any unseen dataset from raw cell values alone, reliably across any domain, a task no prior tabular model can perform. It is the native tabular understanding layer that has been missing from the AI stack.

AI.tldr: First foundation model that natively understands tabular data without preprocessing, outperforming traditional methods and existing tabular models on prediction and imputation tasks.
AI.motivation: Tabular data lacks a native foundation model like text (language models) and images (vision models). Current tabular AI approaches require preprocessing pipelines and don't understand tabular data as a native modality.
AI.method: Introduced Data Language Model (DLM) that understands tables directly from raw cell values without serialization/preprocessing. Built Schema-1, a 140M parameter model trained on 2.3M+ synthetic and real-world tabular datasets.
AI.result: Outperforms gradient-boosted ensembles, AutoML stacks, and other tabular foundation models on row-level prediction benchmarks. Achieves lower reconstruction error than classical statistical methods and frontier LLMs for missing value imputation. Identifies industry sectors from raw cell values alone across any domain.
AI.conclusion: DLM provides the missing native tabular understanding layer for AI systems, eliminating preprocessing pipelines and enabling direct consumption of raw tabular data.

title: Towards Generation-Efficient Uncertainty Estimation in Large Language Models
comment: 21 pages, 6 figures, and 8 tables. The abstract provided in the metadata differs slightly from the manuscript version due to character limits
summary: Uncertainty estimation is important for deploying LLMs in high-stakes applications such as healthcare and finance, where hallucinations can appear fluent and plausible while being factually incorrect, making it difficult for users to judge whether an output should be trusted. Existing methods require one or more full autoregressive generations to estimate uncertainty, which introduces substantial inference cost and often delays uncertainty assessment. In this paper, we investigate whether effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with partial generation or even input-only information. Specifically, we first develop a unified framework that formulates uncertainty estimation as an early estimation problem over the autoregressive generation process of LLMs. This framework organises existing and proposed estimators by the information they observe, ranging from multi-generation to input-only prediction, and clarifies the performance-cost trade-off underlying different uncertainty estimation methods. Building on this view, we study two largely underexplored low-cost settings: estimating uncertainty with part of the generation, and predicting uncertainty from the input prompt. We propose Logit Magnitude, which uses top-M logit evidence to estimate uncertainty from an early-stopped generation prefix, and MetaUE, which distils generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores. Extensive experiments on general and domain-specific benchmarks show that Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance, and partial generations of LLMs are often sufficient for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE further provides a competitive input-only approximation in several settings. These findings suggest that effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed, enabling unreliable responses to be identified earlier.

AI.tldr: LLM uncertainty estimation can be achieved with partial generation or input-only information using Logit Magnitude and MetaUE methods, reducing inference costs while maintaining effectiveness.
AI.motivation: Uncertainty estimation is crucial for LLMs in high-stakes applications where hallucinations appear fluent but incorrect, but existing methods require full autoregressive generations, introducing substantial inference costs and delaying uncertainty assessment.
AI.method: Developed a unified framework for uncertainty estimation as early estimation over LLM generation process. Proposed Logit Magnitude using top-M logit evidence from early-stopped generation prefixes, and MetaUE which distills generation-based uncertainty into a lightweight input-only estimator trained with uncertainty scores.
AI.result: Logit Magnitude achieves strong performance with partial generations, and partial generations are often sufficient for effective uncertainty estimation. MetaUE provides competitive input-only approximation in several settings, showing effective uncertainty estimation requires less generation than commonly assumed.
AI.conclusion: Effective uncertainty estimation can be achieved with less generation than previously thought, enabling earlier identification of unreliable responses and reducing computational costs while maintaining performance.

title: Addressing Labelled Data Scarcity: Taxonomy-Agnostic Annotation of PII Values in HTTP Traffic using LLMs
comment: Accepted to 2026 IEEE European Symposium on Security and Privacy Workshops (EuroS&PW)
summary: Automated privacy audits of web and mobile applications often analyse outbound HTTP traffic to detect Personally Identifiable Information (PII) leakage. However, existing learning-based detectors typically depend on scarce, manually labelled traffic and are tightly coupled to fixed label taxonomies, limiting transferability across domains and evolving definitions of PII. This paper investigates whether Large Language Models (LLMs) can support taxonomy-agnostic annotation of explicitly transmitted PII values in HTTP message bodies when the taxonomy is provided at runtime. We introduce a multi-stage LLM-based pipeline that combines deterministic pre-processing with label-level classification, targeted instance-level value annotation, and output validation. To enable controlled evaluation and exemplar-based prompting without relying on sensitive real-user captures, we further propose an LLM-based generator for synthetic HTTP traffic with manually validated, taxonomy-derived PII annotations. We evaluate the approach across three taxonomies spanning different PII domains and granularity levels. Results show that the pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values for concrete PII taxonomies. Overall, our findings position LLMs as a promising foundation for flexible, taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for creating labelled data under evolving privacy taxonomies.

AI.tldr: LLMs can be used for taxonomy-agnostic PII detection in HTTP traffic with a multi-stage pipeline that accurately identifies and extracts PII values across different privacy taxonomies.
AI.motivation: Existing automated privacy audits have limitations: they rely on scarce manually labeled traffic, are tied to fixed label taxonomies, and lack transferability across domains and evolving PII definitions.
AI.method: Developed a multi-stage LLM-based pipeline combining deterministic pre-processing, label-level classification, targeted instance-level value annotation, and output validation. Also created an LLM-based generator for synthetic HTTP traffic with validated PII annotations to enable controlled evaluation.
AI.result: The pipeline accurately detects PII types and extracts corresponding values across three different taxonomies spanning various PII domains and granularity levels.
AI.conclusion: LLMs are a promising foundation for flexible, taxonomy-agnostic traffic annotation and for creating labeled data under evolving privacy taxonomies.

title: Towards Self-Explainable Document Visual Question Answering with Chain-of-Explanation Predictions
summary: Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) requires vision-language models to reason not only about what information in a document is relevant to a question, but also where the answer is grounded on the page. Existing DocVQA models entangle question-relevant evidence and answer localization and operate largely as black boxes, offering limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence. We propose CoExVQA, a self-explainable DocVQA framework with a grounded reasoning process through a chain-of-explanation design. CoExVQA first identifies question-relevant evidence, then explicitly localizes the answer region, and finally decodes the answer exclusively from the grounded region. Prediction via CoExVQA's chain-of-explanation enables direct inspection and verification of the reasoning process across modalities. Empirical results show that restricting decoding to grounded evidence achieves SotA explainable DocVQA performance on PFL-DocVQA, improving ANLS by 12% over the current explainable baselines while providing transparent and verifiable predictions.

AI.tldr: CoExVQA is a self-explainable DocVQA framework that uses a chain-of-explanation design to first identify question-relevant evidence, then explicitly localize the answer region, and finally decode answers exclusively from the grounded region, achieving state-of-the-art performance with transparent reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing Document Visual Question Answering (DocVQA) models entangle question-relevant evidence and answer localization, operating as black boxes with limited means to verify how predictions depend on visual evidence. There's a need for models that offer transparent and verifiable reasoning processes across modalities.
AI.method: CoExVQA employs a chain-of-explanation design that separates reasoning into three explicit steps: 1) Identifying question-relevant evidence, 2) Explicitly localizing the answer region on the document page, and 3) Decoding the answer exclusively from the grounded region. This structured approach enables direct inspection and verification of the reasoning process.
AI.result: CoExVQA achieves state-of-the-art explainable DocVQA performance on the PFL-DocVQA benchmark, improving ANLS (Average Normalized Levenshtein Similarity) by 12% over current explainable baselines. The model provides transparent and verifiable predictions through its grounded reasoning process.
AI.conclusion: The CoExVQA framework successfully addresses the black-box nature of existing DocVQA models by introducing a self-explainable architecture with grounded reasoning. The chain-of-explanation design enables verifiable predictions while maintaining competitive performance, advancing both accuracy and interpretability in document understanding tasks.

title: Measuring Black-Box Confidence via Reasoning Trajectories: Geometry, Coverage, and Verbalization
summary: Reliable confidence estimation enables safe deployment of chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning through text-only APIs. Yet the dominant black-box baseline, self-consistency over K samples, is linearly expensive and ignores the geometry of the trace. We propose a black-box trajectory-confidence score: we embed a CoT as a sliding-window trajectory and measure its convergence to external answer anchors with a one-parameter softmax. The method needs no logits, hidden states, or supervised calibrators. Across six (benchmark, reasoner) settings on MedQA-USMLE, GPQA Diamond, and MMLU-Pro with Gemini 3.1 Pro and Claude Sonnet 4.6, fusing this score with coverage and verbalized-confidence channels at K=4 yields Pareto improvements over self-consistency at K=8 in 6/6 settings (median AUC 0.78 vs 0.71, deltaAUC=+0.075). A fixed-pick control (+0.060) and E5 cross-embedder replication rule out answer switching and single-vendor artifacts. Geometry peaks in the penultimate window across benchmarks and reasoners, and inverts at the terminal window on GPQA Diamond. Three unscaffolded regimes separate black-box confidence into a judge-mediated Coverage prior (C), within-trace Geometry (G), and a conditional Verbalization channel (V). Across 18 benchmark x reasoner x proposer settings, C and G provide independent signal in 18/18 and 16/18, while V contributes residual signal in 6/18. Swapping the judge from GPT-5-mini to Claude Sonnet 4.6 leaves G-only AUC unchanged (|delta|<=0.013) and shifts C-only AUC by at most +/-0.02 (kappa=0.82). Fusion beats the best single channel in 17/18 settings (median AUC 0.78, max 0.92).

AI.tldr: A black-box trajectory-confidence scoring method for chain-of-thought reasoning that measures convergence to answer anchors using sliding-window trajectories, outperforming self-consistency baselines with fewer samples.
AI.motivation: Existing confidence estimation methods for CoT reasoning are expensive (linear cost with K samples in self-consistency) and ignore the geometric structure of reasoning traces. There's a need for black-box approaches that work with text-only APIs without requiring logits, hidden states, or supervised calibration.
AI.method: Proposes a black-box trajectory-confidence score that embeds CoT reasoning as sliding-window trajectories and measures convergence to external answer anchors using a one-parameter softmax. The approach fuses this geometric score with coverage and verbalized-confidence channels. Experiments across multiple benchmarks (MedQA-USMLE, GPQA Diamond, MMLU-Pro) and models (Gemini 3.1 Pro, Claude Sonnet 4.6) with K=4 samples.
AI.result: The fused method yields Pareto improvements over self-consistency at K=8 in all 6 benchmark-reasoner settings (median AUC 0.78 vs 0.71, deltaAUC=+0.075). Geometric signal peaks in penultimate windows across benchmarks and separates black-box confidence into coverage prior, within-trace geometry, and verbalization channels, with geometry providing independent signal in 16/18 settings. Fusion beats best single channel in 17/18 settings.
AI.conclusion: The proposed black-box trajectory-confidence scoring method provides effective confidence estimation for CoT reasoning through text-only APIs, outperforming expensive self-consistency baselines while revealing separable components of confidence estimation: coverage prior, geometric convergence, and verbalized confidence.

title: Geometry-Aware Simplicial Message Passing
summary: The Weisfeiler--Lehman (WL) test and its simplicial extension (SWL) characterize the combinatorial expressivity of message passing networks, but they are blind to geometry, i.e., meshes with identical connectivity but different embeddings are indistinguishable. We introduce the Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler--Lehman (GSWL) test, which incorporates vertex coordinates into color refinement for geometric simplicial complexes. In addition, we show that (i) the expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message passing schemes is bounded above by GSWL, and (ii) that there exist parameters such that the discriminating power of GSWL is matched by these schemes on any fixed finite family of geometric simplicial complexes. Combined with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), a complete invariant for geometric simplicial complexes, this yields a geometric expressivity characterization together with an approximation framework. Experiments on synthetic and mesh datasets serve to validate our theory, showing a clear hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces GSWL, a geometric version of the Weisfeiler-Lehman test that incorporates vertex coordinates to distinguish geometric simplicial complexes beyond just connectivity, establishing theoretical bounds for geometry-aware neural networks and validating them experimentally.
AI.motivation: The standard WL test and its simplicial extension SWL only consider combinatorial connectivity and are blind to geometric information - meshes with identical connectivity but different embeddings are indistinguishable. This creates a gap in characterizing the expressivity of geometry-aware neural networks operating on geometric simplicial complexes.
AI.method: Introduces the Geometric Simplicial Weisfeiler-Lehman (GSWL) test that incorporates vertex coordinates into color refinement for geometric simplicial complexes. Combines GSWL with the Euler Characteristic Transform (ECT), which is a complete invariant for geometric simplicial complexes. Proves theoretical bounds showing GSWL bounds geometry-aware simplicial message passing schemes.
AI.result: Shows that: (i) expressivity of geometry-aware simplicial message passing schemes is bounded above by GSWL, (ii) there exist parameters such that GSWL's discriminating power is matched by these schemes on any fixed finite family of geometric simplicial complexes. Provides experimental validation on synthetic and mesh datasets showing a clear hierarchy from combinatorial to geometry-aware models.
AI.conclusion: The GSWL test provides a comprehensive geometric expressivity characterization and approximation framework for geometry-aware neural networks on simplicial complexes, bridging the gap between combinatorial and geometric analysis while establishing theoretical foundations for the field.

title: A Regime Theory of Controller Class Selection for LLM Action Decisions
summary: Deployed language and vision-language models must decide, on each input, whether to answer directly, retrieve evidence, defer to a stronger model, or abstain. Contrary to the common monotonicity intuition, greater per-input expressivity is not uniformly beneficial in finite samples: under identical strict cross-validation, different benchmarks prefer different controller classes. This reflects a finite-sample limitation of instance-level uncertainty signals, which can be exhausted at a distribution-dependent scale. We organize controllers into a nested lattice of four classes: fixed actions, partition routers, instance-level controllers, and prior-gated controllers, ordered by complexity. We prove a regime theory that turns three data-estimable bottlenecks into a class choice: how much improvement is possible beyond the best fixed action, whether there are enough samples for instance-level controllers to make reliable decisions, and how much improvement a coarse partition router can recover when instance-level signal is unreliable. The resulting Bernstein-tight threshold has a matching information-theoretic lower bound, and strict nested cross-validation provably selects a near-best class. Across SMS-Spam, HallusionBench, A-OKVQA, and FOLIO, the predicted class matches the empirical winner; the prior-gated controller wins on TextVQA when OCR tokens supply a label-free prediction-time prior. Code is available at https://github.com/Anonymous-Awesome-Submissions/Regime-Theory.

AI.tldr: This paper introduces a regime theory for selecting optimal controller classes in language/vision-language models, showing that greater expressivity doesn't always help in finite samples and providing theoretical thresholds for class selection based on data-estimable bottlenecks.
AI.motivation: Deployed models need to decide how to handle each input (answer, retrieve, defer, abstain), but current approaches lack theoretical understanding of when different controller classes are optimal in finite sample settings.
AI.method: Organizes controllers into a nested lattice of four complexity classes, develops a regime theory with Bernstein-tight thresholds based on three data-estimable bottlenecks, and validates with strict nested cross-validation across multiple benchmarks.
AI.result: The theoretical thresholds match empirical winners across benchmarks (SMS-Spam, HallusionBench, A-OKVQA, FOLIO), and the prior-gated controller wins on TextVQA when using OCR tokens as prediction-time priors.
AI.conclusion: Contrary to monotonicity intuition, greater per-input expressivity isn't always beneficial; the proposed regime theory provides principled class selection based on distribution-dependent signal exhaustion, with theoretical guarantees and empirical validation.

title: Causal Reinforcement Learning for Complex Card Games: A Magic The Gathering Benchmark
summary: Causal reinforcement learning (RL) lacks benchmarks for complex systems that combine sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. We introduce MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark built on Magic: The Gathering with a 3,077-dimensional partial observation, a 478-action masked discrete action space, five competitive Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM) over strategic variables. Every episode exposes causal variables, SCM-predicted intervention effects, and per-factor credit traces, making causal credit assignment, leave-one-out cross-archetype transfer, and policy auditability first-class metrics. We adapt a panel of reference baselines: random, heuristic, masked PPO, a causal-world-model PPO variant, and an architecture-matched scalar control. We propose Causal Graph-Factored Advantage PPO (CGFA-PPO) as a reference causal agent that uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets with an intervention-calibration loss. All comparisons use paired seeds, paired-bootstrap confidence intervals, and Holm-Bonferroni correction within pre-registered families. Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reach competitive in-distribution win rates and exceed the random baseline; per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps expose diagnostic structure that scalar win rate alone cannot. We release the benchmark, reference-baseline results, and full evaluation protocol openly. By coupling a strategically rich, partially observed domain with an explicit causal interface and statistical protocol, MTG-Causal-RL gives causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research a shared testbed for questions current benchmarks cannot pose together: causal credit assignment under masked action spaces, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

AI.tldr: MTG-Causal-RL: A Magic: The Gathering-based benchmark for causal reinforcement learning with complex features like sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure.
AI.motivation: Causal RL lacks benchmarks that combine multiple complex challenges: sequential decision making, hidden information, large masked action spaces, and explicit causal structure. Existing benchmarks cannot adequately test causal credit assignment, structural transfer, and policy auditability together.
AI.method: Created MTG-Causal-RL, a Gymnasium benchmark built on Magic: The Gathering with 3,077-dimensional partial observations, 478-action masked discrete action space, five competitive Standard archetypes, three reward schemes, and a hand-specified Structural Causal Model (SCM). Evaluated multiple baselines including random, heuristic, masked PPO, causal-world-model PPO variant, and proposed Causal Graph-Factored Advantage PPO (CGFA-PPO) that uses SCM parents of win probability as factor-aligned critic targets.
AI.result: Masked PPO and CGFA-PPO reached competitive in-distribution win rates and exceeded random baseline. Per-factor calibration trajectories and leave-one-out transfer gaps revealed diagnostic structure that scalar win rate alone cannot capture. The benchmark successfully exposes causal credit assignment challenges and provides insights beyond traditional win rate metrics.
AI.conclusion: MTG-Causal-RL provides a strategically rich, partially observed domain with explicit causal interface and statistical protocol, offering causal-RL, world-model, and LLM-agent research a shared testbed for questions current benchmarks cannot address together, particularly regarding causal credit assignment under masked action spaces, structural transfer across archetypes, and SCM-grounded policy auditability.

authors: Telmo de Menezes e Silva Filho
title: Mind the Gap? A Distributional Comparison of Real and Synthetic Priors for Tabular Foundation Models
summary: Tabular foundation models are pre-trained on one of three classes of corpus: curated datasets drawn from benchmark repositories, tables harvested at scale from the web, or synthetic tables sampled from a parametric generative prior. Despite the centrality of pre-training data to model performance, little is known about how these corpora relate to one another in distribution, and the impact this has on downstream performance. In this work we take three canonical, archetypal datasets used to train tabular foundation models; the T4 dataset represents web-scraped corpora, the TabFM dataset curated tables from Kaggle, and the TabICL dataset as the only well-used synthetic prior with publicly available parameters. We characterise each corpus using aggregate features over whole tables, columns and correlations, and compare them using discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage metrics. We find that the TabICL synthetic prior occupies a narrow region of the space of real tables, that this mismatch cannot be closed by optimising prior hyper-parameters across more than 86 thousand configurations, and that curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable on a distributional level in feature space. Surprisingly, the distributional gap between synthetic pre-training data and real tables has a clearly detectable effect on performance under neither feature-based proximity measures or TabICL's own internal representations, suggesting that coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL's generalisation.

AI.tldr: Tabular foundation models pre-trained on synthetic data (TabICL) show distributional mismatch with real tables, but surprisingly this doesn't significantly impact downstream performance, suggesting distribution coverage isn't the main driver of generalization.
AI.motivation: Despite the centrality of pre-training data for tabular foundation models, little is known about how different corpora (curated datasets, web-scraped tables, synthetic tables) relate to each other distributionally and how this affects downstream performance.
AI.method: The authors analyze three archetypal datasets: T4 (web-scraped), TabFM (Kaggle curated), and TabICL (synthetic). They characterize each corpus using aggregate features over tables, columns, and correlations, comparing them with discriminator AUCs and k-NN coverage metrics. They also test over 86,000 hyperparameter configurations for TabICL.
AI.result: TabICL synthetic prior occupies a narrow region of real table space, and this mismatch cannot be closed by hyperparameter optimization. Curated and web-scraped corpora are broadly interchangeable distributionally. Surprisingly, the distributional gap between synthetic and real data doesn't clearly affect performance under feature-based measures or TabICL's own representations.
AI.conclusion: Coverage of the real-data distribution is not the primary driver of TabICL's generalization capabilities, challenging conventional assumptions about the importance of distributional alignment for tabular foundation model performance.

title: Normalized Architectures are Natively 4-Bit
summary: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is critical for efficiency. We show that nGPT, an architecture that constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, is inherently more robust to low-precision arithmetic. This removes the need for interventions-such as applying random Hadamard transforms and performing per-tensor scaling calculations-to preserve model quality, and it enables stable end-to-end NVFP4 training. We validate this approach on both a 1.2B dense model and hybrid (Mamba-Transformer) MoE models of up to 3B/30B parameters. We trace this robustness to the dot product: while quantization noise remains largely uncorrelated in both standard and normalized architectures, the signal behaves differently. In nGPT, the hypersphere constraint enhances weak positive correlations among the element-wise products, leading to a constructive accumulation of the signal across the hidden dimension while the noise continues to average out. This yields a higher effective signal-to-noise ratio and a flatter loss landscape, with the effect strengthening as the hidden dimension grows, suggesting increasing advantages at scale. A reference implementation is available at https://github.com/anonymous452026/ngpt-nvfp4

AI.tldr: Normalized GPT (nGPT) architectures with hypersphere-constrained weights and hidden representations enable stable 4-bit precision training without special interventions, showing better signal-to-noise ratio and scaling advantages.
AI.motivation: Training large language models at 4-bit precision is crucial for computational efficiency, but standard models require complex interventions to preserve quality during low-precision training.
AI.method: nGPT constrains weights and hidden representations to the unit hypersphere, which naturally makes the architecture more robust to low-precision arithmetic without needing random Hadamard transforms or per-tensor scaling calculations.
AI.result: The approach enables stable end-to-end NVFP4 training, validated on 1.2B dense models and hybrid MoE models up to 3B/30B parameters. The hypersphere constraint enhances weak positive correlations in dot products, leading to constructive signal accumulation while noise averages out.
AI.conclusion: nGPT provides inherent robustness to 4-bit quantization with higher effective signal-to-noise ratio and flatter loss landscape, offering scaling advantages for large language models without requiring complex training interventions.

title: More Than Can Be Said: A Benchmark and Framework for Pre-Question Scientific Ideation
comment: 19 pages, 2 figures; Code is available at https://github.com/Paradoxtcal/InciteResearch.git
summary: AI research agents have shown strong potential in automating literature search and manuscript refinement, yet most assume a clear and actionable initial input, operating only after a research question has been made explicit. In contrast, human research often begins with tacit friction, a sense of misalignment before a question can be formed. We introduce InciteResearch, a multi-agent framework designed to make a researcher's implicit understanding explicit, inspectable, and actionable. InciteResearch decomposes the logical chain of Socratic questioning and distributes it across the entire pipeline that: (1) Elicits a structured five-dimensional researcher profile state anchored by specific friction points from vague, even domain-unrelated inputs; (2) Violates hidden assumptions by maximizing the feasibility-novelty product with enforcing a 7-stage causal derivation trace; and (3) check whether the proposed method is a Necessary consequence of the reframed insight. We further introduce TF-Bench, the first benchmark for tacit-to-explicit research assistance that distinguishes domain-related from domain-unrelated inspirations across four scientific modes. On TF-Bench, InciteResearch achieves leapfrogging gains over a prompt-based baseline (novelty/impact from 3.671/3.806 to 4.250/4.397), shifting generated proposals from recombination to architectural insight. Our work demonstrates that AI can serve as an extension of thinking itself, rather than merely automating downstream execution.

AI.tldr: InciteResearch is a multi-agent AI framework that helps researchers move from implicit, vague research frictions to explicit, actionable questions by decomposing Socratic questioning across a pipeline that elicits researcher profiles, violates hidden assumptions, and validates derived insights.
AI.motivation: Current AI research agents require clear initial inputs and explicit research questions, but human research often begins with tacit frictions and vague misalignments before questions can be formed. There's a need for systems that can help make implicit understanding explicit and actionable from the very beginning of the research process.
AI.method: InciteResearch is a multi-agent framework that decomposes Socratic questioning across three main components: 1) Elicits a structured five-dimensional researcher profile state anchored by specific friction points from vague inputs, 2) Violates hidden assumptions by maximizing the feasibility-novelty product with a 7-stage causal derivation trace, and 3) Checks whether proposed methods are necessary consequences of reframed insights. The authors also introduce TF-Bench, the first benchmark for tacit-to-explicit research assistance.
AI.result: On the TF-Bench benchmark, InciteResearch achieves significant improvements over prompt-based baselines, increasing novelty scores from 3.671 to 4.250 and impact scores from 3.806 to 4.397. The system shifts generated research proposals from mere recombination to architectural insight, demonstrating leapfrogging gains.
AI.conclusion: The work demonstrates that AI can serve as an extension of thinking itself rather than merely automating downstream execution, bridging the gap between tacit research frictions and explicit, actionable research questions through structured questioning and assumption violation.

title: PRISM: Iterative Cross-Modal Posterior Refinement for Dynamic Text-Attributed Graphs
summary: Dynamic text-attributed graphs (DyTAGs) provide a powerful framework for modeling evolving systems in which node semantics and time-dependent interactions are tightly coupled. Recently, multimodal learning has emerged as a promising yet underexplored direction for enhancing DyTAG representation learning. However, existing methods typically rely on rigid modality partitions and one-shot fusion strategies, which limit their ability to capture the intrinsic and evolving dependencies between node semantics and interaction behaviors. To address these limitations, we propose \textbf{PRISM}, an iterative cross-modal posterior refinement framework for DyTAG representation learning. PRISM organizes DyTAG information into semantic and behavioral modalities, providing a more intrinsic alternative to carrier-level modality partitions. Instead of fusing the two modalities in a single step, PRISM learns a refinement trajectory that progressively transforms semantic priors into behavior-conditioned posterior states through cross-modal interaction with behavioral evidence. Extensive experiments on DTGB benchmark datasets show that PRISM achieves strong performance on temporal link prediction and destination node retrieval tasks. Further ablation studies validate the effectiveness of semantic--behavioral modeling and iterative posterior refinement.

AI.tldr: PRISM proposes an iterative cross-modal posterior refinement framework for dynamic text-attributed graphs, achieving state-of-the-art performance on temporal link prediction and destination node retrieval tasks through progressive refinement of semantic priors with behavioral evidence.
AI.motivation: Existing methods for DyTAG representation learning rely on rigid modality partitions and one-shot fusion strategies, which fail to capture the intrinsic and evolving dependencies between node semantics and time-dependent interaction behaviors. This limitation prevents effective modeling of evolving systems where node semantics and interactions are tightly coupled.
AI.method: PRISM organizes DyTAG information into semantic and behavioral modalities (instead of carrier-level partitions). It uses an iterative cross-modal posterior refinement framework that learns a refinement trajectory, progressively transforming semantic priors into behavior-conditioned posterior states through cross-modal interaction with behavioral evidence. This enables progressive integration rather than one-shot fusion.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on DTGB benchmark datasets show that PRISM achieves strong performance on temporal link prediction and destination node retrieval tasks. Ablation studies validate the effectiveness of both the semantic-behavioral modeling approach and the iterative posterior refinement mechanism.
AI.conclusion: PRISM provides a novel framework for DyTAG representation learning that captures intrinsic dependencies between semantics and behaviors through iterative refinement. The approach outperforms existing methods by moving beyond rigid modality partitions and one-shot fusion, offering better modeling of evolving systems with tightly coupled node semantics and temporal interactions.

title: Prediction and Empowerment: A Theory of Agency through Bridge Interfaces
comment: This is a working draft: feedback and criticism is most welcome
summary: We study agency under partial observability in deterministic physical or simulated worlds, where apparent randomness arises from uncertainty over initial conditions, fixed law bits, and unrolled exogenous noise. We model sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces split between agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel state, inducing a deterministic POMDP through a prior over latent microstates and many-to-one observation coarsening. Within this framework, we prove a separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment. Perfect prediction can be achieved either by identifying the hidden quotient relevant to the target family or by overwrite control that makes the future target action-determined; high empowerment alone is insufficient. Under refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, action-conditioned observation-compression progress reduces posterior uncertainty about the latent quotient, and when refinement requires steering world-side channel conditions, this creates target-conditioned interface empowerment. A bit-string specialization with a conserved information budget makes the resulting tradeoff explicit: prediction by identification requires internal capacity at least the relevant latent entropy, whereas overwrite control requires terminal action capacity over the controlled quotient. For modern AI agents, the results suggest a design principle rather than a theorem of inevitability: objectives should distinguish hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and mere overwrite or distractor control. Human--AI alignment is partly an interface-design problem, where the relevant bridge is between human intent, agent internal state, external tools, and world-side channel conditions. This is a working draft: feedback and criticism is most welcome.

AI.tldr: Paper studies agency in deterministic but partially observable worlds, proving separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment, with implications for AI agent design.
AI.motivation: To understand agency under partial observability in deterministic physical or simulated worlds where apparent randomness arises from uncertainty over initial conditions, fixed bits, and exogenous noise. The goal is to formalize the relationship between prediction, compression, and empowerment in such environments.
AI.method: Models sensing and actuation as bridge interfaces split between agent-controlled parameters and environment-controlled channel state, inducing a deterministic POMDP through priors over latent microstates and many-to-one observation coarsening. Uses theoretical analysis within this framework, with a bit-string specialization to make tradeoffs explicit.
AI.result: Proves separation between prediction, compression, and empowerment: perfect prediction requires either identifying hidden quotient relevant to target family or overwrite control that makes future target action-determined; high empowerment alone is insufficient. Shows that under refinable interfaces and sufficient memory, action-conditioned observation-compression reduces posterior uncertainty about latent quotient, creating target-conditioned interface empowerment.
AI.conclusion: For modern AI agents, suggests design principle: objectives should distinguish hidden-state identification, interface refinement, task-relevant controllability, and mere overwrite/distractor control. Human-AI alignment is partly an interface-design problem involving bridge between human intent, agent internal state, external tools, and world-side channel conditions.

title: Understanding diffusion models requires rethinking (again) generalization
summary: This position paper argues that understanding generalization in diffusion models requires fundamentally new theoretical frameworks that go beyond both classical statistical learning theory and the benign overfitting paradigm developed for supervised learning. In diffusion models, unlike in supervised learning, memorization of training data and generalization to novel samples are incompatible: a model that has fully memorized its training set generates copies rather than novel data. Several theoretical explanations for why practical diffusion models nevertheless generalize have been proposed, based on capacity limitations, implicit regularization from optimization, or architectural inductive biases, but their interactions remain unclear. We argue that the field should pivot from explaining why the diffusion models do not memorize to investigating what the model actually learns during pre-memorization phase. To highlight our stance, we conduct empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10, and we distill the findings into concrete open questions that we believe are key to improve understanding of generalization in diffusion models.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that classical learning theory can't explain generalization in diffusion models, proposes studying what models actually learn before memorization, and presents empirical findings from CIFAR-10 experiments.
AI.motivation: Current theoretical frameworks (classical statistical learning theory and benign overfitting) fail to explain generalization in diffusion models, where memorization and generalization are fundamentally incompatible - a model that fully memorizes training data generates copies rather than novel samples.
AI.method: The paper takes a position paper approach with theoretical arguments and conducts an empirical study of diffusion models trained on CIFAR-10. They analyze what models learn during the "pre-memorization phase" rather than explaining why they don't memorize.
AI.result: The empirical study yields findings that are distilled into concrete open questions. The paper doesn't present specific quantitative results but rather establishes a research direction and framework for understanding generalization in diffusion models.
AI.conclusion: The field needs new theoretical frameworks specifically for diffusion models, shifting focus from explaining non-memorization to understanding what models actually learn before memorization occurs. The open questions distilled from empirical study provide a roadmap for future research.

title: Fast Gauss-Newton for Multiclass Cross-Entropy
summary: In multiclass softmax cross-entropy, the full generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) curvature couples all output logits through the softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products harder to scale as the number of classes grows. We show that the standard multiclass GGN can be decomposed exactly into a true-vs-rest term and a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance term. Fast Gauss-Newton (FGN) retains the first term and drops the second, yielding a positive semidefinite under-approximation of the multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. The derivation uses an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy: the loss and gradient are unchanged, and the approximation enters only at the curvature level. Exploiting the FGN curvature structure, the damped update can be written as an equivalent whitened row-space system with one row per mini-batch example. We solve this system matrix-free by conjugate gradient using Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products of the scalar margin map. Targeted mechanism experiments and an evaluation on a fixed-feature multiclass head support the predictions from the decomposition: FGN stays closest to the full softmax GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.

AI.tldr: FGN (Fast Gauss-Newton) is a scalable approximation of the full multiclass softmax GGN curvature that drops the computationally expensive within-competitor covariance term, maintaining exactness for binary classification and providing a positive semidefinite under-approximation.
AI.motivation: The full generalized Gauss-Newton (GGN) curvature in multiclass softmax cross-entropy couples all output logits through softmax covariance, making curvature-vector products computationally expensive and hard to scale as the number of classes grows.
AI.method: The authors show that the standard multiclass GGN can be decomposed into a true-vs-rest term and a positive semidefinite within-competitor covariance term. FGN retains only the first term and drops the second, using an exact true-vs-rest scalar-margin representation of softmax cross-entropy. They solve the damped update matrix-free via conjugate gradient using Jacobian-vector and vector-Jacobian products of the scalar margin map.
AI.result: FGN provides a positive semidefinite under-approximation of multiclass GGN that is exact for binary classification. Experiments show FGN stays closest to full softmax GGN when competitor mass is concentrated or damping is large, and deviates as the dropped within-competitor covariance grows.
AI.conclusion: FGN offers a computationally efficient alternative to full GGN curvature for multiclass classification that maintains mathematical properties while improving scalability, with predictable behavior based on the characteristics of the dropped covariance term.

title: Debiased Multimodal Personality Understanding through Dual Causal Intervention
summary: Multimodalpersonalityunderstandingplaysacriticalroleinhuman centered artificial intelligence. Previous work mainly focus on learn-ing rich multimodal representations for video personality under standing. However, they often suffer from potential harm caused by subject bias (e.g., observable age and unobservable mental states), as subjects originate from diverse demographic backgrounds. Learn ing such spurious associations between multimodal features and traits may lead to unfair personality understanding. In this work, weconstruct aStructural Causal Model (SCM)toanalyze theimpact of these biases from a causal perspective, and propose a novel Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) to mitigate the interference of subject attributes on personality understanding. Specifically, we design a Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) module to block spurious correlations from observable demographic factors via a prototype-based confounder dictionary, and subsequently ap ply a Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) module to ad dress latent and unobservable biases throughalearnedmediatordic tionary intervention, thereby achieving causal disentanglement of representations for deconfounded reasoning. Importantly, we con struct a Demographic-annotated Multimodal Student Personality (DMSP) dataset to support the analysis and discussion of fairness related factors. Extensive experiments on the benchmark dataset CFI-V2 and our DMSPdataset demonstrate that DCAN consistently improves prediction accuracy, reaching 92.11% and 92.90%, respec tively. Meanwhile, the improvementsinthefairnessmetricsofequal opportunity and demographic parity are 6.57% and 7.97% on CFI-V2, and 15.38% and 20.06% on the DMSP dataset. Our code and DMSP dataset are available at https://github.com/Sabrina-han/DCAN

AI.tldr: DCAN uses dual causal adjustment (back-door and front-door) to debias multimodal personality understanding by addressing subject demographic biases and latent attributes through causal disentanglement.
AI.motivation: Multimodal personality understanding suffers from subject bias (demographic factors like age and mental states) that leads to unfair predictions due to spurious correlations between multimodal features and personality traits.
AI.method: Proposes Dual Causal Adjustment Network (DCAN) with: 1) Back-door Adjustment Causal Learning (BACL) using prototype-based confounder dictionary to block observable demographic biases, and 2) Front-door Adjustment Causal Learning (FACL) using learned mediator dictionary to address latent/unobservable biases for causal disentanglement.
AI.result: Achieves 92.11% accuracy on CFI-V2 and 92.90% on DMSP dataset. Fairness improvements: 6.57% equal opportunity and 7.97% demographic parity on CFI-V2; 15.38% equal opportunity and 20.06% demographic parity on DMSP dataset.
AI.conclusion: DCAN effectively mitigates subject bias in multimodal personality understanding through causal perspective, improving both accuracy and fairness metrics, with novel DMSP dataset supporting fairness analysis.

title: Beyond Autoregressive RTG: Conditioning via Injection Outside Sequential Modeling in Decision Transformer
