1. Identify failure in two-hotel simulator with partial observability (Hotel A can't see competitor's inventory/rule)
2. Diagnose using trace-level protocol (RevPAR, occupancy, ADR, price distributions, L1/JS distances, confidence intervals)
3. Repair with Trace-Prior RL: learn distributional market prior from lagged traces, then train stochastic policy with RevPAR reward + KL penalty to learned prior
AI.result: Trace-Prior RL policies match Hotel B's RevPAR, occupancy, ADR, and price distribution within seed-level uncertainty while still optimizing Hotel A's own reward. Higher action accuracy worsens trace alignment when target is distributional.
AI.conclusion: Main contribution is reproducible failure-and-repair recipe for agentic systems where scalar rewards are easy to game and intended behavior only visible in traces. Not a new optimizer or hotel-pricing leaderboard, but a diagnostic/repair framework for distributional alignment.

title: Bandit Learning in General Open Multi-agent Systems
summary: Recent developments in digital platforms have highlighted the prevalence of open systems, where agents can arrive and depart over time. While bandit learning in open systems has recently received initial attention, existing work imposes structural assumptions that are frequently violated in practice. A learning paradigm for general open systems creates fresh challenges: newly arriving agents induce endogenous non-stationarity; agent patterns determine how quickly information accumulates; and new agents make regret scale further with the time horizon. To this end, we formulate a unified open-system bandit problem with general dynamics, including heterogeneous rewards and general agent patterns. We introduce new concepts to capture the inherent complexities: the \emph{pre-training degree} of new agents quantifies how much information an agent carries upon entry, \emph{stability} measures the impact of new agents on the system, and \emph{global dynamic regret} compares the cumulative expected reward of all active agents with that of the varying optimal arms. We develop certified global-UCB learning methodologies with provable guarantees. Our regret bounds reveal that entry uncertainty enters linearly via the pre-training degree, while in stable regimes, regret is governed by the time needed to identify a persistent optimal arm, as well as by the agent patterns. We further show that these dependencies are tight via lower bounds in hard instances.

AI.tldr: A new bandit learning framework for open systems where agents dynamically arrive/depart, addressing challenges like endogenous non-stationarity, heterogeneous rewards, and general agent patterns with novel concepts like pre-training degree and stability.
AI.motivation: Existing bandit learning in open systems imposes structural assumptions frequently violated in practice. Need for general framework that handles realistic challenges: endogenous non-stationarity from new agents, how agent patterns affect information accumulation, and regret scaling with time horizon.
AI.method: Formulates unified open-system bandit problem with general dynamics. Introduces new concepts: pre-training degree (information new agents carry upon entry), stability (impact of new agents on system), and global dynamic regret. Develops certified global-UCB learning methodologies with provable guarantees.
AI.result: Regret bounds show entry uncertainty enters linearly via pre-training degree. In stable regimes, regret governed by time needed to identify persistent optimal arm plus agent patterns. Proves these dependencies are tight via lower bounds in hard instances.
AI.conclusion: Provides first comprehensive framework for bandit learning in general open systems, addressing key challenges of dynamic agent populations with theoretical guarantees and tight bounds.

title: SpatialEpiBench: Benchmarking Spatial Information and Epidemic Priors in Forecasting
summary: Accurate epidemic forecasting is crucial for public health response, resource allocation, and outbreak intervention, but remains difficult with sparse, noisy, and highly non-stationary data. Because epidemics unfold across interacting regions, spatiotemporal methods are natural candidates for improving forecasts. Despite growing interest in spatial information, no standardized benchmark exists, and current evaluations often use simple chronological train-test splits that do not reflect real-time forecasting practice. We address this gap with SpatialEpiBench, a challenging benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting in realistic public-health settings. SpatialEpiBench includes 11 epidemic datasets with standardized rolling evaluations and outbreak-specific metrics. We evaluate adjacency-informed forecasting models with widely used epidemic priors that adapt general models to epidemiology, but find that most methods underperform a simple last-value baseline from 1 day to 1 month ahead, even during outbreaks and with these priors. We identify three major failure modes: (1) poor outbreak anticipation, (2) difficulty handling sparsity and noise, and (3) limited utility of common geographic adjacency for epidemiological spatial information. We release benchmark data, code, and instructions at https://github.com/Rachel-Lyu/SpatialEpiBench to support development of operationally useful epidemic forecasting models.

AI.tldr: SpatialEpiBench is a standardized benchmark for spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting with realistic evaluations, revealing that most methods underperform simple baselines despite epidemic priors.
AI.motivation: Epidemic forecasting is crucial but challenging due to sparse, noisy, non-stationary data. While spatiotemporal methods are natural for epidemics, there's no standardized benchmark, and current evaluations don't reflect real-time forecasting practice.
AI.method: Created SpatialEpiBench - a challenging benchmark with 11 epidemic datasets, standardized rolling evaluations, and outbreak-specific metrics. Evaluated adjacency-informed forecasting models with epidemic priors against a simple last-value baseline.
AI.result: Most methods underperformed the simple last-value baseline from 1 day to 1 month ahead, even during outbreaks and with epidemic priors. Identified three failure modes: poor outbreak anticipation, difficulty with sparsity/noise, and limited utility of common geographic adjacency for epidemiology.
AI.conclusion: Current spatiotemporal epidemic forecasting methods are inadequate in realistic settings. Geographic adjacency alone may not capture epidemiological spatial relationships. The released benchmark aims to support development of operationally useful models.

title: Federation of Experts: Communication Efficient Distributed Inference for Large Language Models
summary:
Mixture of experts has emerged as the primary mechanism for making Large Language Models (LLMs) computationally efficient. However, in distributed settings, communicating token embeddings between experts is a significant bottleneck.
  We present the novel Federation of Experts (FoE) architecture. FoE restructures the MoE block of a transformer layer into multiple MoE clusters. Each cluster is responsible for only one of the KV heads and expert parallelism is applied between those experts. Between clusters, a sum synchronizes the post-attention residuals, which then drives routing and dispatch for the next MoE block. In a single-node setting, FoE completely eliminates all-to-all communication as all experts within a group are contained on the same GPU. In multi-node settings, FoE confines all-to-all communication to the intra-node fabric, thus significantly reducing communication overhead.
  An implementation of FoE finds that on LongBench, FoE significantly improves inference throughput and latency in both single-node and multi-node settings, reducing the end-to-end forward-pass latency by up to 5.2x, TTFT by 3.62x, and TBT by 1.95x. It does so while achieving comparable generation quality to a mixture of experts model of the same size and training configuration.

AI.tldr: A novel Federation of Experts (FoE) architecture that restructures MoE transformers into multiple clusters to eliminate inter-GPU communication bottlenecks, achieving 5.2x latency reduction while maintaining comparable generation quality.
AI.motivation: Communication of token embeddings between experts in distributed Mixture of Experts (MoE) settings creates significant bottlenecks, limiting computational efficiency in Large Language Models.
AI.method: FoE restructures the MoE block into multiple clusters, each responsible for one KV head with expert parallelism between them. A sum synchronizes post-attention residuals between clusters, driving routing for the next MoE block, eliminating or confining all-to-all communication.
AI.result: FoE reduces end-to-end forward-pass latency by up to 5.2x, TTFT by 3.62x, and TBT by 1.95x on LongBench while achieving comparable generation quality to baseline MoE models.
AI.conclusion: The Federation of Experts architecture successfully addresses communication bottlenecks in distributed MoE systems, significantly improving inference performance in both single-node and multi-node settings without sacrificing model quality.

title: Ex Ante Evaluation of AI-Induced Idea Diversity Collapse
summary: Creative AI systems are typically evaluated at the level of individual utility, yet creative outputs are consumed in populations: an idea loses value when many others produce similar ones. This creates an evaluation blind spot, as AI can improve individual outputs while increasing population-level crowding. We introduce a human-relative framework for benchmarking AI-induced human diversity collapse without requiring human-AI interaction data, providing an ex ante protocol to estimate crowding risk from model-only generations and matched unaided human baselines. By modeling ideas as congestible resources, we show that source-level crowding is identifiable from within-distribution comparisons, yielding an excess-crowding coefficient $Δ$ and a human-relative diversity ratio $ρ$. We show that $ρ\ge1$ is the no-excess-crowding parity condition and connect $Δ$ to an adoption game with exposure-dependent redundancy costs. Across short stories, marketing slogans, and alternative-uses tasks, three frontier LLMs fall below parity across crowding kernels. Estimates stabilize with feasible model-only sample sizes. Importantly, generation-protocol variants show that crowding can be reduced through targeted design, making diversity collapse an actionable, development-time evaluation target for population-aware creative AI.

AI.tldr: AI creativity systems can improve individual outputs but reduce population-level diversity by causing idea crowding, measured via human-relative metrics without needing human-AI interaction data.
AI.motivation: Current creative AI evaluation focuses on individual utility, ignoring population-level effects where AI-generated outputs can cause diversity collapse through idea crowding when many similar ideas are produced.
AI.method: Introduce human-relative framework using congestion modeling to benchmark AI-induced diversity collapse, developing excess-crowding coefficient Δ and human-relative diversity ratio ρ from within-distribution comparisons of model-only generations and unaided human baselines.
AI.result: Three frontier LLMs fall below the no-excess-crowding parity condition (ρ≥1) across short stories, marketing slogans, and alternative-uses tasks, showing crowding can be reduced through targeted generation-protocol design.
AI.conclusion: AI-induced diversity collapse is measurable and actionable, enabling development-time evaluation for population-aware creative AI systems through targeted design interventions to reduce crowding.

title: Contrastive Identification and Generation in the Limit
summary: In the classical identification in the limit model of Gold [1967], a stream of positive examples is presented round by round, and the learner must eventually recover the target hypothesis. Recently, Kleinberg and Mullainathan [2024] introduced generation in the limit, where the learner instead must eventually output novel elements of the target's support. Both lines of work focus on positive-only or fully labeled data. Yet many natural supervision signals are inherently relational rather than singleton, which encode relationships between examples rather than labels of individual ones. We initiate the study of contrastive identification and generation in the limit, where the learner observes a contrastive presentation of data: a stream of unordered pairs $\{x,y\}$ satisfying $h(x)\ne h(y)$ for an unknown target binary hypothesis $h$, but which element is positive is hidden from the learner. We first present three results in the noiseless setting: an exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes (a one-line geometric refinement of Angluin [1980]'s tell-tale condition), a combinatorial dimension called contrastive closure dimension (a contrasitive analogue of the closure dimension in Raman et al. [2025]) and exactly characterizing uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity, and a strict hierarchy in which contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. We then prove a sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption: there exist classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by a single budget-independent algorithm, yet not identifiable from positive examples under even one corrupted observation. The unifying technical object is the common crossing graph, which encodes pairwise ambiguity, family-level generation obstructions, and corruption defects in a single coverage-and-incidence language.

AI.tldr: Introduces contrastive identification and generation in the limit learning model, studying relational supervision via unordered contrastive pairs where only inequality is known, not which element is positive. Provides characterizations in noiseless setting and shows surprising robustness to corruption compared to positive-only learning.
AI.motivation: Existing identification and generation in the limit models focus on positive-only or fully labeled data, but many natural supervision signals are relational rather than singleton. Contrastive pairs (showing two elements are different but not which is positive) provide a more realistic learning scenario that captures pairwise relationships rather than individual labels.
AI.method: Introduces contrastive identification and generation in the limit model, where learner observes unordered pairs {x,y} satisfying h(x)≠h(y) for unknown binary hypothesis h. Uses common crossing graph to encode pairwise ambiguity, family-level generation obstructions, and corruption defects. Develops combinatorial dimensions (contrastive closure dimension) and proves characterizations via geometric refinements of tell-tale conditions.
AI.result: 1) Exact characterization of contrastive identifiable classes via geometric refinement of Angluin's tell-tale condition. 2) Contrastive closure dimension that exactly characterizes uniform contrastive generation with tight sample complexity. 3) Strict hierarchy showing contrastive generation and text identification are mutually incomparable. 4) Sharp reversal under finite adversarial corruption: classes identifiable from contrastive pairs under any finite corruption budget by single algorithm, but not identifiable from positive examples under even one corrupted observation.
AI.conclusion: Contrastive learning in the limit offers a theoretically rich framework for relational supervision that differs fundamentally from positive-only learning. The model shows surprising robustness to corruption and introduces new combinatorial dimensions. The common crossing graph provides a unifying technical framework for analyzing pairwise ambiguity and corruption effects in contrastive learning.

title: Improved techniques for fine-tuning flow models via adjoint matching: a deterministic control pipeline
summary: We propose a deterministic adjoint matching framework that formulates human preference alignment for flow-based generative models as an optimal control problem over velocity fields. One can directly regress the control toward a value-gradient-induced target under the current policy, leading to a simple and stable training objective. Building on this perspective, we introduce a truncated adjoint scheme that focuses computation on the terminal portion of the trajectory, where reward-relevant signals concentrate, which yields substantial computational savings while preserving alignment quality. We further generalize the framework beyond standard KL-based regularization, allowing more flexible trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation. Experiments on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B demonstrate consistent gains across multiple alignment metrics, along with substantially improved diversity and mode preservation.

AI.tldr: A deterministic adjoint matching framework for human preference alignment in flow-based generative models, formulated as an optimal control problem with truncated adjoint computation for efficiency and flexible regularization beyond KL divergence.
AI.motivation: Existing human preference alignment methods for flow-based generative models need improvement in computational efficiency and flexibility. Current approaches may be computationally expensive and limited to KL-based regularization, restricting trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation.
AI.method: Proposes a deterministic adjoint matching framework that formulates preference alignment as an optimal control problem over velocity fields. Uses direct regression of control toward value-gradient-induced targets. Introduces truncated adjoint scheme focusing computation on terminal trajectory portions where reward signals concentrate. Generalizes beyond KL-based regularization for more flexible trade-offs.
AI.result: Experiments on SiT-XL/2 and FLUX.2-Klein-4B show consistent gains across multiple alignment metrics. Achieves substantial computational savings while preserving alignment quality. Demonstrates improved diversity and mode preservation compared to baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: The proposed deterministic adjoint matching framework provides an effective, computationally efficient approach to human preference alignment for flow-based generative models, offering better trade-offs between alignment strength and distributional preservation with improved diversity and mode coverage.

title: Playing the network backward: A Game Theoretic Attribution Framework
summary: Attribution methods explain which input features drive a model's prediction, making them central to model debugging and mechanistic interpretability. Yet backward attribution methods, including gradients, LRP, and transformer-specific rules, lack a shared framework in which to compare the underlying backward calculations. We introduce such a framework by recasting backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on Gaubert and Vlassopoulos' ReLU Net Game. Gradients and the full alpha-beta-LRP family arise as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria, so attribution maps become projections of trajectory distributions rather than the primary object. Desired explanation properties, such as localisation focus, robustness to input noise, or stable attention routing, can be specified as game-theoretic concepts, including policy regularization, risk aversion, and extended action sets, and translate directly into novel adaptations of the well-known backward rules. On ViT-B/16, one such selected adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific backward methods across all considered localisation metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a game-theoretic framework for backward attribution methods, showing that gradients and LRP rules emerge as integrals over game trajectories, enabling novel adaptations with improved performance on vision transformers.
AI.motivation: Backward attribution methods (gradients, LRP, transformer-specific rules) lack a unified framework for comparison, making it difficult to systematically analyze and improve attribution techniques for model interpretability.
AI.method: The authors recast backward attribution as a two-player game on an extended network graph, building on the ReLU Net Game. Gradients and alpha-beta-LRP family are shown to emerge as integrals over game trajectories under specific equilibria.
AI.result: Desired explanation properties (localization focus, robustness, stable attention routing) translate to game-theoretic concepts, enabling novel adaptations of backward rules. One adaptation of alpha-beta-LRP outperforms prior transformer-specific methods on ViT-B/16 across localization metrics.
AI.conclusion: The game-theoretic framework provides a unifying foundation for backward attribution methods, enabling systematic derivation of novel attribution techniques with improved performance on transformer architectures.

title: NeuroAgent: LLM Agents for Multimodal Neuroimaging Analysis and Research
summary: Multimodal neuroimaging analysis often involves complex, modality-specific preprocessing workflows that require careful configuration, quality control, and coordination across heterogeneous toolchains. Beyond preprocessing, downstream statistical analysis and disease classification commonly require task-specific code, evaluation protocols, and data-format conventions, creating additional barriers between raw acquisitions and reproducible scientific analysis. We present NeuroAgent, an LLM-driven agentic framework that automates key preprocessing and analysis steps for heterogeneous neuroimaging data, including sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, and PET, and supports interactive downstream analysis through natural-language queries. NeuroAgent employs a hierarchical multi-agent architecture with a feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine: agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect and recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity. We evaluate the system on 1,470 subjects pooled across all ADNI phases (CN=1,000, AD=470), where all subjects have sMRI and tabular data, with subsets also having Tau-PET (n=469), fMRI (n=278), and DTI ($n=620$). Pipeline ablation studies across multiple LLM backends show that capable models reach up to 100% intent-parsing accuracy, with the strongest backend (Qwen3.5-27B) reaching 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing step correctness. Automated recovery limits manual intervention to edge cases where human review is required via the Human-In-The-Loop interface. For Alzheimer's Disease classification using automatically preprocessed multimodal data, our agent ensemble achieves an AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines. These results show that NeuroAgent can reduce the manual effort required for neuroimaging preprocessing and enable end-to-end automated analysis pipelines for neuroimaging research.

AI.tldr: NeuroAgent is an LLM-driven agentic framework that automates multimodal neuroimaging preprocessing and analysis, reducing manual effort and enabling end-to-end automated pipelines for neuroimaging research.
AI.motivation: Multimodal neuroimaging analysis requires complex, modality-specific preprocessing workflows with careful configuration and quality control. Downstream analysis also needs task-specific code and conventions, creating barriers between raw data and reproducible scientific analysis.
AI.method: NeuroAgent uses an LLM-driven agentic framework with hierarchical multi-agent architecture and a feedback-driven Generate-Execute-Validate engine. Agents autonomously generate executable preprocessing code, detect/recover from runtime errors, and validate output integrity for sMRI, fMRI, dMRI, and PET data.
AI.result: Evaluation on 1,470 ADNI subjects showed up to 100% intent-parsing accuracy and 84.8% end-to-end preprocessing correctness with Qwen3.5-27B. For Alzheimer's Disease classification, the agent ensemble achieved AUC of 0.9518 with four modalities, outperforming all single-modality baselines.
AI.conclusion: NeuroAgent can significantly reduce manual effort for neuroimaging preprocessing and enable end-to-end automated analysis pipelines, demonstrating practical value for neuroimaging research through its LLM-driven agentic framework.

title: AffineLens: Capturing the Continuous Piecewise Affine Functions of Neural Networks
summary: Piecewise affine neural networks (PANNs) provide a principled geometric perspective on neural network expressivity by characterizing the input--output map as a continuous piecewise affine (CPA) function whose complexity is governed by the number, arrangement, and shapes of its affine regions. However, existing interpretability and expressivity analyses often rely on indirect proxies (e.g., activation statistics or theoretical upper bounds) and rarely offer practical, accurate tools for enumerating and visualizing the induced region partition under realistic architectures and bounded input domains. In this work, we present AffineLens, a unified framework for computing the hyperplane arrangements and polyhedral structures underlying PANNs. Given a calibrated (bounded) input polytope, AffineLens identifies the subset of neuron-induced hyperplanes that intersect the domain, enumerates the resulting affine sub-regions in a layer-wise manner, and returns provably non-empty maximal CPA regions together with interior representatives. The framework further provides visualizations of region partitioning and decision boundaries, enabling qualitative inspection alongside quantitative region counts. By exploiting the affine restriction property of CPA networks under fixed activation patterns, AffineLens supports a broad class of modern components, including batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, multilayer perceptrons, and convolutional layers. Finally, we use AffineLens to perform a systematic empirical study of architectural expressivity, comparing networks through region complexity metrics and revealing how design choices influence the geometry of learned functions.

AI.tldr: AffineLens is a framework that directly computes and visualizes the hyperplane arrangements and polyhedral regions in piecewise affine neural networks, enabling practical analysis of their expressivity beyond theoretical bounds.
AI.motivation: Existing analyses of piecewise affine neural networks rely on indirect proxies like activation statistics or theoretical upper bounds, lacking practical tools to actually compute and visualize the affine region partitions for realistic architectures on bounded input domains.
AI.method: AffineLens framework: given a bounded input polytope, identifies intersecting neuron-induced hyperplanes, enumerates affine sub-regions layer-wise, returns non-empty maximal regions with interior representatives, and provides visualization of region partitioning and decision boundaries.
AI.result: The framework supports modern neural network components (batch normalization, pooling, residual connections, MLPs, convolutional layers) and enables systematic empirical study of architectural expressivity through region complexity metrics.
AI.conclusion: AffineLens provides a unified, practical framework for directly computing and analyzing the geometric structure of piecewise affine neural networks, revealing how architectural choices influence the learned function's geometry and expressivity.

title: SkillOS: Learning Skill Curation for Self-Evolving Agents
summary: LLM-based agents are increasingly deployed to handle streaming tasks, yet they often remain one-off problem solvers that fail to learn from past interactions. Reusable skills distilled from experience provide a natural substrate for self-evolution, where high-quality skill curation serves as the key bottleneck. Existing approaches either rely on manual skill curation, prescribe heuristic skill operations, or train for short-horizon skill operations. However, they still struggle to learn complex long-term curation policies from indirect and delayed feedback. To tackle this challenge, we propose SkillOS, an experience-driven RL training recipe for learning skill curation in self-evolving agents. SkillOS pairs a frozen agent executor that retrieves and applies skills with a trainable skill curator that updates an external SkillRepo from accumulated experience. To provide learning signals for curation, we design composite rewards and train on grouped task streams based on skill-relevant task dependencies, where earlier trajectories update the SkillRepo, and later related tasks evaluate these updates. Across multi-turn agentic tasks and single-turn reasoning tasks, SkillOS consistently outperforms memory-free and strong memory-based baselines in both effectiveness and efficiency, with the learned skill curator generalizing across different executor backbones and task domains. Further analyses show that the learned curator produces more targeted skill use, while the skills in SkillRepo evolve into more richly structured Markdown files that encode higher-level meta-skills over time.

AI.tldr: SkillOS: An RL-based training framework for self-evolving LLM agents that learns to curate reusable skills from experience, enabling better long-term learning and generalization across tasks.
AI.motivation: Current LLM-based agents deployed for streaming tasks are typically one-off problem solvers that don't learn from past interactions. While reusable skills offer potential for self-evolution, existing approaches struggle with skill curation bottlenecks: they either rely on manual curation, use heuristic skill operations, or only train for short-horizon operations, failing to learn complex long-term curation policies from delayed feedback.
AI.method: SkillOS is an experience-driven RL training recipe with two components: 1) a frozen agent executor that retrieves and applies skills, and 2) a trainable skill curator that updates an external SkillRepo. Learning signals are provided through composite rewards and training on grouped task streams with skill-relevant task dependencies, where earlier trajectories update the SkillRepo and later related tasks evaluate these updates.
AI.result: SkillOS consistently outperforms memory-free and strong memory-based baselines across both multi-turn agentic tasks and single-turn reasoning tasks in effectiveness and efficiency. The learned skill curator generalizes across different executor backbones and task domains, producing more targeted skill use. Skills in SkillRepo evolve into richly structured Markdown files encoding higher-level meta-skills over time.
AI.conclusion: SkillOS successfully addresses the challenge of learning complex long-term curation policies for self-evolving agents, enabling effective skill distillation from experience with good generalization capabilities, representing a significant advancement in developing lifelong learning LLM agents.

title: Memory Inception: Latent-Space KV Cache Manipulation for Steering LLMs
summary: Steering large language models (LLMs) is usually done by either instruction prompting or activation steering. Prompting often gives strong control, but caches guidance tokens at every layer and can clutter long interactions; activation steering is compact but typically weaker and does not support large structured reminders. We introduce memory inception (MI), a training-free method that steers in latent attention space by inserting text-derived key-value (KV) banks only at selected layers. Rather than materializing reminder content throughout the prompt cache, MI treats steering as selective KV allocation, injecting latent slots only where the model routes to them. On matched personality-steering tasks, MI gives the best overall control--drift trade-off, remaining competitive with prompting while consistently outperforming CAA. On updateable guidance, MI supports mid-conversation behavior shifts without rewriting the visible transcript, achieving the highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3. On structured reasoning, MI outperforms visible prompting on HARDMath and PHYSICS (10/12 subject$\times$mode cells), serving as proxies for structured reasoning in verifiable domains, while cutting content-matched KV storage by up to 118$\times$. These results position MI as a powerful steering method when guidance is persistent, structured, or expensive to keep in the visible transcript.

AI.tldr: Memory Inception (MI) is a training-free method that steers LLMs by inserting text-derived key-value banks only at selected layers in latent attention space, achieving better control-drift trade-off than prompting or activation steering.
AI.motivation: Current LLM steering methods have limitations: instruction prompting gives strong control but clutters interactions with visible guidance tokens; activation steering is compact but weaker and doesn't support large structured reminders. There's a need for a method that combines the strengths of both approaches.
AI.method: MI treats steering as selective KV allocation in latent attention space, injecting key-value banks derived from text only at layers where the model routes to them, rather than materializing reminder content throughout the prompt cache. It's training-free and works by inserting text-derived KV banks at selected layers.
AI.result: MI achieves the best control-drift trade-off on personality-steering tasks, outperforming activation steering (CAA) while remaining competitive with prompting. It supports mid-conversation behavior shifts without transcript rewriting (highest post-shift alignment on Qwen3). On structured reasoning tasks (HARDMath, PHYSICS), it outperforms visible prompting in 10/12 subject×mode cells while reducing KV storage by up to 118×.
AI.conclusion: MI is a powerful steering method for scenarios where guidance needs to be persistent, structured, or too expensive to keep in the visible transcript, offering a superior alternative to both prompting and activation steering.

title: MASPO: Joint Prompt Optimization for LLM-based Multi-Agent Systems
summary: Large language model (LLM)-based Multi-agent systems (MAS) have shown promise in tackling complex collaborative tasks, where agents are typically orchestrated via role-specific prompts. While the quality of these prompts is pivotal, jointly optimizing them across interacting agents remains a non-trivial challenge, primarily due to the misalignment between local agent objectives and holistic system goals. To address this, we introduce MASPO, a novel framework designed to automatically and iteratively refine prompts across the entire system. A core innovation of MASPO is its joint evaluation mechanism, which assesses prompts not merely by their local validity, but by their capacity to facilitate downstream success for successor agents. This effectively bridges the gap between local interactions and global outcomes without relying on ground-truth labels. Furthermore, MASPO employs a data-driven evolutionary beam search to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional prompt space. Extensive empirical evaluations across 6 diverse tasks demonstrate that MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9. We release our code at https://github.com/wangzx1219/MASPO.

AI.tldr: MASPO: A framework for automatically optimizing prompts across multiple interacting LLM agents by jointly evaluating prompts based on downstream success rather than just local validity.
AI.motivation: In LLM-based multi-agent systems, prompt quality is crucial but challenging to optimize jointly across agents due to misalignment between local agent objectives and holistic system goals. Current methods lack effective joint optimization of prompts for interacting agents.
AI.method: MASPO introduces a joint evaluation mechanism that assesses prompts by their capacity to facilitate downstream success for successor agents, bridging local interactions and global outcomes. It uses data-driven evolutionary beam search to efficiently navigate the high-dimensional prompt space and automatically refines prompts iteratively across the entire system.
AI.result: Extensive evaluations across 6 diverse tasks show MASPO consistently outperforms state-of-the-art prompt optimization methods, achieving an average accuracy improvement of 2.9%.
AI.conclusion: MASPO effectively addresses the challenge of joint prompt optimization in multi-agent systems without ground-truth labels by aligning local interactions with global outcomes through downstream success evaluation.

title: Soft Deterministic Policy Gradient with Gaussian Smoothing
summary: Deterministic policy gradient (DPG) is widely utilized for continuous control; however, it inherently relies on the differentiability of the critic with respect to the action during policy updates. This assumption is violated in practical control problems involving sparse or discrete rewards, leading to ill-defined policy gradients and unstable learning. To address these challenges, we propose a principled alternative based on a smoothed Bellman equation formulated via Gaussian smoothing. Specifically, we define a novel action-value function based on a smoothed Bellman equation and derive the soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG). Our formulation eliminates explicit dependence on critic action-gradients and ensures that the gradient remains well-defined even for non-smooth Q-functions. We instantiate this framework into a deep reinforcement learning algorithm, which we call soft deep deterministic policy gradient (Soft DDPG). Empirical evaluations on standard continuous control benchmarks and their discretized-reward variants show that Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and provides clear gains in most discretized-reward environments, where standard DDPG is more sensitive to irregular critic landscapes.

AI.tldr: Soft DDPG: A deterministic policy gradient method using Gaussian smoothing of the Bellman equation to handle non-smooth Q-functions in sparse/discrete reward environments.
AI.motivation: DPG methods require differentiability of critic with respect to actions, which fails in practical control problems with sparse/discrete rewards, leading to ill-defined gradients and unstable learning.
AI.method: Proposes a smoothed Bellman equation via Gaussian smoothing, defines novel action-value function based on smoothed Bellman equation, derives soft deterministic policy gradient (Soft-DPG) that eliminates explicit dependence on critic action-gradients.
AI.result: Soft DDPG remains competitive in dense-reward settings and provides clear gains in most discretized-reward environments where standard DDPG fails due to sensitivity to irregular critic landscapes.
AI.conclusion: The Gaussian smoothing approach provides a principled alternative to standard DPG that handles non-smooth Q-functions effectively, making DPG methods more robust for practical control problems with sparse/discrete rewards.

title: Can RL Teach Long-Horizon Reasoning to LLMs? Expressiveness Is Key
summary: Reinforcement learning (RL) has been applied to improve large language model (LLM) reasoning, yet the systematic study of how training scales with task difficulty has been hampered by the lack of controlled, scalable environments. We introduce ScaleLogic, a synthetic logical reasoning framework that offers independent control over two axes of difficulty: the depth of the required proof planning (i.e., the horizon) and the expressiveness of the underlying logic. Our proposed framework supports a wide range of logics: from simple implication-only logic ("if-then") towards more expressive first-order reasoning with conjunction ("and"), disjunction ("or"), negation ("not"), and universal quantification ("for all"). Using this framework, we show that the RL training compute $T$ follows a power law with respect to reasoning depth $D$ ($T \propto D^γ$, $R^{2} > 0.99$), and that the scaling exponent $γ$ increases monotonically with logical expressiveness, from $1.04$ to $2.60$. On downstream mathematics and general reasoning benchmarks, more expressive training settings yield both larger performance gains (up to $+10.66$ points) and more compute-efficient transfer compared to less expressive settings, demonstrating that what a model is trained on, not just how much it is trained, shapes downstream transfer. We further show that the power-law relationship holds across multiple RL methods, and curriculum-based training substantially improves scaling efficiency.

AI.tldr: ScaleLogic is a synthetic logical reasoning framework for studying RL scaling with LLM reasoning, showing power-law compute scaling with reasoning depth that worsens with more expressive logic, but more expressive training yields better downstream transfer.
AI.motivation: There's a lack of controlled, scalable environments to systematically study how RL training scales with task difficulty for improving LLM reasoning. Current research is hampered by this limitation.
AI.method: Introduce ScaleLogic framework with independent control over two difficulty axes: proof planning depth (horizon) and logical expressiveness. Framework supports progressive logics from simple implication-only to full first-order reasoning with conjunction, disjunction, negation, and universal quantification.
AI.result: RL training compute follows power law with reasoning depth (T ∝ D^γ, R² > 0.99). Scaling exponent γ increases monotonically with logical expressiveness (1.04 to 2.60). More expressive training yields larger performance gains (+10.66 points) and more compute-efficient transfer on downstream math/reasoning benchmarks. Power-law holds across RL methods; curriculum training improves scaling efficiency.
AI.conclusion: What models are trained on (logical expressiveness) matters as much as how much they're trained for downstream transfer. More expressive logical training provides better computational efficiency and performance gains despite steeper scaling exponents.

title: Band Together: Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination against Evasion-based Promotion Attacks
summary: Multimodal recommender systems exploit visual and textual signals to alleviate data sparsity, but this also makes them more vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks. Existing defenses are largely limited to single-modal settings and mainly focus on poisoning-based threats, leaving evasion-based threats underexplored. In this work, we first identify a cross-modal gradient mismatch under the multi-user promotion setting, where visual and textual perturbations are optimized in inconsistent directions due to the dominance of distinct user groups. This phenomenon dilutes the attack effectiveness and leads robust training to underestimate worst-case risks. To address this issue, we propose Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC). UAT-MC tackles the challenge of unknown targeted items in evasion-based attacks (as opposed to poisoning-based attacks) by treating all items as potential targets, and introduces a gradient alignment mechanism to explicitly correct this mismatch. This design ensures synchronized perturbations across modalities, thereby maximizing adversarial strength for robust training. Extensive experiments demonstrate that UAT-MC significantly improves robustness against promotion attacks while maintaining acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off. Code is available at https://github.com/gmXian/UAT-MC.

AI.tldr: UAT-MC is a novel defense method for multimodal recommender systems that addresses evasion-based promotion attacks through gradient alignment across visual and textual modalities, improving robustness while maintaining reasonable recommendation accuracy.
AI.motivation: Multimodal recommender systems that use visual and textual signals are vulnerable to evasion-based promotion attacks, but existing defenses focus mainly on single-modal settings and poisoning-based threats, leaving evasion-based threats underexplored.
AI.method: The authors identify cross-modal gradient mismatch in multi-user promotion settings, then propose Untargeted Adversarial Training with Multimodal Coordination (UAT-MC). This method treats all items as potential targets (for unknown targeted items in evasion attacks) and introduces a gradient alignment mechanism to synchronize perturbations across modalities.
AI.result: Extensive experiments show UAT-MC significantly improves robustness against promotion attacks while maintaining acceptable recommendation performance under the defense-accuracy trade-off.
AI.conclusion: UAT-MC effectively addresses cross-modal gradient mismatch in evasion-based attacks, providing synchronized multimodal perturbations that maximize adversarial strength for robust training of multimodal recommender systems.

title: GlazyBench: A Benchmark for Ceramic Glaze Property Prediction and Image Generation
summary: Developing ceramic glazes is a costly, time-consuming process of trial and error due to complex chemistry, placing a significant burden on independent artists. While recent advances in multimodal AI offer a modern solution, the field lacks the large-scale datasets required to train these models. We propose GlazyBench, the first dataset for AI-assisted glaze design. Comprising 23,148 real glaze formulations, GlazyBench supports two primary tasks: predicting post-firing surface properties, such as color and transparency, from raw materials, and generating accurate visual representations of the glaze based on these properties. We establish comprehensive baselines for property prediction using traditional machine learning and large language models, alongside image generation benchmarks using deep generative and large multimodal models. Our experiments demonstrate promising yet challenging results. GlazyBench pioneers a new research direction in AI-assisted material design, providing a standardized benchmark for systematic evaluation.

AI.tldr: GlazyBench is the first dataset for AI-assisted ceramic glaze design, containing 23,148 real glaze formulations to enable property prediction and visual generation tasks, establishing baselines for machine learning and multimodal AI approaches.
AI.motivation: Developing ceramic glazes is expensive and time-consuming due to complex chemistry, placing significant burden on independent artists. The field lacks large-scale datasets needed to train modern multimodal AI systems that could offer solutions.
AI.method: Created GlazyBench dataset with 23,148 real glaze formulations supporting two main tasks: 1) predicting post-firing surface properties (color, transparency) from raw materials, and 2) generating visual representations based on these properties. Established baselines using traditional ML, LLMs, and image generation benchmarks with deep generative and large multimodal models.
AI.result: Experiments demonstrate promising yet challenging results for both property prediction and visual generation tasks. The dataset enables systematic evaluation and benchmarking of AI approaches for ceramic glaze design.
AI.conclusion: GlazyBench pioneers a new research direction in AI-assisted material design by providing the first standardized benchmark for ceramic glaze development, addressing the data scarcity problem and enabling future systematic evaluation of AI methods in this domain.

title: When Graph Language Models Go Beyond Memorization
summary: It remains unclear whether graph language models learn structural regularities or merely memorize training graphs; this cannot be resolved by current aggregate fidelity metrics alone. We develop a calibrated diagnostic protocol that combines frequent subgraph mining, a graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification to disentangle memorization from structural alignment. Using this framework, we show that graph language models can acquire structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, primarily in the high-frequency regime. This is supported by the following empirical evidence: On five TU benchmarks, LLaMA-style graph language models reach high subgraph-rank correlation, yet their alignment is matched or exceeded by the memorization bootstrap in most cases. At small scale, under our bootstrap diagnostic, fidelity is largely indistinguishable from verbatim recall. In contrast, at large scale with 3.75M graphs, verbatim memorization drops sharply while rank correlation remains near ceiling. Crucially, in a separate fixed-subsample analysis, frequent subgraph mining restricted to the novel-only subset closely tracks the corresponding all-generation Spearman correlation, providing evidence that the alignment is not driven solely by verbatim recall. Across all scales, high-frequency patterns are well reproduced, while rare patterns remain poorly covered, and this deficit narrows only marginally as capacity increases. We observe the same scale-dependent crossover under two distinct graph serializations (canonical DFS code and action sequences), providing evidence of robustness in our analysis.

AI.tldr: Graph language models can learn structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, particularly for high-frequency patterns, but struggle with rare patterns even as capacity increases.
AI.motivation: There's an unclear distinction in graph language models between whether they genuinely learn structural regularities or simply memorize training graphs, which cannot be resolved by current aggregate fidelity metrics alone.
AI.method: Developed a calibrated diagnostic protocol combining frequent subgraph mining, graph-level bootstrap baseline, and three-level frequency stratification to disentangle memorization from structural alignment. Applied to LLaMA-style graph language models across five TU benchmarks at different scales, with both canonical DFS code and action sequence serializations.
AI.result: At small scale, fidelity is largely indistinguishable from verbatim recall. At large scale (3.75M graphs), verbatim memorization drops sharply while rank correlation remains near ceiling. High-frequency patterns are well reproduced across all scales, while rare patterns remain poorly covered. The scale-dependent crossover is robust across different graph serializations.
AI.conclusion: Graph language models can acquire structural regularities beyond memorization at scale, primarily in the high-frequency regime, though they still struggle with rare patterns even as capacity increases.

title: AI Co-Mathematician: Accelerating Mathematicians with Agentic AI
summary: We introduce the AI co-mathematician, a workbench for mathematicians to interactively leverage AI agents to pursue open-ended research. The AI co-mathematician is optimized to provide holistic support for the exploratory and iterative reality of mathematical workflows, including ideation, literature search, computational exploration, theorem proving and theory building. By providing an asynchronous, stateful workspace that manages uncertainty, refines user intent, tracks failed hypotheses, and outputs native mathematical artifacts, the system mirrors human collaborative workflows. In early tests, the AI co-mathematician helped researchers solve open problems, identify new research directions, and uncover overlooked literature references. Besides demonstrating a highly interactive paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery, the AI co-mathematician also achieves state of the art results on hard problem-solving benchmarks, including scoring 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4, a new high score among all AI systems evaluated.

AI.tldr: AI co-mathematician is an interactive workbench that helps mathematicians conduct research using AI agents for ideation, literature search, computation, theorem proving, and theory building.
AI.motivation: Mathematical research involves exploratory, iterative workflows that current AI systems don't fully support. There's a need for AI systems that can provide holistic support for the complete mathematical research process, including managing uncertainty, refining intent, and tracking failed hypotheses.
AI.method: Developed an asynchronous, stateful workspace where mathematicians can interactively leverage AI agents. The system manages uncertainty, refines user intent, tracks failed hypotheses, and outputs native mathematical artifacts, mirroring human collaborative workflows.
AI.result: In early tests, the system helped researchers solve open problems, identify new research directions, and uncover overlooked literature references. It achieved state-of-the-art results on hard problem-solving benchmarks, scoring 48% on FrontierMath Tier 4 (new high score among AI systems).
AI.conclusion: The AI co-mathematician demonstrates a highly interactive paradigm for AI-assisted mathematical discovery that effectively supports the exploratory nature of mathematical research while achieving competitive benchmark performance.

title: Cumulative-Goodness Free-Riding in Forward-Forward Networks: Real, Repairable, but Not Accuracy-Dominant
summary: Forward-Forward (FF) training allows each layer to learn from a local goodness criterion. In cumulative-goodness variants, however, later layers can inherit a task that earlier layers have already partially separated. We formalize this phenomenon as layer free-riding: under the softplus FF criterion, the class-discrimination gradient reaching block $d$ decays exponentially with the positive margin accumulated by preceding blocks. We then study three local remedies -- per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled -- that recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients. On CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, these remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics, with $4\times$--$45\times$ gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point for non-degenerate training procedures. Tiny ImageNet provides a tougher cross-dataset check for our selected block-wise configuration and reveals the same qualitative gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments further show that architecture and augmentation choices have a larger effect on final accuracy than the training-rule modifications studied here. Cumulative free-riding is therefore a real and repairable optimization pathology. Nonetheless, for the FF training rules, architectures, and datasets we study, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies "layer free-riding" in Forward-Forward training where later layers inherit tasks already solved by earlier layers, proposes local remedies to improve layer separation, but finds these training rule modifications have minimal impact on final accuracy compared to architectural choices.
AI.motivation: The motivation is to address the optimization pathology in Forward-Forward training where later layers can "free-ride" on the work done by earlier layers, inheriting tasks that have already been partially separated, which prevents each layer from developing its own discriminative capabilities.
AI.method: The authors formalize "layer free-riding" phenomenon, show that class-discrimination gradients decay exponentially with positive margin accumulated by preceding blocks, and propose three local remedies: per-block, hardness-gated, and depth-scaled approaches that recover current-layer separation measures without relying on backpropagated gradients.
AI.result: On CIFAR-10 and CIFAR-100, the remedies dramatically improve layer-separation statistics with 4×–45× gains in deeper layers, while changing accuracy by less than one percentage point. Tiny ImageNet confirms the gap between layer-health diagnostics and final accuracy. Calibration experiments show architecture and augmentation choices have larger effects on final accuracy than training-rule modifications.
AI.conclusion: Cumulative free-riding is a real and repairable optimization pathology in Forward-Forward training, but for the training rules, architectures, and datasets studied, it is not the dominant factor limiting achievable accuracy - architectural and data augmentation choices have more significant impact.

title: A Note on TurboQuant and the Earlier DRIVE/EDEN Line of Work
summary:
This note clarifies the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and the earlier DRIVE (NeurIPS 2021) and EDEN (ICML 2022) schemes. DRIVE is a 1-bit quantizer that EDEN extended to any $b>0$ bits per coordinate; we refer to them collectively as EDEN.
  First, TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ is a special case of EDEN obtained by fixing EDEN's scalar scale parameter to $S=1$. EDEN supports both biased and unbiased quantization, each optimized by a different $S$ (chosen via methods described in the EDEN works). The fixed choice $S=1$ used by TurboQuant is generally suboptimal, although the optimal $S$ for biased EDEN converges to $1$ as the dimension grows; accordingly TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$ approaches EDEN's behavior for large $d$.
  Second, TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$ combines a biased $(b-1)$-bit EDEN step with an unbiased 1-bit QJL quantization of the residual. It is suboptimal in three ways: (1) its $(b-1)$-bit step uses the suboptimal $S=1$; (2) its 1-bit unbiased residual quantization has worse MSE than (unbiased) 1-bit EDEN; (3) chaining a biased $(b-1)$-bit step with a 1-bit unbiased residual step is inferior to unbiasedly quantizing the input directly with $b$-bit EDEN.
  Third, some of the analysis in the TurboQuant work mirrors that of the EDEN works: both exploit the connection between random rotations and the shifted Beta distribution, use the Lloyd-Max algorithm, and note that Randomized Hadamard Transforms can replace uniform random rotations.
  Experiments support these claims: biased EDEN (with optimized $S$) is more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{mse}}$, and unbiased EDEN is markedly more accurate than TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$, often by more than a bit (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant$_{\text{prod}}$). We also repeat all accuracy experiments from the TurboQuant paper, showing that EDEN outperforms it in every setup we have tried.

AI.tldr: This note clarifies that TurboQuant is actually a suboptimal special case of the earlier EDEN quantization framework, and EDEN outperforms TurboQuant in all experiments.
AI.motivation: To clarify the relationship between the recent TurboQuant work and earlier DRIVE/EDEN quantization schemes, showing that TurboQuant is essentially a limited version of EDEN with suboptimal parameter choices.
AI.method: Comparative analysis showing that: (1) TurboQuant_mse is EDEN with fixed S=1 (suboptimal), (2) TurboQuant_prod combines suboptimal biased (b-1)-bit EDEN with worse 1-bit residual quantization, (3) Much of TurboQuant's analysis mirrors EDEN's approach.
AI.result: Experiments show biased EDEN (with optimized S) is more accurate than TurboQuant_mse, and unbiased EDEN is significantly more accurate than TurboQuant_prod (e.g., 2-bit EDEN beats 3-bit TurboQuant_prod). EDEN outperforms TurboQuant in every experimental setup tested.
AI.conclusion: TurboQuant is a limited special case of the more general and superior EDEN quantization framework, with EDEN providing better accuracy through optimized parameters and better algorithmic design.

title: Structure-Preserving Gaussian Processes Via Discrete Euler-Lagrange Equations
summary: In this paper, we propose Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) for probabilistic and data-efficient learning of dynamics via discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations. Importantly, the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle, which governs the motion of dynamical systems, is preserved by construction in the absence of external forces. This allows learning physically consistent models that overcome erroneous drift in the system's energy, thereby providing stable long-term predictions. At the core of our approach lie linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning, constructed from discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes. Thereby and unlike prior work, the method enables learning dynamics from discrete position snapshots, i.e., without access to a system's velocities or momenta. This is particularly relevant for a large class of practical scenarios where only position measurements are available, for instance, in motion capture or visual servoing applications. We demonstrate the data-efficiency and generalization capabilities of the LGPs in various synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real-world soft robot with hysteresis. The experimental results underscore that the LGPs learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification solely from sparse positional data and enable stable long-term predictions.

AI.tldr: Lagrangian Gaussian Processes (LGPs) provide a data-efficient method for learning dynamics from discrete position measurements only, preserving physical structure for stable long-term predictions.
AI.motivation: Traditional methods often require velocity/momentum data for learning dynamics, but many practical applications (like motion capture or visual servoing) only provide position measurements. Current approaches may suffer from energy drift and unstable long-term predictions due to lack of physical structure preservation.
AI.method: LGPs use discrete forced Euler-Lagrange equations and variational discretization schemes to create linear operators for Gaussian process conditioning. The method preserves the geometric structure of the Lagrange-d'Alembert principle by construction, ensuring physical consistency without needing velocity data.
AI.result: LGPs demonstrate data-efficiency and generalization in synthetic and real-world case studies, including a real soft robot with hysteresis. They learn physically consistent dynamics with uncertainty quantification from sparse positional data alone and enable stable long-term predictions.
AI.conclusion: LGPs offer a principled approach to learning dynamics from position-only measurements, overcoming limitations of previous methods by preserving physical structure, preventing energy drift, and enabling stable long-term predictions with uncertainty quantification.

title: The Role of Node Features in Graph Pooling
summary: Graph pooling is commonly applied in graph classification, yet its empirical gains over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs are often marginal or inconsistent. We study this gap by analysing the interaction between node features and graph topology and their effect on pooling objectives. Our analysis reveals that pooling operators require node features that are well-aligned with the graph's topology -- a condition often overlooked and not guaranteed in empirical networks. We formalise fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling, and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. Our empirical evaluation shows that, when these requirements are satisfied, pooling can be beneficial and improve performance on appropriate datasets.

AI.tldr: Pooling in graph classification has limited empirical gains because it requires node features well-aligned with graph topology, a condition often missing in real networks. The paper formalizes feature requirements and introduces a quality measure to predict when pooling will be effective.
AI.motivation: Graph pooling shows marginal or inconsistent improvements over standard WL-1 expressive GNNs in graph classification tasks. The authors investigate why this performance gap exists, focusing on the interaction between node features and graph topology and its impact on pooling effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors analyze the interaction between node features and graph topology for pooling objectives. They formalize fundamental requirements for node features to enable effective pooling and introduce a quantitative measure of feature quality. They conduct empirical evaluation to validate their theoretical findings.
AI.result: Empirical evaluation shows that when node features satisfy the formalized requirements (being well-aligned with graph topology), pooling can be beneficial and improve performance on appropriate datasets. The introduced feature quality measure helps predict when pooling will be effective.
AI.conclusion: The performance gap in graph pooling stems from the often overlooked requirement that node features must be well-aligned with graph topology. By formalizing these requirements and introducing feature quality measures, researchers can better predict when pooling will be effective and identify suitable datasets for pooling-based approaches.

title: The Weight Gram Matrix Captures Sequential Feature Linearization in Deep Networks
summary: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. In this work, we propose a feature-centric framework for analyzing neural network training by relating weight updates to feature evolution. We introduce a simple identity, the Feature Learning Equation, which identifies the weight Gram matrix as the key object capturing feature dynamics. This enables us to interpret gradient descent as implicitly inducing a hypothetical evolution of features, whose covariance structure - termed the Virtual Covariance - characterizes how representations evolve during training. Building on this perspective, we introduce Target Linearity, a measure quantifying the linear alignment between features and targets. By analyzing the training and layer-wise dynamics, we show that deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure. This linearization perspective provides a unified interpretation of several empirical phenomena, including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.

AI.tldr: A feature-centric framework analyzes neural network training by relating weight updates to feature evolution via a Feature Learning Equation, revealing that deep networks sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure.
AI.motivation: Understanding how deep neural networks learn representations remains a central challenge in machine learning theory. The paper aims to develop a theoretical framework for analyzing feature evolution during neural network training.
AI.method: Proposes a feature-centric framework with a Feature Learning Equation that identifies weight Gram matrix as key object capturing feature dynamics. Introduces Virtual Covariance to characterize representation evolution and Target Linearity measure to quantify linear alignment between features and targets.
AI.result: Analysis shows that deep networks learn to sequentially transform representations toward target-linear structure. This linearization perspective provides unified interpretation of empirical phenomena including Neural Collapse and linear interpolation in generative models.
AI.conclusion: The feature learning framework offers new theoretical insights into how neural networks evolve representations during training, with implications for understanding phenomena like Neural Collapse and providing a unified perspective on feature learning dynamics.

title: Trade-off Functions for DP-SGD with Subsampling based on Random Shuffling: Tight Upper and Lower Bounds
summary: We derive a tight analysis of the trade-off function for Differentially Private Stochastic Gradient Descent (DP-SGD) with subsampling based on random shuffling within the $f$-DP framework. Our analysis covers the regime $σ\geq \sqrt{3/\ln M}$, where $σ$ is the noise multiplier and $M$ is the number of rounds within a single epoch. Unlike $f$-DP analyses for Poisson subsampling, which yield non-closed implicit formulas that can be machine computed but are non-transparent, random shuffling admits a tight analysis yielding transparent and interpretable closed-form bounds. Our concrete bounds, derived via the Berry-Esseen theorem, are tight up to constant factors within the proof framework. We demonstrate worked parameter settings for a single epoch ($E=1$) with a corresponding trade-off function $\geq 1-a-δ$, that is, only $δ$ below the ideal random guessing diagonal $1-a$: For $δ= 1/100$ and $σ= 1$, roughly $M \approx 1.14\times 10^6$ rounds and $N \approx 1.14\times 10^7$ training samples suffice to achieve meaningful differential privacy. This is in contrast to recent negative results for the regime $σ\leq 1/\sqrt{2 \ln M}$. Our concrete bounds can be composed over multiple epochs leading to $δ$ having a linear in $E$ dependency, which restricts $E=O(\sqrt{M})$. To go beyond Berry--Esseen, we introduce a new proof technique based on a generalization of the law of large numbers that yields an asymptotic random guessing diagonal-limit result: if $E=c_M^2M$ with $c_M\to 0$, then the $E$-fold composed trade-off function satisfies $f^{\otimes E}(a)\to 1-a$ uniformly in $a\in[0,1]$ with $δ$ having only an $O(\sqrt{E})$ dependency. We compare this asymptotic regime with the corresponding Poisson subsampling asymptotic, and highlight the characterization of explicit convergence rates as an open question.

AI.tldr: Tight f-DP analysis of DP-SGD with random shuffling yields transparent closed-form bounds, enabling meaningful privacy with ~10^7 samples at δ=1/100, improving over Poisson subsampling and recent negative results.
AI.motivation: Existing f-DP analyses for DP-SGD with Poisson subsampling produce non-closed, non-transparent formulas, while random shuffling analysis has been incomplete. Recent negative results for σ≤1/√(2lnM) regime need to be addressed.
AI.method: Tight f-DP analysis of DP-SGD with random shuffling using Berry-Esseen theorem for concrete bounds (tight up to constants), and new proof technique based on generalized law of large numbers for asymptotic analysis.
AI.result: Derived transparent closed-form bounds showing meaningful differential privacy achievable with δ=1/100, σ=1, M≈1.14×10^6 rounds, N≈1.14×10^7 samples. Asymptotic analysis shows E-fold composition yields f^{⊗E}(a)→1-a uniformly when E=c_M²M with c_M→0, with only O(√E) dependency on δ.
AI.conclusion: Random shuffling admits tight, interpretable f-DP analysis superior to Poisson subsampling, enabling practical DP-SGD parameter settings. New proof techniques provide both concrete bounds and asymptotic results, though explicit convergence rates remain open.

title: Beyond Rigid Alignment: Graph Federated Learning via Dual Manifold Calibration
summary: Graph Federated Learning (GFL) enables collaborative representation learning across distributed subgraphs while preserving privacy. However, heterogeneity remains a critical challenge, as subgraphs across clients typically differ significantly in both semantics and structures. Existing methods address heterogeneity by enforcing the rigid alignment of model parameters or prototypes between clients and the server. However, these alignments implicitly rely on a restrictive global linearity assumption that summarizes local data distributions using a single and globally consistent representation space. This severely compresses the personalized representation space of clients and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Federated Graph Manifold Calibration (FedGMC), a novel paradigm that tackles semantic heterogeneity and structural heterogeneity from a unified manifold perspective. Instead of enforcing rigid alignment, FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism that preserves global commonalities while maximizing the personalized representation space of local clients. Specifically, for semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs a geometrically optimal semantic manifold via equidistant semantic anchors, so as to guide the calibration of local semantic manifolds. For structural heterogeneity, the server constructs a global structural manifold by building global structural templates, so as to guide the calibration of local structural manifolds. Finally, the server dynamically refines both global semantic manifolds and structural manifolds by aggregating local manifolds. Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate that FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, thereby significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.

AI.tldr: FedGMC is a federated graph learning method that uses dual manifold calibration instead of rigid alignment to handle semantic and structural heterogeneity across distributed subgraphs, achieving better balance between global commonality and local personalization.
AI.motivation: Existing GFL methods address heterogeneity through rigid alignment of model parameters or prototypes, which relies on a restrictive global linearity assumption that compresses personalized representation spaces and fails to preserve diverse local graph distributions.
AI.method: FedGMC introduces a dual manifold calibration mechanism: (1) For semantic heterogeneity, the server constructs geometrically optimal semantic manifolds via equidistant semantic anchors to calibrate local semantic manifolds. (2) For structural heterogeneity, the server builds global structural manifolds using structural templates to calibrate local structural manifolds. The server dynamically refines both global manifolds by aggregating local manifolds.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on eleven homophilic and heterophilic graphs demonstrate that FedGMC effectively balances global commonality and local personalization, significantly outperforming state-of-the-art baseline methods.
AI.conclusion: FedGMC provides a novel manifold perspective for addressing heterogeneity in federated graph learning, overcoming limitations of rigid alignment approaches by preserving global commonalities while maximizing personalized representation spaces for local clients.

title: Inference-Time Refinement Closes the Synthetic-Real Gap in Tabular Diffusion
summary: Diffusion-based generators set the current state of the art for synthetic tabular data. These methods approach but rarely exceed real-data utility, and closing this synthetic-real gap has so far been pursued exclusively at training time, via architectural advances, scaling, and retraining of monolithic generators. The inference-time alternative, i.e., refining the outputs of a pre-trained backbone with parameters left untouched, has remained largely unexplored for tabular synthesis. We introduce TARDIS (Tabular generation through Refinement, Distillation, and Inference-time Sampling), an inference-time refinement framework that operates on a frozen pre-trained backbone, configured per dataset by a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion, with each trial's objective set by an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and an optional soft-label distillation step. The search space encodes a single mathematical pattern we name Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR): the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples is minimized both continuously, via a score-level gradient, and discretely, via batch-ranking post-generation. The per-dataset search recovers BCR-aligned configurations on most datasets, evidence for BCR as the dominant refinement pattern. Across 15 binary, multiclass, and regression benchmarks TARDIS achieves a median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (95% CI [+3.3, +16.4], Wilcoxon p=0.016, 11/15 strict wins) and improves over the TabDiff backbone on all 15 datasets (mean +12.9%, p<10^-4), matching the backbone on manifold fidelity, diversity, and sample-level privacy. Inference-time refinement of a pre-trained tabular diffusion backbone reaches and exceeds real-data utility in 1 to 80 minutes on a single consumer-grade GPU.

AI.tldr: TARDIS is an inference-time refinement framework for tabular diffusion models that enhances synthetic data quality without retraining the backbone model, achieving better-than-real-data performance on downstream tasks.
AI.motivation: Current tabular diffusion models approach but rarely exceed real-data utility, and existing approaches focus on training-time improvements. The authors want to explore inference-time refinement as an alternative to closing the synthetic-real data gap without retraining the entire model.
AI.method: TARDIS uses a Tree-structured Parzen Estimator search over score-level guidance during reverse diffusion of a frozen pre-trained backbone. Each trial includes an inner grid search over post-hoc sample selectors and optional soft-label distillation. The framework implements Bidirectional Chamfer Refinement (BCR) which minimizes the symmetric Chamfer functional between synthetic and real samples continuously via score-level gradients and discretely via batch-ranking.
AI.result: Across 15 benchmarks (binary, multiclass, and regression), TARDIS achieves median +8.6% downstream-task improvement over models trained on real data (11/15 strict wins) and +12.9% improvement over the TabDiff backbone on all datasets, while maintaining manifold fidelity, diversity, and privacy. Inference-time refinement takes 1-80 minutes on a single consumer GPU.
AI.conclusion: Inference-time refinement of pre-trained tabular diffusion models can exceed real-data utility, with BCR emerging as the dominant refinement pattern. This approach provides a practical alternative to expensive retraining while achieving state-of-the-art synthetic data quality.

title: Can Attribution Predict Risk? From Multi-View Attribution to Planning Risk Signals in End-to-End Autonomous Driving
summary: End-to-end autonomous driving models generate future trajectories from multi-view inputs, improving system integration but introducing opaque decisions and hard-to-localize risks. Existing methods either rely on auxiliary monitoring models or generate textual explanations, but are decoupled from the planning process and fail to reveal the visual evidence underlying trajectory generation. While attribution offers a direct alternative, planning differs from image classification by taking six-view camera images as input and predicting continuous multi-step trajectories, requiring attribution to capture both critical views and regions and their influence on outputs. Moreover, whether attribution maps can support risk identification remains underexplored. To address this, we propose a hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end planning. Specifically, using L2 consistency with the original trajectory as the objective, we design a coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy that searches candidate regions across the full six-view input and refines attribution within them. We further extract three attribution statistics as predictive signals for planning risk, including attribution entropy to measure how concentrated the planner's reliance is over the joint visual space, within-camera spatial variance to characterize how spread out the attribution is within each view, and cross-camera Gini coefficient to quantify how unevenly attribution is distributed across the six cameras. Experiments on BridgeAD, UniAD, and GenAD show that these statistics correlate with planning risk, achieving Spearman correlations of $0.30 \pm 0.07$ with trajectory error and AUROC of $0.77 \pm 0.04$ for collision detection.
