The signal generalizes to held-out scenes with negligible degradation and remains stable under an alternative attribution baseline.

AI.tldr: Proposes hierarchical attribution framework for end-to-end autonomous driving models to reveal visual evidence behind trajectory decisions and identify planning risks through attribution statistics.
AI.motivation: End-to-end autonomous driving models are opaque in their decision-making and have hard-to-localize risks. Existing explanation methods are decoupled from planning process and fail to reveal visual evidence underlying trajectory generation, while attribution for planning faces challenges with multi-view inputs and continuous trajectory outputs.
AI.method: Hierarchical attribution framework with coarse-to-fine region attribution strategy: searches candidate regions across six-view camera inputs and refines attribution within them using L2 consistency objective. Extracts three attribution statistics: attribution entropy (concentration of planner's reliance), within-camera spatial variance (spread of attribution within each view), and cross-camera Gini coefficient (unevenness of attribution distribution across cameras).
AI.result: Experiments on BridgeAD, UniAD, and GenAD show attribution statistics correlate with planning risk: Spearman correlations of 0.30±0.07 with trajectory error and AUROC of 0.77±0.04 for collision detection. The signal generalizes to held-out scenes with negligible degradation and remains stable under alternative attribution baseline.
AI.conclusion: The proposed hierarchical attribution framework successfully reveals visual evidence in end-to-end autonomous driving models and provides predictive signals for planning risk identification, offering a promising approach for explaining and monitoring opaque planning decisions.

title: A Flow Matching Algorithm for Many-Shot Adaptation to Unseen Distributions
summary: While generative modeling has achieved remarkable success on tasks like natural language-conditioned image generation, enabling model adaptation from example data points remains a relatively underexplored and challenging problem. To this end, we propose Function Projection for Flow Matching (FP-FM), an algorithm that directly conditions generation on samples from the target distribution. FP-FM learns basis functions to span the velocity fields corresponding to a set of training distributions, and adapts to new distributions by computing a simple least-squares projection onto this basis. This enables efficient generation of samples from diverse target distributions without additional training at inference time. We further introduce multiple variants of FP-FM that provide a trade-off in expressivity and compute by enriching the coefficient calculation, e.g., by making the coefficients dependent on time. FP-FM achieves greatly improved precision and recall relative to baselines across synthetic and image-based datasets, with especially strong gains on unseen distributions.

AI.tldr: FP-FM is a flow matching method that enables efficient adaptation to new distributions by learning basis functions for velocity fields and using simple least-squares projection, achieving strong performance on unseen distributions without additional training.
AI.motivation: While generative modeling has achieved success in conditional tasks like text-to-image generation, adapting models from example data points remains challenging and underexplored. The paper aims to address the problem of enabling model adaptation directly from target distribution samples.
AI.method: Proposes Function Projection for Flow Matching (FP-FM) which learns basis functions to span velocity fields corresponding to training distributions. Adaptation to new distributions is achieved through a simple least-squares projection onto this learned basis. Also introduces variants that provide trade-offs in expressivity and compute by enriching coefficient calculations, such as making coefficients time-dependent.
AI.result: FP-FM achieves significantly improved precision and recall relative to baselines across synthetic and image-based datasets. The method shows especially strong gains on unseen distributions, demonstrating effective adaptation capabilities.
AI.conclusion: FP-FM provides an effective approach for generative model adaptation from example data points without additional training at inference time. The method's efficiency and strong performance on unseen distributions make it a promising solution for distribution adaptation problems in generative modeling.

title: When Labels Have Structure: Improving Image Classification with Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy
summary: Standard cross-entropy is the default classification loss across virtually all of machine learning, yet it treats all misclassifications equally, ignoring the semantic distances that a class hierarchy encodes. We propose Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy (HACE), a drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy that incorporates a known class hierarchy directly into the loss. HACE combines two components: prediction aggregation, which propagates the model's probability mass upward through the class hierarchy to ensure that parent nodes accumulate the confidence of their children; and ancestral label smoothing, which distributes the ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root. We evaluate HACE on CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, and NABirds in two regimes: end-to-end training across six architectures spanning convolutional and attention-based designs, and linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features. In end-to-end training, HACE improves accuracy over standard cross-entropy in 15 out of 18 architecture--dataset pairs, with a mean gain of 4.66\%. In linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features, HACE outperforms all competing methods on all three datasets, with a mean improvement of 2.18\% over the next best baseline.

AI.tldr: HACE (Hierarchy-Aware Cross-Entropy) is a drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy that incorporates class hierarchies into the loss function to improve classification performance by considering semantic distances between classes.
AI.motivation: Standard cross-entropy loss treats all misclassifications equally, ignoring the semantic distances encoded in class hierarchies. This means that predicting a semantically similar class (e.g., different bird species) is penalized as heavily as predicting a completely unrelated class (e.g., bird vs. car).
AI.method: HACE combines two components: 1) Prediction aggregation - propagates probability mass upward through the class hierarchy so parent nodes accumulate confidence of their children, and 2) Ancestral label smoothing - distributes ground-truth signal along the path from the true class to the root.
AI.result: In end-to-end training: HACE improved accuracy over standard cross-entropy in 15 out of 18 architecture-dataset pairs with mean gain of 4.66%. In linear probing on frozen DINOv2-Large features: HACE outperformed all competing methods on all three datasets (CIFAR-100, FGVC Aircraft, NABirds) with mean improvement of 2.18% over next best baseline.
AI.conclusion: HACE effectively incorporates class hierarchy information into the classification loss, leading to consistent improvements over standard cross-entropy across different architectures and training regimes. The method provides a simple yet effective drop-in replacement for standard cross-entropy when class hierarchies are available.

title: PACE: Prune-And-Compress Ensemble Models
summary: Ensemble models achieve state-of-the-art performance on prediction tasks, but usually require aggregating a large number of weak learners. This can hinder deployment, interpretability, and downstream tasks such as robustness verification. Remedies to this issue fall into two main camps: pruning, which discards redundant learners, and compression, which generates new ones from scratch. We introduce PACE, a framework that interleaves these paradigms in a two-phase strategy. First, new learners are actively generated via a theoretically grounded procedure to enhance the diversity of the initial ensemble. When no more relevant learners can be found, a second phase of pruning is performed on this enriched ensemble. During both operations, PACE allows fine control on the faithfulness to the original ensemble. Experiments show that our method outperforms prior pruning and compression methods while offering principled control of faithfulness guarantees.

AI.tldr: PACE is a two-phase framework that first actively generates new learners to enhance ensemble diversity, then prunes the enriched ensemble, while maintaining fine control over faithfulness to the original ensemble.
AI.motivation: Ensemble models achieve state-of-the-art performance but have deployment issues due to requiring many weak learners, which hinders interpretability, verification, and practical use.
AI.method: Two-phase approach: 1) Active generation of new learners via theoretically grounded procedure to enhance ensemble diversity, 2) Pruning performed on the enriched ensemble. Both phases allow fine control of faithfulness to original ensemble.
AI.result: Outperforms prior pruning and compression methods while offering principled control of faithfulness guarantees.
AI.conclusion: PACE provides an effective framework that interleaves compression and pruning paradigms to reduce ensemble size while maintaining performance and offering control over faithfulness.

title: INEUS: Iterative Neural Solver for High-Dimensional PIDEs
summary: In this paper, we introduce INEUS, a meshfree iterative neural solver for partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs). The method replaces the explicit evaluation of nonlocal jump integrals with single-jump sampling and reformulates PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems. Like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs), INEUS learns global solutions over the entire space-time domain, yet it offers a more efficient treatment of nonlocal terms and avoids the computationally expensive differentiation of full PIDE residuals. These features make INEUS particularly well suited for high-dimensional PDEs and PIDEs. Supported by a contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs, our numerical experiments show that INEUS delivers accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear examples.

AI.tldr: INEUS: a meshfree neural solver for PIDEs using single-jump sampling instead of nonlocal integrals, formulated as recursive regression problems with better efficiency than PINNs.
AI.motivation: Existing methods like Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) have computational limitations when handling partial integro-differential equations (PIDEs), particularly with nonlocal jump integrals. There's a need for more efficient neural solvers for high-dimensional PIDEs.
AI.method: INEUS replaces explicit evaluation of nonlocal jump integrals with single-jump sampling, reformulating PIDE solving as a sequence of recursive regression problems. It's meshfree, learns global solutions like PINNs, but avoids expensive differentiation of full PIDE residuals.
AI.result: Numerical experiments demonstrate INEUS delivers accurate and scalable solutions for various high-dimensional linear and nonlinear PIDEs, supported by contraction-based convergence proof for linear PIDEs.
AI.conclusion: INEUS is a promising neural solver for high-dimensional PIDEs that offers computational advantages over PINNs, with theoretical convergence guarantees and demonstrated effectiveness on challenging examples.

title: Attributions All the Way Down? The Metagame of Interpretability
summary: We introduce the metagame, a conceptual framework for quantifying second-order interaction effects of model explanations. For any first-order attribution $φ(f)$ explaining a model $f$, we measure the directional influence of feature $j$ on the attribution of feature $i$, denoted as meta-attribution $\varphi_{j \to i}(f)$, by treating the attribution method itself as a cooperative game and computing its Shapley value. Theoretically, we prove that attributions hierarchically decompose into meta-attributions, and establish these as directional extensions of existing interaction indices. Empirically, we demonstrate that the metagame delivers insights across diverse interpretability applications: (i) quantifying token interactions in instruction-tuned language models, (ii) explaining cross-modal similarity in vision-language encoders, and (iii) interpreting text-to-image concepts in multimodal diffusion transformers.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces the metagame framework for quantifying second-order interaction effects in model explanations by treating attribution methods as cooperative games and computing meta-attributions via Shapley values.
AI.motivation: Current first-order attribution methods (like SHAP, Grad-CAM, etc.) explain individual feature importance but fail to capture how features interact with each other's explanations - essentially lacking a way to quantify second-order interactions in model interpretability.
AI.method: The metagame framework treats any first-order attribution method φ(f) as a cooperative game itself, then computes meta-attributions φ_{j→i}(f) using Shapley values to measure the directional influence of feature j on the attribution of feature i. This creates a hierarchical decomposition where attributions break down into meta-attributions.
AI.result: Theoretical results prove that attributions hierarchically decompose into meta-attributions and establish them as directional extensions of existing interaction indices. Empirical demonstrations show the framework provides insights in: (1) token interactions in instruction-tuned language models, (2) cross-modal similarity in vision-language encoders, and (3) text-to-image concept interpretation in multimodal diffusion transformers.
AI.conclusion: The metagame provides a principled framework for quantifying second-order interaction effects in model explanations, extending beyond first-order attribution methods to reveal how features influence each other's importance across various AI architectures and applications.

title: Region Seeding via Pre-Activation Regularization: A Geometric View from Piecewise Affine Nerual Networks
summary: Deep networks with continuous piecewise affine activations induce polyhedral partitions of the input space, making the number of realized affine regions a natural measure of expressive capacity and a key determinant of how well the model can approximate nonlinear target functions. In practice, standard training realizes far fewer region refinements in data-visited neighborhoods than the architecture could in principle support, while existing region-count theory is primarily architectural and offers little guidance on how optimization shapes the realized partition near the data. Our theory provides a sufficient condition under which bringing neuron switching surfaces sufficiently close to data points ensures their intersection with local neighborhoods, which in turn implies a strict increase in the local affine-region count, yielding a principled training-time handle for seeding data-relevant partitions early in optimization. Guided by these results, we propose a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that encourages early partitioning while allowing task-driven refinement to dominate later in training. Experiments show that the regularizer increases the number of realized affine regions via exact enumeration and improves overall performance on toy datasets, while also improving early-stage accuracy and achieving comparable (or slightly improved) final accuracy on ImageNet-1k for classical models.

AI.tldr: Deep neural networks with piecewise affine activations create polyhedral partitions of input space. Training typically realizes far fewer regions near data than possible. The paper provides theory connecting neuron switching surfaces near data to increased local region counts, proposes a region-seeding regularizer for early partitioning, and demonstrates improved performance.
AI.motivation: Standard training of deep networks with piecewise affine activations fails to realize the full expressive capacity of the architecture in practice - it creates far fewer affine regions in data-visited neighborhoods than theoretically possible. Existing region-count theory focuses on architectural capacity but doesn't address how optimization shapes partitions near data during training.
AI.method: Develops theoretical framework showing sufficient conditions for increasing local affine-region count: bringing neuron switching surfaces sufficiently close to data points ensures their intersection with local neighborhoods. Based on this theory, proposes a plug-and-play region-seeding regularizer that encourages early partitioning during training while allowing task-driven refinement to dominate later.
AI.result: The regularizer increases number of realized affine regions (verified via exact enumeration) and improves performance on toy datasets. On ImageNet-1k with classical models, it improves early-stage accuracy and achieves comparable or slightly improved final accuracy compared to standard training.
AI.conclusion: The work provides both theoretical understanding and practical method for realizing more of the expressive capacity of deep networks with piecewise affine activations. The region-seeding regularizer offers a principled way to improve early partitioning while maintaining task-driven optimization, bridging the gap between architectural capacity and realized expressive power in practice.

title: Molecules Meet Language: Confound-Aware Representation Learning and Chemical Property Steering in Transformer-VAE Latent Spaces
summary: Molecular generative models often assume meaningful latent geometry, but apparent property predictability can reflect sequence-level shortcuts rather than chemical organization. We study this issue in an unsupervised autoregressive Transformer-VAE trained on SELFIES. After training, we freeze the model, fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors, and use the probe weights as candidate global steering directions. To separate chemical signal from SELFIES artifacts, we introduce a confound-aware evaluation based on residualization, confound-direction alignment analysis, and decoded-molecule traversal. This is necessary because SELFIES length, branch tokens, ring tokens, and token entropy are strongly encoded in the latent space. Under this confound-aware evaluation, we find robust monotonic steering for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA. Nonlinear probes further show that some properties admit stable global directions, while others are better described by local latent gradients. Overall, our results show that chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but only when validated through decoded molecules and controlled for representation-level confounds.

AI.tldr: Molecular generative models' latent geometry may reflect sequence artifacts rather than chemical meaning; confound-aware evaluation separates real chemical steering from SELFIES artifacts.
AI.motivation: Molecular generative models assume meaningful latent geometry, but apparent property predictability could be misleading due to sequence-level shortcuts rather than genuine chemical organization.
AI.method: Train unsupervised Transformer-VAE on SELFIES, freeze model, fit linear probes to RDKit descriptors, use probe weights as candidate global steering directions. Introduce confound-aware evaluation with residualization, confound-direction alignment analysis, and decoded-molecule traversal.
AI.result: Robust monotonic steering found for cLogP, FractionCSP3, HeavyAtomCount, TPSA, BertzCT, and HBA under confound-aware evaluation. Nonlinear probes show some properties admit stable global directions while others are better described by local latent gradients.
AI.conclusion: Chemically meaningful steering can emerge in entangled molecular latent spaces, but only when validated through decoded molecules and controlled for representation-level confounds like SELFIES artifacts.

title: Perceive, Route and Modulate: Dynamic Pattern Recalibration for Time Series Forecasting
summary: Local temporal patterns in real-world time series continuously shift, rendering globally shared transformations suboptimal. Current deep forecasting models, despite their scale and complexity, rely on fixed weight matrices applied uniformly to all temporal tokens. This creates a static pattern response: models settle into a compromised average, unable to adapt to changing local dynamics. We introduce Dynamic Pattern Recalibration (DPR), a backbone-agnostic mechanism that resolves this via token-level recalibration. Through a lightweight "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline, DPR computes a soft-routing distribution over a learned basis of adaptive response patterns, generating a time-aware modulation vector that recalibrates hidden states via a residual Hadamard product. As a backbone-agnostic adapter, DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead, confirming it addresses a general bottleneck. As a minimalist standalone model, DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks, validating dynamic recalibration against macroscopic parameter scaling.

AI.tldr: DPR (Dynamic Pattern Recalibration) is a backbone-agnostic mechanism that addresses the limitation of fixed weight matrices in deep forecasting models by enabling token-level recalibration through a lightweight "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline.
AI.motivation: Current deep forecasting models use fixed weight matrices applied uniformly to all temporal tokens, creating a "static pattern response" where models settle into compromised averages and cannot adapt to changing local dynamics in real-world time series.
AI.method: DPR introduces a "Perceive-Route-Modulate" pipeline that computes a soft-routing distribution over learned basis patterns, generating time-aware modulation vectors that recalibrate hidden states via residual Hadamard product. It's designed as both a backbone-agnostic adapter and a standalone model (DPRNet).
AI.result: DPR enhances forecasting across diverse architectures with minimal overhead, and DPRNet achieves competitive performance across 12 benchmarks, validating dynamic recalibration against macroscopic parameter scaling.
AI.conclusion: Dynamic pattern recalibration addresses a general bottleneck in time series forecasting models, offering an effective alternative to simply scaling model parameters, and provides both adapter and standalone model implementations for flexible deployment.

title: When Does $\ell_2$-Boosting Overfit Benignly? High-Dimensional Risk Asymptotics and the $\ell_1$ Implicit Bias
summary: Benign overfitting is well-characterized in $\ell_2$ geometries, but its behavior under the $\ell_1$ implicit bias of greedy ensembles remains challenging. The analytical barrier stems from the non-linear coupling of coordinate selection thresholds, which invalidates standard spectral resolvent tools. To isolate this algorithmic bias, we characterize the high-dimensional risk of continuous-time $\ell_2$-Boosting over $p$ features and $n$ samples. By coupling the Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem with delicate asymptotic expansions of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments, we analytically resolve the non-smooth $\ell_1$ interpolant. Under an isotropic pure-noise model, we prove that benign overfitting fails at the linear rate: greedy selection localizes noise into sparse active sets, and the excess variance decays at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(p/n))$ for noise variance $σ^2$. We remark that while this localization mechanism should persist in the presence of signals, the exact signal-noise decomposition remains an open problem. For spiked-isotropic designs with $k^*$ head eigenvalues and $r_2 = p - k^*$ tail dimensions, the risk converges to zero when $r_{2} \gg n$, but only at a logarithmic rate $Θ(σ^2/\log(r_2/n))$, which is slower than the linear decay observed in $\ell_2$ geometries. To avoid this slow convergence, we analyze the non-smooth subdifferential dynamics of the boosting flow. This yields a tuning-free early stopping rule that, under a bounded $\ell_1$-path condition, recovers the Lasso basic inequality and attains the minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for $\ell_1$-bounded signals.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes benign overfitting under ℓ₁ implicit bias in greedy ensembles, showing it fails at linear rate and decays logarithmically in pure-noise settings. An early stopping rule is proposed to achieve minimax-optimal rates for ℓ₁-bounded signals.
AI.motivation: Benign overfitting is well-understood in ℓ₂ geometries but remains challenging under ℓ₁ implicit bias of greedy ensembles, due to non-linear coupling of coordinate selection thresholds that invalidates standard spectral resolvent tools.
AI.method: Characterizes high-dimensional risk of continuous-time ℓ₂-Boosting using Convex Gaussian Minimax Theorem coupled with asymptotic expansions of double-sided truncated Gaussian moments to analytically resolve non-smooth ℓ₁ interpolants. Analyzes non-smooth subdifferential dynamics of boosting flow to develop tuning-free early stopping rules.
AI.result: Under isotropic pure-noise model, benign overfitting fails at linear rate: greedy selection localizes noise into sparse active sets, excess variance decays at logarithmic rate Θ(σ²/log(p/n)). For spiked-isotropic designs, risk converges to zero when r₂ ≫ n but only at logarithmic rate Θ(σ²/log(r₂/n)). Early stopping rule recovers Lasso basic inequality and attains minimax-optimal empirical prediction rate for ℓ₁-bounded signals.
AI.conclusion: ℓ₁ implicit bias in greedy ensembles leads to fundamentally different behavior than ℓ₂ geometries, with slower logarithmic convergence rates for benign overfitting. However, proper early stopping can recover optimal rates, highlighting the importance of algorithmic regularization in high-dimensional settings with ℓ₁ geometry.

title: Pro-KLShampoo: Projected KL-Shampoo with Whitening Recovered by Orthogonalization
summary: Optimizers that exploit the matrix structure of gradients are central to modern LLM pre-training, with two distinct frontiers: explicit Kronecker-factored preconditioning -- most recently KL-Shampoo, which estimates the preconditioner via KL divergence minimization -- and orthogonalization of the gradient momentum, exemplified by Muon and analyzed as steepest descent under the spectral norm. The two routes are typically developed in isolation. We make a structural observation about KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners: their eigenvalue spectra exhibit a \emph{spike-and-flat} shape -- a few dominant eigenvalues followed by an approximately uniform tail -- across layers and training stages, holding exactly under a rank-$ρ$ signal-plus-noise gradient model. We exploit this structure by restricting one of KL-Shampoo's Kronecker factors to a parametric family aligned with the spike-and-flat shape: full spectral structure on a tracked $r$-dimensional subspace, single shared eigenvalue across the remaining $n-r$ directions. On these directions, we apply orthogonalization. An identity shows that this orthogonalization recovers the algebraic form of full KL-Shampoo's preconditioner. On four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M / 350M, LLaMA 134M / 450M), Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo at every subspace rank we test in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory, and wallclock time to reach each loss level.

AI.tldr: Pro-KLShampoo combines Kronecker-factored preconditioning with gradient orthogonalization, exploiting the spike-and-flat eigenvalue structure of KL-Shampoo's preconditioners to achieve better performance with reduced memory and computational costs.
AI.motivation: Two distinct approaches to LLM pre-training optimization exist in isolation: explicit Kronecker-factored preconditioning (KL-Shampoo) and orthogonalization of gradient momentum (Muon). The paper aims to bridge these approaches by observing and exploiting the structural properties of KL-Shampoo's preconditioners.
AI.method: The authors observed that KL-Shampoo's Kronecker preconditioners exhibit a "spike-and-flat" eigenvalue spectrum. They exploit this by restricting one Kronecker factor to a parametric family aligned with this structure: full spectral structure on a tracked r-dimensional subspace, and a single shared eigenvalue across the remaining n-r directions. Orthogonalization is applied to these remaining directions, which algebraically recovers the full KL-Shampoo preconditioner form.
AI.result: Pro-KLShampoo consistently outperforms KL-Shampoo across four pre-training scales (GPT-2 124M/350M, LLaMA 134M/450M) in validation loss, peak per-GPU memory usage, and wallclock time to reach each loss level, at every subspace rank tested.
AI.conclusion: The spike-and-flat structure in KL-Shampoo's preconditioners provides an opportunity to combine Kronecker-factored preconditioning with orthogonalization techniques, resulting in Pro-KLShampoo which delivers superior performance with reduced computational resources.

title: SMolLM: Small Language Models Learn Small Molecular Grammar
summary: Language models for molecular design have scaled to hundreds of millions of parameters, yet how they learn chemical grammar is poorly understood. We train SMolLM, a 53K-parameter weight-shared transformer, to generate novel SMILES with 95% validity on the ZINC-250K drug-like-molecule benchmark, outperforming a standard GPT with 10 times more parameters. Mechanistically, the same block resolves SMILES constraints across passes in a fixed order: brackets first, rings second, and valence last, as shown by error classification, linear probing, and sparse autoencoders. A systematic ablation across attention heads and passes further localizes the first bracket-matching step to a single attention head. Together, these results yield a compact, mechanistically interpretable molecular generator and a testbed for studying iterative computation in formal-language domains.

AI.tldr: A tiny 53K-parameter transformer (SMolLM) outperforms 10x larger GPT models in generating valid SMILES molecular representations by using weight-sharing and iterative processing, with mechanistic insights showing a fixed three-step constraint resolution order.
AI.motivation: Despite language models scaling to hundreds of millions of parameters for molecular design, there's poor understanding of how they learn chemical grammar. The authors aim to create a compact, interpretable model to understand molecular generation mechanisms and iterative computation in formal-language domains.
AI.method: Developed SMolLM, a 53K-parameter weight-shared transformer trained to generate novel SMILES strings. Used mechanistic analysis including error classification, linear probing, and sparse autoencoders to understand how constraints are resolved. Conducted systematic ablation studies across attention heads and passes.
AI.result: SMolLM achieves 95% validity on ZINC-250K drug-like-molecule benchmark, outperforming standard GPT models with 10 times more parameters. The model resolves SMILES constraints in a fixed three-step order across passes: brackets first, rings second, and valence last. The first bracket-matching step was localized to a single attention head.
AI.conclusion: The work yields both a compact, high-performance molecular generator and a testbed for studying iterative computation in formal-language domains. It demonstrates that small, well-designed models can outperform larger ones while providing mechanistic interpretability.

title: LINC: Decoupling Local Consequence Scoring from Hidden Matching in Constructive Neural Routing
summary: Constructive neural routing solvers usually score the next action by matching a decoder context to candidate embeddings, hiding deterministic one-step consequences such as travel, waiting, slack, and capacity changes. We propose LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison), a decoder-side candidate decision architecture that computes these consequences explicitly. LINC uses them according to their decision role: centered relative consequences are compared by a shared linear local scorer, while feasible-set summaries modulate the decoder context. This preserves standard global matching and relieves the hidden state from rediscovering transition arithmetic. The Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem with Time Windows (CVRPTW) serves as the main constrained-routing stress test; the same interface extends to the Capacitated Vehicle Routing Problem (CVRP) and Traveling Salesman Problem (TSP). In particular, for CVRPTW, LINC reduces PolyNet's Solomon/Homberger gaps from 13.83\%/38.15\% to 7.26\%/14.71\%; for TSP and CVRP, it also improves external-benchmark gaps.

AI.tldr: LINC (Local Inference via Normed Comparison) is a new decoder architecture for neural routing solvers that explicitly computes and uses one-step consequences instead of hiding them, improving performance on routing problems like CVRPTW, CVRP, and TSP.
AI.motivation: Current neural routing solvers hide deterministic one-step consequences (like travel times, waiting, slack, capacity changes) in their scoring functions, making the model rediscover transition arithmetic. The authors aim to make these consequences explicit and usable in decision-making to improve performance.
AI.method: LINC is a decoder-side candidate decision architecture that: 1) explicitly computes one-step consequences of actions, 2) uses centered relative consequences with shared linear local scorer for comparison, 3) incorporates feasible-set summaries to modulate decoder context while preserving standard global matching.
AI.result: Significant improvements on routing benchmarks: For CVRPTW, reduces PolyNet's gaps from 13.83%/38.15% to 7.26%/14.71% on Solomon/Homberger datasets. Also shows improvements for TSP and CVRP compared to external benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly computing and using deterministic one-step consequences in neural routing solvers improves performance by relieving the hidden state from rediscovering transition arithmetic, demonstrating the value of structured reasoning in learned routing models.

title: Eliciting associations between clinical variables from LLMs via comparison questions across populations
summary: The training data of large language models (LLMs) comprises a wide range of biomedical literature, reflecting data from many different patient populations. We investigate how it might be possible to recover information on correlation and causal links between patient characteristics, as a key building block for medical decision making. To avoid the pitfalls of direct elicitation, we propose an approach based on structured comparison questions, specifically patient comparison triplet questions. This is combined with a statistical model for the LLM representation that provides estimates of correlations without access to activations or model internals. Intuitively, we consider how similarity decisions of LLMs based on a first variable are affected by providing information on a second variable for one of the patients being assessed. We then induce prompt-level environment shifts to obtain correlation estimates for different subpopulations, which enables an invariant causal prediction (ICP) approach to obtain conservative candidate parent links. We demonstrate the method in two clinical domains, chronic obstructive pulmonary disease (COPD) and multiple sclerosis (MS). Across prompted environments, the elicited correlations are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable, yet vary in a statistically significant way that supports downstream invariance testing, such that ICP provides a small set of candidate invariant parent links. These results show that indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and offer a cautious route from implicit correlations to causal statements that are congruent with LLM answering patterns.

AI.tldr: LLMs trained on biomedical literature can indirectly recover patient characteristic correlations via structured triplet comparison questions, enabling causal link identification through invariant causal prediction without accessing model internals.
AI.motivation: Large language models are trained on diverse biomedical literature containing information about many patient populations. The paper investigates how to recover information about correlations and causal links between patient characteristics from LLMs, which is crucial for medical decision making but difficult to extract directly due to elicitation pitfalls.
AI.method: Proposes an approach using structured comparison questions (patient comparison triplets) combined with a statistical model for LLM representations. Uses similarity decisions based on one variable and observes how they're affected by information about a second variable. Induces prompt-level environment shifts to get correlation estimates for different subpopulations, enabling invariant causal prediction (ICP) to identify conservative candidate parent links.
AI.result: Demonstrated in COPD and MS clinical domains. Elicited correlations across prompted environments are smooth, stable, and clinically interpretable, yet vary in statistically significant ways that support downstream invariance testing. ICP provides a small set of candidate invariant parent links.
AI.conclusion: Indirect elicitation via triplet comparisons can recover meaningful association structure from LLMs and offers a cautious approach to move from implicit correlations to causal statements that align with LLM answering patterns.

title: Is Escalation Worth It? A Decision-Theoretic Characterization of LLM Cascades
summary: Model cascades, in which a cheap LLM defers to an expensive one on low-confidence queries, are widely used to navigate the cost-quality tradeoff at deployment. Existing approaches largely treat the deferral threshold as an empirical hyperparameter, with limited guidance on the geometry of the resulting cost-quality frontier over a model pool. We develop a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. For a two-model cascade, we establish piecewise concavity of the cost-quality frontier on decreasing-benefit regions of the confidence support, with reciprocal shadow prices linking the budget- and quality-constrained formulations. Given a pool of $k$ models, we characterize the frontier achievable by deterministic two-model threshold cascades as the pointwise envelope over $\binom{k}{2}$ pairwise cascades, with switching points where the optimal pair changes. For $k$-model cascades, we derive first-order conditions in which a single shadow price equalizes marginal quality-per-cost across stage boundaries. We validate the framework on five benchmarks (MATH, MMLU, TriviaQA, SimpleQA, LiveCodeBench) across eight models from five providers. Within the deterministic threshold-cascade class, full fixed chains underperform the pairwise envelope, and optimized subsequence cascades do not deliver practically meaningful held-out gains over it. A lightweight pre-generation router exceeds the best cascade policy on four of five datasets, mainly because it avoids the cheap model's generation cost on queries sent directly to a larger model rather than because of a stronger routing signal. These results suggest that cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost, since cascades pay the cheap model before any escalation decision, rather than by a shortage of intermediate stages.

AI.tldr: This paper develops a decision-theoretic framework for model cascades that optimizes cost-quality tradeoffs, characterizes the cost-quality frontier geometry, and finds that lightweight pre-generation routers often outperform cascades by avoiding initial cheap model costs.
AI.motivation: Model cascades use cheap LLMs first and defer to expensive ones on low-confidence queries, but existing approaches treat deferral thresholds as empirical hyperparameters without theoretical understanding of the cost-quality frontier geometry.
AI.method: Developed a decision-theoretic framework grounded in constrained optimization and duality. For two-model cascades, established piecewise concavity properties. For k-model pools, characterized frontiers as envelopes over pairwise cascades and derived first-order conditions with shadow prices. Validated on five benchmarks with eight models from five providers.
AI.result: Deterministic threshold-cascade chains underperform pairwise envelope combinations. Optimized subsequence cascades don't deliver meaningful gains over pairwise envelope. Lightweight pre-generation routers exceed best cascade policies on 4/5 datasets, primarily by avoiding cheap model generation costs rather than stronger routing signals.
AI.conclusion: Cascade performance is limited primarily by structural cost (paying cheap model before escalation decisions) rather than shortage of intermediate stages, suggesting that avoiding initial generation costs through pre-generation routing is more effective.

title: Topological Signatures of Grokking
summary: We study the grokking phenomenon through the lens of topology. Using persistent homology on point clouds derived from the embedding matrices of a range of models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes, we identify a clear and consistent topological signature of grokking: a sharp increase in both the maximum and total persistence of first homology ($H_1$). Persistence diagrams reveal the emergence of a dominant long-lived topological feature together with increasingly structured secondary features, reflecting the underlying cyclic structure of the task. Compared to existing spectral and geometric diagnostics -- specifically, Fourier analysis and local intrinsic dimension -- persistent homology provides a unified geometric and topological characterization of representation learning, capturing both local and global multi-scale structure. Ablations across data regimes and control settings show that these topological transitions are tied to generalization rather than memorization. Our results suggest that persistent homology offers a principled and interpretable framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure during training.

AI.tldr: Topological analysis of neural networks reveals grokking characterized by sharp increases in persistent homology features, capturing latent cyclic structure in modular arithmetic tasks.
AI.motivation: To understand the grokking phenomenon through topological methods rather than existing spectral/geometric diagnostics, seeking a unified geometric-topological characterization of representation learning.
AI.method: Using persistent homology on point clouds from embedding matrices of models trained on modular arithmetic with varying primes, analyzing changes in maximum/total persistence of first homology (H₁).
AI.result: Clear topological signature of grokking: sharp increase in both maximum and total persistence of H₁, emergence of dominant long-lived topological feature with structured secondary features reflecting cyclic task structure.
AI.conclusion: Persistent homology provides principled, interpretable framework for analyzing how neural networks internalize latent structure, with topological transitions tied to generalization rather than memorization.

title: Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Modelling with Missing Data
summary: Order-Agnostic autoregressive models have demonstrated strong performance in deep generative modeling, yet their use in settings with incomplete data remains largely unexplored. In this work, we reinterpret them through the lens of missing data. First, we show that their standard training procedure on fully observed data implicitly performs imputation under a missing completely at random mechanism, resulting in robust out-of-sample imputation performance in settings with high missingness. Second, we introduce the first principled framework for training them directly on incomplete datasets under general missingness mechanisms. Third, we leverage their amortized conditional density estimation to perform active information acquisition, i.e., sequentially selecting the most informative missing variables for downstream prediction or inference. Across a suite of real-world benchmarks, our Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM) consistently outperforms established imputation baselines.

AI.tldr: Order-agnostic autoregressive models trained on incomplete datasets outperform traditional imputation methods and enable active information acquisition by sequentially selecting informative missing variables.
AI.motivation: Order-agnostic autoregressive models show strong performance in generative modeling but their application to incomplete data settings remains unexplored. The paper aims to bridge this gap by reinterpreting these models through a missing data framework.
AI.method: Three main contributions: 1) Show standard training on fully observed data performs implicit imputation under Missing Completely At Random (MCAR) mechanism; 2) Introduce principled framework for training directly on incomplete datasets under general missingness mechanisms; 3) Use amortized conditional density estimation for active information acquisition to sequentially select most informative missing variables.
AI.result: The proposed Missingness-Aware Order-Agnostic Autoregressive Model (MO-ARM) consistently outperforms established imputation baselines across real-world benchmarks.
AI.conclusion: Order-agnostic autoregressive models provide a powerful framework for handling incomplete data, offering robust imputation capabilities, flexible training on incomplete datasets, and enabling active information acquisition strategies for improved downstream prediction and inference.

title: Memory Efficient Full-gradient Attacks (MEFA) Framework for Adversarial Defense Evaluations
summary: This work studies the robust evaluation of iterative stochastic purification defenses under white-box adversarial attacks. Our key technical insight is that gradient checkpointing makes exact end-to-end gradient computation through long purification trajectories practical by trading additional recomputation for substantially lower memory usage. This enables full-gradient adaptive attacks against diffusion- and Langevin-based purification defenses, where prior evaluations often resort to approximate backpropagation due to memory constraints. These approximations can weaken the attack signal and risk overestimating robustness. In parallel, stochasticity in iterative purification is frequently under-controlled, even though different purification trajectories can substantially change reported robustness metrics. Building on this insight, we introduce a memory-efficient full-gradient evaluation framework for stochastic purification defenses. The framework combines checkpointed backpropagation with evaluation protocols that control stochastic variability, thereby reducing memory bottlenecks while preserving exact gradients. We evaluate diffusion-based purification and Langevin sampling with Energy-Based Models (EBMs), demonstrating that full-gradient attacks uncover vulnerabilities missed by approximate-gradient evaluations. Our framework yields stronger state-of-the-art $\ell_{\infty}$ and $\ell_{2}$ white-box attacks and further supports probing out-of-distribution robustness. Overall, our results show that exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a memory-efficient framework using gradient checkpointing to enable exact end-to-end gradient computation for robust evaluation of iterative stochastic purification defenses against white-box attacks, showing that full-gradient attacks uncover vulnerabilities missed by approximate methods.
AI.motivation: Prior evaluations of iterative stochastic purification defenses often use approximate backpropagation due to memory constraints, which can weaken attack signals and risk overestimating robustness. Additionally, stochasticity in purification is frequently under-controlled, affecting robustness metrics.
AI.method: The authors use gradient checkpointing to trade additional recomputation for substantially lower memory usage, enabling exact end-to-end gradient computation through long purification trajectories. They combine checkpointed backpropagation with evaluation protocols that control stochastic variability.
AI.result: The framework enables full-gradient adaptive attacks against diffusion- and Langevin-based purification defenses, uncovering vulnerabilities missed by approximate-gradient evaluations. It yields stronger state-of-the-art ℓ∞ and ℓ₂ white-box attacks and supports probing out-of-distribution robustness.
AI.conclusion: Exact-gradient evaluation is essential for reliable benchmarking of iterative stochastic defenses, as approximate methods can overestimate robustness by missing vulnerabilities that full-gradient attacks uncover.

title: Preliminary Insights in Chronos Frequency Data Understanding and Reconstruction
summary: This paper presents a preliminary analysis of the ability of Chronos foundation model to process and internally represent frequency domain information. Foundation models that process time-series data offer practitioners a unified architecture capable of learning generic temporal representations across diverse tasks and domains, reducing the need for task-specific feature engineering and enabling transfer across signal modalities. Despite their growing adoption, the extent to which such models encode fundamental signal properties remains insufficiently characterised. We address this gap by analysing Chronos under controlled conditions, starting from the simplest class of signals: discrete sinusoids generated at fixed frequencies. Using lightweight online minimum description length probes applied to the decoder architecture, we test for the presence and separability of frequency information in the model's internal representations. The results provide insight into how frequential content is captured across the frequency spectrum and highlight regimes in which representation quality may degrade or require particular care. These findings offer practical guidance for users of Chronos in signal processing and information fusion contexts, and contribute to ongoing efforts to improve the interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data.

AI.tldr: Chronos foundation model's ability to represent frequency domain information is analyzed using discrete sinusoids and minimum description length probes to understand how it encodes frequency content across the spectrum.
AI.motivation: Foundation models for time-series data offer unified architectures for learning generic temporal representations, but there's insufficient understanding of how these models encode fundamental signal properties like frequency information, creating a gap in characterizing their capabilities.
AI.method: Analyzed Chronos under controlled conditions using discrete sinusoids at fixed frequencies as the simplest signal class. Applied lightweight online minimum description length probes to the decoder architecture to test for presence and separability of frequency information in internal representations.
AI.result: Provided insight into how frequency content is captured across the frequency spectrum and identified regimes where representation quality may degrade or require special care, offering practical guidance for signal processing applications.
AI.conclusion: The findings contribute to improving interpretability and evaluation of foundation models for temporal data, offering practical guidance for Chronos users in signal processing and information fusion contexts while advancing understanding of how such models encode fundamental signal properties.

title: Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths
summary: We introduce a new generative modeling framework, \textbf{Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths (AuxPath-FM)}, which generalizes conditional flow matching by incorporating an auxiliary variable drawn from an arbitrary distribution into the probability path. Unlike prior methods that restrict auxiliary components to Gaussian noise, AuxPath-FM allows the variable $η$ to follow any distribution, producing trajectories of the form $X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η$. We theoretically demonstrate that this construction preserves the continuity equation and maintains a training objective consistent with the marginal formulation. This flexibility enables the design of diverse probability paths using various priors, including Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, and discrete Rademacher distributions, each offering unique geometric properties for generative flows. Furthermore, our framework allows for specialized tasks such as label-guided generation by encoding structured semantic information into the auxiliary distribution. Overall, AuxPath-FM provides a principled and general foundation for probability path design, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse generative modeling tasks.

AI.tldr: AuxPath-FM extends flow matching by incorporating arbitrary auxiliary distributions into probability paths, enabling more flexible generative modeling beyond Gaussian noise constraints.
AI.motivation: Prior flow matching methods restrict auxiliary variables to Gaussian noise distributions, limiting the flexibility in probability path design and the ability to incorporate diverse geometric properties or structured semantic information into the generative process.
AI.method: Proposes Flow Matching with Arbitrary Auxiliary Paths (AuxPath-FM), which incorporates an auxiliary variable η drawn from any distribution (Gaussian, Uniform, Laplace, Rademacher, etc.) into probability paths of the form X_t = a(t)X_1 + b(t)X_0 + c(t)η. The framework preserves the continuity equation and maintains training consistency with marginal formulations.
AI.result: Theoretical demonstration that the construction preserves continuity equations and maintains consistent training objectives. Enables design of diverse probability paths with unique geometric properties and supports specialized tasks like label-guided generation by encoding semantic information into auxiliary distributions.
AI.conclusion: AuxPath-FM provides a principled, general foundation for probability path design in generative modeling, offering both theoretical generality and practical flexibility for diverse tasks, expanding beyond the limitations of Gaussian-only auxiliary distributions.

title: Layer Collapse in Diffusion Language Models
summary: Diffusion language models (DLMs) have recently emerged as competitive alternatives to autoregressive (AR) language models, yet differences in their activation dynamics remain poorly understood. We characterize these dynamics in LLaDA-8B and identify a striking layer-collapse property: a few early layers exhibit highly similar, collapsed activation patterns dominated by a single large super-outlier persisting over a long token range. Despite its apparent redundancy, this outlier is critical: pruning it causes outputs to degrade into repetitive random token loops. Paradoxically, layers in LLaDA contain more redundant representations overall, with redundancy most pronounced in earlier layers -- the reverse of AR models, where deeper layers grow redundant due to undertraining. Our analysis indicates that layer collapse in DLMs is not driven by undertraining but by overtraining: a dominant outlier becomes an indispensable information carrier while remaining representations collapse into redundant structure. These findings have strong practical implications, verified through controlled pre-training experiments. DLMs are surprisingly robust to compression: LLaDA under 3-bit GPTQ quantization drops only -1.8% on GSM8K, whereas Llama-3.1-8B drops -64.7%. Optimal sparsity allocation also reverses between families: at 50% average sparsity, allocating more to early layers in LLaDA yields +8.4% over the reverse strategy, while the same allocation costs Llama -8.4%. Our findings reveal that the DLM training objective fundamentally reshapes layer dynamics relative to AR models, with direct consequences for compression and deployment. Code: github.com/Conzel/super-outlier-dlm.

AI.tldr: DLMs exhibit unique layer-collapse dynamics where early layers develop redundant representations dominated by a critical super-outlier, making them surprisingly robust to quantization and optimal for compression when allocating sparsity to early layers.
AI.motivation: Diffusion language models have emerged as competitive alternatives to autoregressive models, but their internal activation dynamics and layer representations remain poorly understood compared to AR models.
AI.method: Analyzed activation dynamics in LLaDA-8B, identified layer-collapse property, studied super-outlier behavior through pruning experiments, compared with Llama-3.1-8B AR model, conducted controlled pre-training experiments, and tested compression robustness through quantization and sparsity allocation strategies.
AI.result: Found that DLMs exhibit reverse redundancy patterns (early layers most redundant), critical super-outliers essential for preventing output degradation, surprising robustness to quantization (only -1.8% vs -64.7% drop for AR model), and optimal compression achieved by allocating more sparsity to early layers (+8.4% improvement vs -8.4% cost for AR model).
AI.conclusion: The DLM training objective fundamentally reshapes layer dynamics relative to AR models, creating unique redundancy patterns and critical super-outliers that make DLMs surprisingly robust to compression techniques, with important practical implications for deployment optimization.

title: A Unified Pair-GRPO Family: From Implicit to Explicit Preference Constraints for Stable and General RL Alignment
summary: Large language model (LLM) alignment via reinforcement learning from human preferences (RLHF) suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in mainstream pairwise preference learning paradigms. To systematically address these limitations, we establish a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization centered on the Pair-GRPO family, comprising two tightly coupled variants: Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO. Soft-Pair-GRPO is a minimal modification of Group Relative Policy Optimization (GRPO) that replaces group-normalized scalar rewards with binary pairwise preference rewards, retaining GRPO's clipped surrogate and KL-regularized structure. We prove a critical gradient equivalence theorem: under first-order Taylor expansion around the current policy, Soft-Pair-GRPO's gradient is a positive scalar multiple of standard GRPO's gradient, explaining its empirical stability despite discarding continuous reward magnitudes. Building on this foundation, we propose Hard-Pair-GRPO, an advanced variant introducing explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization to further suppress gradient noise and global policy drift. We provide comprehensive theoretical guarantees for both variants--including monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, gradient-variance reduction, and dynamic step-size convergence. Extensive experiments on standard LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF,UltraFeedback) and the MuJoCo continuous control task HalfCheetah-v4 demonstrate that our Pair-GRPO family consistently outperforms state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general reinforcement learning. Ablation studies validate the critical contributions of each core component.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pair-GRPO, a unified theoretical framework for preference-based RL optimization that addresses limitations of current LLM alignment methods through two variants (Soft-Pair-GRPO and Hard-Pair-GRPO) with improved stability, interpretability, and performance.
AI.motivation: Current LLM alignment via RLHF suffers from unstable policy updates, ambiguous gradient directions, poor interpretability, and high gradient variance in pairwise preference learning paradigms. These limitations hinder effective and stable training of language models using human preferences.
AI.method: Establishes Pair-GRPO family with two variants: 1) Soft-Pair-GRPO (minimal modification of GRPO replacing scalar rewards with binary pairwise preferences), and 2) Hard-Pair-GRPO (advanced variant with explicit local probability constraints and constrained KL-fitting optimization). Provides theoretical guarantees including gradient equivalence theorem, monotonic policy improvement, deterministic gradient direction, and gradient-variance reduction.
AI.result: Extensive experiments on LLM alignment benchmarks (HH-RLHF, UltraFeedback) and MuJoCo HalfCheetah-v4 show consistent outperformance over state-of-the-art baselines in alignment quality, human preference win rate, training stability, and generalization to general RL tasks. Ablation studies validate core components.
AI.conclusion: The Pair-GRPO framework systematically addresses key limitations of current preference-based RL optimization, offering stable, interpretable, and effective alignment methods with strong theoretical foundations and empirical performance across diverse domains.

title: MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades
summary: We show that the MinMax algebra provides a form of recurrence that is expressively powerful, efficiently implementable, and most importantly it is not affected by vanishing or exploding gradient. We call MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) the models obtained by cascading several layers of neurons that employ such recurrence. We show that MinMax RNCs enjoy many favourable theoretical properties. First, their formal expressivity includes all regular languages, arguably the maximal expressivity for a finite-memory system. Second, they can be evaluated in parallel with a runtime that is logarithmic in the input length given enough processors; and they can also be evaluated sequentially. Third, their state and activations are bounded uniformly for all input lengths. Fourth, at almost all points, their loss gradient exists and it is bounded. Fifth, they do not exhibit a vanishing state gradient: the gradient of a state w.r.t. a past state can have constant value one regardless of the time distance between the two states. Finally, we find empirical evidence that the favourable theoretical properties of MinMax RNCs are matched by their practical capabilities: they are able to perfectly solve a number of synthetic tasks, showing superior performance compared to the considered state-of-the-art recurrent neural networks; also, we train a MinMax RNC of 127M parameters on next-token prediction, and the obtained model shows competitive performance for its size, providing evidence of the potential of MinMax RNCs on real-world tasks.

AI.tldr: MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) use MinMax algebra recurrence to avoid vanishing/exploding gradients while achieving high expressivity, parallel computation, bounded states, and strong empirical performance on both synthetic and real-world tasks.
AI.motivation: Traditional recurrent neural networks suffer from vanishing and exploding gradient problems, limiting their effectiveness in learning long-term dependencies. The authors seek an alternative recurrence mechanism that maintains gradient stability while offering strong theoretical guarantees and practical performance.
AI.method: Propose MinMax Recurrent Neural Cascades (RNCs) using MinMax algebra recurrence. This involves cascading multiple layers of neurons with MinMax recurrence, which provides formal expressivity for regular languages, parallel evaluation capability, and bounded state dynamics.
AI.result: MinMax RNCs demonstrate multiple theoretical advantages: express all regular languages, support parallel evaluation (O(log n) time), maintain uniformly bounded states, have bounded gradients at almost all points, and avoid vanishing state gradients. Empirically, they outperform state-of-the-art RNNs on synthetic tasks and show competitive performance on next-token prediction with a 127M parameter model.
AI.conclusion: MinMax RNCs provide a theoretically sound and practically effective alternative to traditional RNNs, addressing gradient stability issues while maintaining high expressivity and computational efficiency. The approach shows promise for both synthetic and real-world sequence modeling tasks.

title: Data-Driven Covariate Selection for Nonparametric and Cycle-Agnostic Causal Effect Estimation
summary: Estimating causal effects from observational data requires identifying valid adjustment sets. This task is especially challenging in realistic settings where latent confounding and feedback loops are present. Existing approaches typically assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, limiting applicability and computational efficiency. In this work, we study a local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence information. While this method is known to be sound and complete in acyclic causal models, its validity in the presence of cycles has remained unclear. Our main contribution is to show that these guarantees extend to cyclic causal models. In particular, our result relies on the invariance of conditional independence assertions under $σ$-acyclification. These findings establish a unified, cycle-agnostic perspective on covariate selection and causal effect estimation, showing that the method applies across cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirically, we validate this on extensive synthetic data, showing reliable performance in cyclic causal models.

AI.tldr: A local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence extends to cyclic causal models, providing a unified approach for causal effect estimation in both cyclic and acyclic settings.
AI.motivation: Estimating causal effects from observational data is challenging in realistic settings with latent confounding and feedback loops. Existing approaches typically assume acyclicity or rely on global causal structure learning, which limits applicability and computational efficiency.
AI.method: A local, data-driven method for covariate selection based on conditional independence information, which is known to be sound and complete in acyclic causal models. The authors investigate its validity in cyclic models through the invariance of conditional independence assertions under σ-acyclification.
AI.result: The main contribution shows that the soundness and completeness guarantees of the conditional independence-based covariate selection method extend to cyclic causal models. This establishes a unified, cycle-agnostic perspective on covariate selection and causal effect estimation.
AI.conclusion: The method applies across both cyclic and acyclic settings without modification. Empirical validation on extensive synthetic data shows reliable performance in cyclic causal models, establishing a unified approach for causal effect estimation regardless of cycle presence.

title: Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation: Bridging Exploitation and Imitation at the Token Level
summary: On-policy distillation (OPD) trains a student on its own trajectories with token-level teacher feedback and often outperforms off-policy distillation and standard reinforcement learning. However, we find that its standard advantage weighted policy gradient suffers from three structural weaknesses, including high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient.We therefore propose Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD), which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning. Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show that AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD, with average gains of 4.09 / 8.34 under strong / weak initialization, respectively. AOPD also maintains higher policy entropy during training and better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.

AI.tldr: AOPD improves on standard on-policy distillation by replacing ineffective negative reinforcement with divergence minimization in zero/negative advantage regions while preserving positive reinforcement learning, achieving better performance and exploration.
AI.motivation: Standard on-policy distillation (OPD) suffers from three structural weaknesses: high variance updates, vanishing gradients in zero-advantage regions, and exploration bottlenecks when corrective signals are insufficient. These limitations hinder its effectiveness despite OPD generally outperforming off-policy distillation and standard RL.
AI.method: Proposes Asymmetric On-Policy Distillation (AOPD) which replaces ineffective negative reinforcement with localized divergence minimization in non-positive advantage regions (where advantage ≤ 0) while preserving positive reinforcement learning. This asymmetric approach addresses the weaknesses of standard advantage weighted policy gradient.
AI.result: Experiments on mathematical reasoning benchmarks show AOPD consistently outperforms standard OPD with average gains of 4.09 under strong initialization and 8.34 under weak initialization. AOPD maintains higher policy entropy during training and demonstrates better capability retention during sequential tool-use adaptation.
AI.conclusion: AOPD effectively addresses the structural weaknesses of standard OPD by asymmetrically handling positive and non-positive advantage regions, leading to improved performance, better exploration, and enhanced capability retention in challenging reasoning tasks.

title: Consistent Geometric Deep Learning via Hilbert Bundles and Cellular Sheaves
summary: Modern deep learning architectures increasingly contend with sophisticated signals that are natively infinite-dimensional, such as time series, probability distributions, or operators, and are defined over irregular domains. Yet, a unified learning theory for these settings has been lacking. To start addressing this gap, we introduce a novel convolutional learning framework for possibly infinite-dimensional signals supported on a manifold. Namely, we use the connection Laplacian associated with a Hilbert bundle as a convolutional operator, and we derive filters and neural networks, dubbed as \textit{HilbNets}. We make HilbNets and, more generally, the convolution operation, implementable via a two-stage sampling procedure. First, we show that sampling the manifold induces a Hilbert Cellular Sheaf, a generalized graph structure with Hilbert feature spaces and edge-wise coupling rules, and we prove that its sheaf Laplacian converges in probability to the underlying connection Laplacian as the sampling density increases. Notably, this result is a generalization to the infinite-dimensional bundle setting of the Belkin \& Niyogi \cite{BELKIN20081289} convergence result for the graph Laplacian to the manifold Laplacian, a theoretical cornerstone of geometric learning methods. Second, we discretize the signals and prove that the discretized (implementable) HilbNets converge to the underlying continuous architectures and are transferable across different samplings of the same bundle, providing consistency for learning. Finally, we validate our framework on synthetic and real-world tasks. Overall, our results broaden the scope of geometric learning as a whole by lifting classical Laplacian-based frameworks to settings where the signal at each point lives in its own Hilbert space.

AI.tldr: A unified convolutional learning framework (HilbNets) for infinite-dimensional signals on manifolds using connection Laplacians from Hilbert bundles, made implementable via a two-stage sampling procedure with convergence guarantees.
AI.motivation: Modern deep learning deals with increasingly sophisticated infinite-dimensional signals (time series, probability distributions, operators) defined over irregular domains, but lacks a unified learning theory for these settings.
AI.method: Introduces HilbNets - convolutional networks using connection Laplacian associated with Hilbert bundles as convolutional operators. Makes them implementable via a two-stage sampling: 1) Sampling manifold induces Hilbert Cellular Sheaf (generalized graph with Hilbert feature spaces), proving sheaf Laplacian converges to connection Laplacian; 2) Discretizing signals proves discretized HilbNets converge to continuous architectures and are transferable across samplings.
AI.result: 1) Hilbert Cellular Sheaf's sheaf Laplacian converges in probability to underlying connection Laplacian as sampling density increases (generalizing Belkin & Niyogi's graph Laplacian convergence result to infinite-dimensional bundles); 2) Discretized HilbNets converge to continuous architectures and are transferable across samplings; 3) Framework validated on synthetic and real-world tasks.
AI.conclusion: The work broadens scope of geometric learning by lifting classical Laplacian-based frameworks to settings where signals at each point live in their own Hilbert space, providing a unified convolutional learning theory for infinite-dimensional signals on manifolds.

title: SparseForge: Efficient Semi-Structured LLM Sparsification via Annealing of Hessian-Guided Soft-Mask
summary:
Semi-structured sparsity provides a practical path to accelerate large language models (LLMs) with native hardware support, but post-training semi-structured pruning often suffers from substantial quality degradation due to strong structural coupling. Existing methods rely on large-scale sparse retraining to recover accuracy, resulting in high computational cost.
  We propose SparseForge, a post-training framework that improves recovery efficiency by directly optimizing the sparsity mask rather than scaling up retraining tokens. SparseForge combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity, enabling stable and efficient sparse recovery. On LLaMA-2-7B under 2:4 sparsity, SparseForge achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only $\textbf{5B}$ retraining tokens, surpassing the dense model's 56.43% accuracy and approaching the 57.52% result of a state-of-the-art method using $\textbf{40B}$ tokens. Such improvements on the accuracy-efficiency trade-off from SparseForge are shown to be consistent across model families.

AI.tldr: SparseForge is a post-training pruning framework that efficiently recovers LLM accuracy after semi-structured sparsity pruning by optimizing sparsity masks directly, reducing retraining tokens from 40B to 5B while matching state-of-the-art performance.
AI.motivation: Semi-structured sparsity enables LLM acceleration with native hardware support, but post-training pruning suffers from significant quality degradation due to structural coupling. Existing recovery methods require massive retraining (40B tokens) with high computational cost, creating an efficiency bottleneck.
AI.method: Proposes SparseForge framework that directly optimizes sparsity masks instead of scaling retraining tokens. Combines Hessian-aware importance estimation with progressive annealing of soft masks into hardware-executable structured sparsity for stable and efficient recovery.
AI.result: On LLaMA-2-7B with 2:4 sparsity, achieves 57.27% average zero-shot accuracy with only 5B retraining tokens, surpassing dense model's 56.43% and approaching state-of-the-art 57.52% (which requires 40B tokens). Shows consistent improvements across model families.
AI.conclusion: SparseForge significantly improves the accuracy-efficiency trade-off for post-training semi-structured pruning of LLMs, enabling near state-of-the-art performance with dramatically reduced retraining cost (8x fewer tokens). Provides a practical path for efficient LLM acceleration with hardware-supported sparsity.

title: FRInGe: Distribution-Space Integrated Gradients with Fisher--Rao Geometry
summary: Gradient-based attribution methods are model-faithful and scalable, but Integrated Gradients (IG) can be brittle because explanations depend on heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation. We propose Fisher--Rao Integrated Gradients (FRInGe), which defines both the reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space. FRInGe replaces input baselines with a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. The corresponding input-space trajectory is realized through the pullback Fisher metric and stabilized by KL and Euclidean trust regions; attributions are obtained by integrating input gradients along this trajectory. Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe most clearly improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics, especially MAS scores, while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity.

AI.tldr: FRInGe proposes a new gradient-based attribution method that replaces Integrated Gradients' heuristic baselines with maximum-entropy references and uses Fisher-Rao geodesics in probability space for more stable explanations.
AI.motivation: Integrated Gradients (IG) attribution method is brittle due to dependencies on heuristic baselines, straight-line paths, discretization, and saturation effects. There's a need for more robust gradient-based attribution methods.
AI.method: FRInGe defines both reference and interpolation schedule in predictive distribution space using a maximum-entropy predictive reference and follows a Fisher-Rao geodesic on the probability simplex. Input-space trajectory is realized through pullback Fisher metric with KL and Euclidean trust regions for stabilization.
AI.result: Across six ImageNet architectures, FRInGe significantly improves calibration-oriented attribution metrics (especially MAS scores) while remaining competitive on perturbation AUC and infidelity measures compared to other methods.
AI.conclusion: FRInGe provides a principled approach to gradient-based attribution by working in predictive distribution space with Fisher-Rao geometry, resulting in more stable and calibrated explanations while maintaining performance on established metrics.

title: E = T*H/(O+B): A Dimensionless Control Parameter for Mixture-of-Experts Ecology
comment: 12 experiments, 11,000+ training epochs, cross-modal validation (vision + language). Extended version of the Claude-in-the-Loop ecology framework
summary: We introduce E = T*H/(O+B), a dimensionless control parameter that predicts whether Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) models will develop a healthy expert ecology or collapse into dead experts. E combines four hyperparameters -- routing temperature T, routing entropy weight H, oracle weight O, and balance weight B -- into a single quantity. Through 12 controlled experiments (8 vision, 4 language) totaling over 11,000 training epochs, we establish that E >= 0.5 alone is sufficient to guarantee zero dead experts, removing the necessity for handcrafted load-balancing auxiliary losses.
